2.2: ein qadosh kaYHWH ki ein biltecha vein tzur kebogheinu: 'There is none holy like YHWH: for there is none besides you; there is no rock like our God.' Hannah's prayer is the OT's most concentrated statement of YHWH's incomparable holiness and sovereignty — expressed through the reversal-of-fortunes theme (vv. 4-8: the bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble bind on strength). The pattern is: YHWH exalts the lowly and humbles the proud, not based on human merit but on divine grace and election. Paul's 'God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Cor 1:27) is the Christological application of Hannah's insight: the cross itself is YHWH's ultimate reversal, where the weak and crucified Son defeats the strong.
2.1: Hannah's prayer opens: ʿālaz libbî bYHWH rāmāh qarnî bYHWH — my heart exults in YHWH; my horn is exalted in YHWH. ʿālaz (H5937, exult intensely — used in Ps 28:7; Zeph 3:14) connotes fierce, fighting joy. qeren (H7161, horn) — the horn of a bull or ram as the seat of its power; 'my horn is exalted' means my strength/dignity/honor has been lifted. Hannah's prayer closes (v10) with wĕyārēm qeren mĕšîḥô (he will exalt the horn of his anointed) — the same rûm qeren formula. The opening personal exaltation and the closing messianic exaltation are connected: Hannah's lifted horn is the prototype of the ultimate horn-exaltation. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) expands this prayer, applying the reversals to the coming of the Messiah.
2.3: 'Do not speak with such great pride' — ʾal-tarḇû tĕḏabbĕrû gĕḇōhāh gĕḇōhāh: the doubled gĕḇōhāh (H1364, high/proud) is an intensifying repetition. 'YHWH is a God of knowledges' (ʾēl dēʿôt YHWH) — the plural dēʿôt (knowledges) indicates exhaustive or multidimensional knowledge. 'And by him actions are weighed' (wĕlô niṯkĕnû ʿălilôt) — tāḵan (H8254, to weigh/assess) is used in Prov 16:2; 21:2 for YHWH's weighing of human spirits. The sovereignty of YHWH's knowledge grounds the prohibition of arrogance: one cannot boast before the One who weighs every action in his comprehensive, plural knowledge.
2.4: 'The bows of the mighty are shattered, but those who stumbled are girded with strength' — qešet gibbōrîm ḥattîm wĕniḵšālîm ʾāzĕrû ḥāyil. gibbōrîm (H1368, mighty warriors) are those who rely on their military strength; their bows (qešet, the premier long-range weapon) are 'shattered' (ḥattat, H2865 — the same root as 'terror/dismay' that overcomes enemies). niḵšālîm (those who stumbled, H3782) — the visibly vulnerable who are then 'girded with strength' (ʾāzar ḥayil). The pattern — strong rendered powerless, stumbling made strong — is the theological reversal Paul applies in 1 Cor 1:27-29 and that the cross itself enacts.
2.5: 'The barren woman has borne seven, but she who has many children languishes' (ʿăqārāh yāleḏāh šiḇʿāh wĕraḇbat bānîm ʾumlālāh). The seven children (the perfect, complete number — seven as fullness) born to the formerly barren is the direct application to Hannah: she was barren; YHWH will give her five more children (v21) approaching the prayer's 'seven.' The 'many-son woman' (raḇbat bānîm) — Peninnah — who now 'languishes' (ʾumlālāh, H535, wilts/weakens). The prayer is simultaneously a theological statement about YHWH's reversals and a personal testimony about Hannah's own experience — and a prophecy about what is coming for Peninnah's position of advantage.
2.6: 'YHWH kills and makes alive; he brings down to Sheol and raises up' (YHWH mēmît ûmĕḥayyeh môrîḏ šĕʾôl wayaʿal). This is the OT's most direct paired statement of divine sovereignty over life and death before Deut 32:39. šĕʾôl (H7585) — the realm of the dead, conceived as below the earth (Job 10:21-22; Ps 88:3-6). YHWH's power to 'bring up' from Sheol (wayyaʿal, to ascend/bring up) is the background for NT resurrection language. Deut 32:39 parallels exactly: 'I kill and make alive.' Both texts assert the same monotheistic sovereignty: YHWH alone controls the boundary between life and death — no competing power shares this authority.
2.7: 'He raises up the poor from the dust, lifts the needy from the ash heap' (mĕqîm mēʿāfār dāl mēʾašpōt yārîm ʾeḇyôn). ʿāfār (H6083, dust/earth) — the lowest position, associated with mourning (Job 2:12) and humiliation (Isa 47:1). ʾašpōt (H830, ash-heap/dung-heap/refuse pile) — where the destitute and sick sat (Job 2:8). Ps 113:7-8 quotes these phrases almost verbatim: 'He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap, to make them sit with princes.' The ash-heap as the lowest social location from which YHWH lifts his faithful ones is the OT's canonical image of divine reversal — social and economic inversion as YHWH's self-expression.
2.8: 'For the pillars of the earth are YHWH's, and on them he has set the world' (kî lYHWH mĕṣûqê ʾereṣ wayyāšeṯ ʿălêhem tēḇēl). mĕṣûqê ʾereṣ (H4690, pillars/pegs of the earth) — the cosmological foundations that support the world. The cosmic grounding of the social reversal is the prayer's key insight: YHWH can reverse human fortunes because he owns and controls the foundations of creation. The one who set the world on its pillars can lift a person from the ash heap to the prince's seat — both acts are expressions of the same sovereign power operating at different scales of reality.
2.9: 'He will guard the feet of his faithful ones, but the wicked shall be cut off in darkness' (raglê ḥăsîḏāyw yišmōr wĕrĕšāʿîm baḥōšeḵ yiddāmmû). ḥăsîḏāyw (H2623, his faithful/covenant-loyal ones) — plural of ḥāsîḏ, one who practices hesed. 'For not by strength shall a man prevail' (kî lōʾ ḇĕkoaḥ yiḡbar ʾîš). This summary principle dissolves the apparent security of the strong: human strength is never the decisive factor in YHWH's economy. The strong fall not because of a rival's greater strength but because YHWH does not honor self-reliance. This theological axiom grounds every reversal in the prayer: the source of outcomes is YHWH's initiative, not human capacity.
2.10: 'He will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed' (wĕyārēm qeren mĕšîḥô). The climax of Hannah's prayer is the first use of māšîaḥ (H4899, his anointed one) in Samuel. At this point in the narrative there is no king in Israel — Hannah prays for a future king and anointed one in the same breath as describing YHWH's reversals. The prayer thus looks beyond Samuel (YHWH's immediate answer to Hannah's prayer) to the king YHWH will establish (the structural answer to Israel's need) — and beyond Israel's historical kings to the ultimate māšîaḥ. Acts 2:36 ('God has made him both Lord and Christ') and Rev 12:10 ('the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christ') are the full realization of this first māšîaḥ invocation.
2.11: Elkanah returns to Ramah; Samuel ministers before YHWH under Eli. mĕšārēt (H8334, to minister/serve in sanctuary context) — the technical term for Levitical and priestly ministry (Num 8:22; Deut 10:8). Samuel, an Ephraimite rather than a Levite, ministers at the central sanctuary — his role transcends ordinary tribal categories, anticipating the prophetic office that will also transcend tribal boundaries. The contrast is established: Samuel ministers before YHWH; Eli's sons (about to be introduced) minister for themselves.
2.12: Eli's sons are bĕnê bĕliyyāʿal (sons of worthlessness/Belial) who 'did not know YHWH' (lōʾ yāḏĕʿû ʾet-YHWH). yāḏaʿ (H3045, to know) in the covenantal sense means relational, personal knowledge — the knowledge that comes from covenant relationship. The same verb will describe Samuel's condition in 3:7 ('Samuel did not yet know YHWH') — but the situations are opposite: Eli's sons do not know YHWH due to spiritual corruption; Samuel does not yet know YHWH because YHWH has not yet revealed himself to him. Same surface description, opposite diagnoses. The sons' not-knowing is culpable; Samuel's not-knowing is circumstantial.
2.13: The priests' sin is technically precise: a three-pronged fork (mazlēg H4207) used to take from the boiling sacrificial pot before the fat was burned to YHWH. Lev 3:3-5 and 7:29-34 specify that the fat must be burned to YHWH first and the breast and right thigh given to the priest. Hophni and Phinehas are taking before YHWH's portion — inverting the sacrificial order and claiming priority over YHWH. This is the specific violation: not taking an unauthorized amount but taking at the wrong time, before the sacred portion designated for YHWH. The timing violation is the theological content of the sin.
2.14: The three-pronged fork's arbitrary catch — 'all that the fork brought up, the priest took' — reflects the opportunistic character of the sin. No prescribed portion is being observed; the priests take whatever they can get. The verb kōhēn (acting as priest) used in v13 is deeply ironic: those who exercise the priestly office are doing so for self-service. The corruption of sacred office — using covenant functions as vehicles for personal enrichment — is the OT's consistent warning about the danger that attaches to positions of sacred privilege (cf. Ezek 34 on self-serving shepherds).
2.15: The sin escalates: even before the fat was burned (bĕṭerem yaqṭirûn ʾet-haḥēleḇ), the priest's servant demands raw meat rather than boiled. The preference for raw over cooked meat is a quality preference — roasted meat is superior to boiled. The progression: improper timing (before YHWH's portion is burned) → improper method (raw for roasting rather than the prescribed cooked portion). The escalation pattern is the OT's description of how sin in religious office compounds: first a small violation of timing, then an escalation to a quality preference, then (v16) outright coercion. Sin in sacred office follows an escalating logic.
2.16: If the worshiper protests — 'burn the fat first, then take as much as you wish' — the servant's response is coercive: 'No, you must give it now; if not, I will take it by force' (wĕʾim-lōʾ lāqaḥtî ḇĕḥāzāqāh). ḥāzāq (H2388, force/strength) applied to the taking of sacrificial portions is the language of extortion. The priest who should facilitate the worshiper's offering to YHWH is now threatening force against the worshiper. This is the inversion of the priestly role from covenant mediator to covenant predator — using the authority of the priestly office as a coercive instrument against the very people the priest exists to serve.
2.17: 'The sin of the young men was very great in the sight of YHWH, for the men treated the offering of YHWH with contempt' (kî nîʾăṣû hāʾănāšîm ʾet-minḥat YHWH). nāʾaṣ (H5006, to despise/contemn) — used in Num 14:23 for those who treated YHWH with contempt in the wilderness. The 'offering of YHWH' (minḥat YHWH) is the vehicle of the contempt: by taking before YHWH's portion and by using coercion, the priests make the worship system express contempt toward YHWH rather than honor. The narrator's theological precision: the gravity of the sin is not violation of a procedure but the contempt it expresses toward YHWH himself. Contempt for the sacred is contempt for the Holy One.
2.18: Samuel ministers before YHWH as a naʿar (youth) wearing a linen ephod (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ). The ʾēpôḏ (H646) was primarily the high-priestly garment (Exod 28:6-14) associated with the Urim and Thummim oracle-function (Exod 28:30). Samuel wearing the ephod at this young age — though he is not a Levite — foreshadows his prophetic-oracular role. The contrast with Eli's sons is stark: they take meat for themselves; Samuel wears the vestment of priestly ministry. The linen ephod as the badge of genuine covenant service (cf. 2 Sam 6:14 where David wears it) marks Samuel as the growing alternative to the corrupt priestly establishment.
2.19: Hannah makes a little robe (mĕʿîl qāṭōn) for Samuel each year. mĕʿîl (H4598, robe/outer garment) — the same word for Samuel's prophetic robe in his adult ministry (15:27; 28:14). The annual garment is Hannah's ongoing investment in Samuel despite his absence: the mother who gave her son to YHWH remains connected to him through the yearly robe. The robe tracks his growth — each year a new robe, each year larger. Hannah's faithfulness to Samuel mirrors YHWH's faithfulness to Hannah: both continue their covenant relationship across distance.
2.20: Eli's blessing on Elkanah and Hannah — 'May YHWH give you children by this woman in place of the one she asked for YHWH' (taḥat haššĕʾēlāh ʾăšer šāʾal lYHWH) — uses šāʾal again: 'in place of the requesting she asked.' The blessing-language of substitution (taḥat, in place of/instead of) is significant: Hannah's gift to YHWH will be replaced by YHWH's gift to Hannah. The exchange is not equal — Samuel was given once; YHWH will give five more. This is the economics of Prov 11:24-25: 'One gives freely yet grows all the richer.' YHWH's reciprocal giving exceeds the human giving that initiated it.
2.21: YHWH visits (pāqaḏ, H6485 — the covenant verb of divine initiative, as in 1:19) Hannah and she conceives three more sons and two daughters. The five additional children approaching the seven of Hannah's prayer (v5) represent YHWH's abundant answer. Samuel 'grew' (wayyiḡdal šĕmûʾēl) with YHWH (ʿim YHWH) — the presence of YHWH characterizes his formation. The brief notice of Samuel's growth between the narratives of Eli's corrupt sons and the oracle against Eli's house (vv22-36) keeps the alternative before the reader: while Eli's house disintegrates, YHWH's chosen servant grows.
2.22: Eli confronts his sons: 'they lay with the women who were serving at the entrance to the tent of meeting' (ʾet-hannāšîm haṣṣōḇĕʾôt petaḥ ʾōhel môʿēḏ). These women were mentioned in Exod 38:8 as performing some function at the tabernacle entrance. The violation of sanctuary servants by the priests is a desecration of both sanctuary and persons. The two violations together — theft from the altar (vv13-17) and sexual exploitation of sanctuary staff (v22) — constitute total inversion of the priestly calling: the priest who should guard the sacred space and protect the vulnerable has become both thief and predator.
2.23: Eli hears reports (šōmēaʿ ʾet-dĕḇārêhem) from all the people and verbally rebukes his sons — but does not remove them from office. This gap between hearing/knowing and acting to remove is the narrator's critique of Eli's leadership. The information is available; the authority is present; the will is absent. 3:13 will render the explicit verdict: 'his sons were blaspheming God, and he did not restrain them (wĕlōʾ kihāh bām).' The verb kāhāh (H3543, to restrain/rebuke/grow dim) connects Eli's failure to restrain his sons to his own 'growing dim' — the same root for both his ethical and physical weakness.
2.24: 'It is no good report that I hear the people of YHWH spreading about' — maʿăḇîrîm ʿam-YHWH (the people of YHWH spreading it). The šĕmûʿāh (H8052, report/fame) of YHWH's sanctuary in the community is at stake. The priests who should cause YHWH's name to be honored among his people are causing evil reports to spread instead. Ezek 36:20-23 diagnoses Israel's exile as the consequence of this same dynamic: profaning YHWH's name among the peoples. The priestly function of maintaining YHWH's honor in the community's speech is the inverse of what Hophni and Phinehas are producing.
2.25: 'If someone sins against a man, God can mediate (wĕpillel lô ʾĕlōhîm); but if someone sins against YHWH, who can intercede?' (mî yitpallel-lô). The root pālal (H6419) appears in both verbs: God mediates/arbitrates (piel: to serve as mediator) human-human conflicts; but for sin against YHWH there is no higher court of appeal. The distinction is crucial: the sins of Hophni and Phinehas are not merely against worshipers but against YHWH himself — they have stolen his portions and desecrated his house. 'But they would not listen to their father's voice, because YHWH desired to kill them' (kî ḥāpēṣ YHWH lahămîtām) — the divine will in their judicial hardening: YHWH's decision to judge them is the reason the rebuke cannot reach them.
2.26: 'Now the young man Samuel continued to grow both in stature and in favor with YHWH and with men' (wĕhannāʿar šĕmûʾēl hōlēḵ wĕḡāḏōl wāṭôḇ gam ʿim-YHWH wĕgam ʿim-ʾănāšîm). The formula of double-favor (with YHWH and with men) appears in Luke 2:52 for Jesus: 'And Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man.' Both Samuel and Jesus are described with this double-alignment formula. The OT use establishes the pattern: the covenant servant who grows in YHWH's estimation and in human recognition is the model for the one who will perfectly embody this double-favor in the messianic age.
2.27: An unnamed 'man of God' (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) delivers an oracle against Eli's house. The designation 'man of God' is used for prophets throughout the historical books (Moses in Deut 33:1; the unnamed prophet in Judg 13:6; Elijah in 1 Kgs 17:18; Elisha throughout 2 Kgs). The oracle begins with YHWH's prior revelation to the Aaronic priesthood in Egypt — 'niglōh niglêṯî' (I revealed — did I indeed reveal?) — the doubly emphatic infinitive absolute stressing the certainty and completeness of the original revelation. The oracle frames the judgment as covenant violation: YHWH gave extraordinary privilege; extraordinary privilege has been extraordinarily corrupted.
2.28: The threefold catalog of YHWH's gifts to Aaron's house: (1) chosen from all tribes; (2) altar access; (3) incense-burning. Plus: wearing the ephod; eating the fire offerings. Each item represents a layer of covenant privilege — access to the divine presence, mediation, oracular equipment, and sacrificial sustenance. The catalog's comprehensiveness makes the corruption more indictable: every dimension of covenant privilege has been turned into a vehicle for self-enrichment. The inventory of gift before the verdict of abuse is the OT's standard covenant-lawsuit pattern: 'I gave you everything; you turned everything into something for yourselves.'
2.29: 'Why do you scorn my sacrifices... and honor your sons above me?' (wattĕkabbēḏ ʾet-bānêḵā mimmennî). The verb kāḇēḏ (H3513, to honor/glorify — the root of kāḇôḏ, glory) used here and in v30: Eli has honored (kāḇēḏ) his sons more than he honored YHWH (mimmennî). The sin is one of disordered honor: giving to his sons the weight of priority that belongs to YHWH. This is the specific violation of the first commandment at the household level — functionally placing his sons before YHWH in the order of his decisive actions. The sin of inaction is the same as actively choosing his sons over YHWH.
2.30: 'Those who honor me I will honor; those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed' (kî mĕkabbĕḏay ʾăkabbēḏ ûḇōzay yēqāllû). This is the covenant honor-economy's most explicit statement: the kāḇēḏ (honor/weight) you give to YHWH is the measure of what you receive in return. bāzāh (H959, to despise/treat as contemptible) → qālal (H7043, to make light/contemptible). The reversal applies: Eli's sons who 'despised' (nāʾaṣ, v17) YHWH's offerings will themselves be made contemptible. The law of sowing and reaping applied to the honor economy of covenant relationship.
2.31: 'I will cut off your arm (zĕrôʿ) and the arm of your father's house, so that there will not be an old man in your house' — zĕrôʿ (H2220, arm/strength/line) means both physical power and genealogical line. The cutting off of zĕrôʿ combines the end of physical power and the end of genealogical continuation. Near fulfillment: the Nob massacre (22:18-19) kills 85 priests. Ultimate fulfillment: Solomon deposes Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:26-27), fulfilling 'I will cut off your arm from the altar.'
2.32: Eli's house will see the prosperity that comes to Israel through the faithful priest — the distress of the sanctuary will be witnessed by the deposed priestly house while YHWH's purposes succeed through another line. The judgment is not only on the guilty generation but on the lineage: the covenant privilege given to Aaron's house cannot be inherited by those who corrupt it. The revision of the eternal promise in v30 ('I promised... but now: far be it from me') demonstrates the conditional nature of priestly privilege — YHWH will fulfill his covenant through a faithful priest, but not through this house.
2.33: One survivor is preserved to weep — lĕḵallôt ʾet-ʿênāyw ûlĕdaʾăḇ ʾet-napšô (to wear out his eyes and to grieve his soul). The partial preservation is more bitter than total elimination: the survivor witnesses the complete fulfillment of the oracle without being able to prevent any of it. Fulfilled in Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:26-27), who is exiled to Anathoth and lives to see the Zadokite priesthood replace his line in Solomon's temple. The survivor who witnesses the judgment is the living sign of the oracle's complete fulfillment.
2.34: 'Both of them shall die on the same day' — the simultaneous death of Hophni and Phinehas as a covenant sign (ʾôt, H226). ʾôt is the same word for the circumcision sign (Gen 17:11) and the Exodus signs — a divine marker that the event is a judgment-act, not historical accident. The precise prediction (same day) makes the fulfillment unambiguous: when both sons die on the same day in 4:11, the entire oracle's reliability is confirmed. The sign-character of their simultaneous death establishes Samuel's prophetic authority — YHWH speaks through him what YHWH then fulfills exactly.
2.35: 'I will raise up for myself a faithful priest who shall do according to what is in my heart and in my mind' (kōhēn neʾĕmān kăʾăšer bilĕḇāḇî ûḇĕnapšî yāʿăśeh). neʾĕmān (H539, faithful/trustworthy — from ʾāman, the root of 'amen') characterizes the promised priest. The criterion of faithfulness — acting 'according to what is in my heart and in my mind' rather than what serves personal interest — is the inversion of Hophni and Phinehas who acted according to their own appetites. The near fulfillment is Zadok; the ultimate fulfillment is Christ as great high priest who acts entirely according to the Father's will (Heb 5:7-10: he learned obedience through what he suffered and was appointed high priest forever).
2.36: The survivors of Eli's house will come to bow before the faithful priest for a piece of silver or a morsel of bread — the descendants of the priests who stole sacrificial portions will beg for scraps. The inversion is precise: they took more than their portion by force (v16); they will receive far less than their portion by begging. The oracle closes the covenant-lawsuit with the pattern of complete reversal: what was taken out of place will be needed in diminishment. The faithful priest who receives the transferred privilege must do 'what is in YHWH's heart and mind' — the only basis on which covenant office can be legitimately held.

4.3: The elders' proposal — 'Let us bring the ark of the covenant of YHWH from Shiloh to us, so it may come among us and save us from the power of our enemies' — treats the Ark as a divine weapon that can be deployed independently of covenant faithfulness. The Ark is ʾărôn bərît YHWH (ark of the covenant of YHWH), the physical locus of divine presence between the cherubim. The elders' logic inverts the relationship: the Ark is to be brought to the army, not the army brought into covenant alignment with YHWH. The same magical theology of religious objects — use the right apparatus, get the right result — is the root error of every subsequent Israelite cult failure (Micah's shrine, Jeroboam's calves, the bronze serpent, Nehushtan). What cannot be manipulated is YHWH's sovereign presence; the Ark is not a talisman but a covenant sign.
4.4: The Ark is identified here as ʾărôn bərît YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt yōšēḇ hakkərūḇîm — 'the ark of the covenant of YHWH of armies who is enthroned upon the cherubim.' The title YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt (YHWH of armies/hosts) appears here for one of its first occurrences in the narrative corpus (also 1:3, 11; it is rare in the Pentateuch). The divine warrior epithet marks YHWH as the commander of cosmic and terrestrial armies — precisely the character that Israel is invoking when it brings the Ark into battle. The enthroned-upon-cherubim epithet identifies YHWH's throne-presence as the reality the Ark represents: the portable throne of the cosmic king. The irony is devastating — Israel attempts to manipulate into battle the throne of the One who cannot be manipulated.
4.21: The naming of the child ʾîḵāḇôḏ — 'Where is the glory?' or 'No glory' — is the Ark Narrative's theological center. Kāḇôḏ (כָּבוֹד) is the most comprehensive Hebrew term for YHWH's weighty, substantial, self-manifesting presence — the cloud-and-fire glory of Exod 16:10; 24:16; 40:34-35. The mother's explanation: gālāh kāḇôḏ miyyiśrāʾēl — 'the glory has gone into exile (gālāh) from Israel.' The verb gālāh is the exile verb — the same word used for Israel's deportation to Babylon. The glory departing is the beginning of the exile trajectory that will culminate in Ezekiel's vision of the divine chariot-throne rising from the temple and departing eastward (Ezek 10:18-19; 11:22-23). Ichabod is the OT's single most concentrated naming of divine absence.
4.1: The verse opens with vayehi devar-Shemu’el le-khol Yisra’el (“and the word of Samuel came to all Israel”). The clause is textually difficult: the Septuagint lacks it, and many commentators attach it as the closing sentence of ch. 3 rather than the opening of ch. 4. The MKT follows the MT reading that begins ch. 4 with this statement, functioning as an introduction to the Aphek battle. The next clause — Israel went out to fight the Philistines — is entirely without motivation in the Hebrew; the sequence word-of-Samuel / battle-outcome suggests the battle followed a prophetic summons, though the content of Samuel’s word is never given.
4.2: The battle spread (vattipal ha-milchamah, literally “the battle spread out”). The verb naphal (fall) with milchamah (battle) as subject is unusual; some render it as “the battle was joined” or “developed.” The MKT’s “battle spread” captures the sense of expanding engagement. Veyukku Yisra’el lifnei Pelishtim (“Israel was defeated before the Philistines”) — the verb nakah in the hophal (was struck/defeated) and the preposition lifnei (before/in front of) are standard military defeat terminology. Four thousand killed: the numbers in Samuel are often difficult textually but serve to calibrate the scale before the catastrophic second battle.
4.5: Vayari’u khol Yisra’el teru’ah gedolah vatiham ha-aretz — “all Israel raised such a great shout (teru’ah) that the ground shook.” The teru’ah is the battle cry/shout associated with YHWH’s presence throughout the Exodus and conquest traditions (Num 10:9; Josh 6:5). Its use here is significant: Israel treats the ark as a battle-magic object that will produce the teru’ah effect on demand. The ground shaking (vattiham ha-aretz) is theophanic language — a ground-quaking accompanies YHWH’s powerful presence in Ps 18:7 and elsewhere. But here the language of YHWH’s genuine presence frames YHWH’s absence.
4.6: The Philistines ask: mah qol ha-teru’ah ha-gedolah hazot (“What is this great shouting?”). The teru’ah is recognized as characteristically Israelite. The report that reaches the Philistines: ba aron YHWH el-ha-machaneh (“the ark of the LORD has come into the camp”). The term here is aron YHWH (ark of YHWH) — used by Israelites and Philistines interchangeably with aron ha-elohim (ark of God) across the narrative. The MKT consistently renders YHWH as LORD (small caps) and elohim as God; the ark’s identification as belonging to YHWH specifically carries covenantal weight.
4.7: Vayir’u ha-Pelishtim (“the Philistines were afraid”). The fear response mirrors the Rahab narrative (Josh 2:9-11: “I know that the LORD has given you the land”) and the Jethro narrative (Exod 18:11). The Philistine confession — ba elohim el-ha-machaneh (“a god has come into the camp”) — uses the generic elohim. The Philistines acknowledge divine power but do not know the divine name. Their exclamation oi lanu (“woe to us”) is an interjection of alarm (oi = woe); the doubled oi lanu in v. 8 intensifies the alarm rhetorically.
4.8: “These are the gods who struck the Egyptians with every kind of plague in the wilderness (bamidbar).” The Philistines confuse geography — the plagues were in Egypt, not the wilderness — but the narrative presents their report as plausible from their perspective. The word makkah (blow/plague/stroke) is the standard term for the Exodus plagues. Bekol makkah (“with every kind of blow”) is a comprehensive plural. The irony is layered: the Philistines fear YHWH’s genuine power but are wrong that the ark guarantees it will be deployed on Israel’s behalf.
4.9: The Philistine self-exhortation: hithazzeku vihyu le-anashim (“be strong and act like men”). The verb chazaq (hishtaphel: be strong/strengthen yourselves) and the idiom heyeh le-ish/anashim (be/become men) appears in the same breath at the critical moment of Joshua’s commission (Josh 1:6-9: chazaq ve-ematz) and David’s deathbed charge (1 Kgs 2:2). The Philistines borrow the vocabulary of YHWH’s warriors to bolster themselves against YHWH’s warriors. The fear-then-exhortation pattern is characteristic of ancient battle speeches found across Akkadian and Egyptian texts.
4.10: The second battle: Israel is defeated again; every man fled to his tent (vayanus ish le-ohalav). The phrase ish le-ohalav (each man to his tent) is an idiom for troop dispersal (2 Sam 20:1; 1 Kgs 12:16). The casualty figure: sheloshim elef ragli (“thirty thousand foot soldiers”). Ragli (foot soldier, from regel, foot) specifies infantry as distinct from chariotry. The tenfold increase from the first battle (4,000 to 30,000) is likely hyperbolic calibration rather than precise census data — a narrative device to communicate catastrophic defeat.
4.11: Varon ha-elohim nilkach (“the ark of God was captured”). The terse passive construction — subject first, passive verb second — mimics the shock of the event. Nilkach is niphal of lakach (take/seize): taken by an enemy, implying complete military defeat. Ushne benei Eli metu (“and Eli’s two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, died”). The waw-consecutive links ark-capture and sons’ death as a single disaster unit, fulfilling the oracle of 2:34 precisely. The brevity of the death report — no narrative of the dying, just the terse statement — is the narrator’s way of confirming prophetic fulfillment without dramatization.
4.12: The messenger from Benjamin: ben-Binyamin with torn garments (begadav qeru’im) and dust on his head (adama al-rosho). Both are standard mourning signs in the ancient Levant, indicating the bearer of bad news and the community’s distress (2 Sam 1:2; 13:31; Josh 7:6). The messenger ran (vayaratz) from the battlefield to Shiloh “on the same day” (bayyom hahu). The distance from Aphek (coastal plain) to Shiloh (central highlands) was approximately 30 km — a hard day’s run, emphasizing the urgency of the news.
4.13: Eli was sitting on his seat beside the road (al-ha-kisseh al-yad ha-derekh) watching (tzopeh), for “his heart trembled with anxiety (chared libbo) over the ark of God.” The verb charad (tremble/shake with anxiety) is used for Isaac’s trembling when he realized Jacob had deceived him (Gen 27:33) and for prophetic visions of terror (Dan 10:11). Eli’s anxiety is explicitly for the ark rather than for his sons — a detail that implicitly restores his theological priorities even as everything collapses around him. The whole city tzaachah (cried out) when the messenger arrived.
4.14: Eli heard the qol hatzeaqah (sound of the cry) and asked: meh qol he-hamon hazeh (“what is the meaning of this commotion?”). The word hamon (crowd noise/tumult) is the same word used for the great crowd that accompanied the ark (the Philistines would have used this cognate). The man (ha-ish) hurried and reported to Eli. The narrative pacing slows: the disaster is reported step by step, each element registering.
4.15: “Now Eli was ninety-eight years old (ben-tisha’im veshmone shanah), and his eyes were fixed (qamu einav) and he could not see.” The phrase qamu einav (his eyes stood/fixed) uses the verb qum (stand, arise) for failing eyesight — an unusual but contextually precise term for eyes that no longer move or track (related to the same term for “standing” eyes in cataract or extreme age). This verse provides the narrative context for why the messenger had to come to him rather than Eli reading the scene.
4.16: The messenger’s identification: anokhi ha-ba min ha-ma’arakhah (“I am the one who has come from the battle line”). The word ma’arakhah (battle array/formation) is the technical military term for a drawn-up battle line (cf. 17:8, 10: the Philistine challenge to Israel’s ma’arakhah in the Goliath narrative). Eli’s response — mah hayah ha-davar beni (“how did it go, my son?”) — uses the paternal beni (my son) for a stranger, consistent with his paternal address to Samuel throughout ch. 3. Eli’s relational warmth remains even in crisis.
4.17: The four-fold bad news: (1) Israel fled before the Philistines (nas Yisra’el lifnei Pelishtim); (2) a great slaughter among the people (mageiphah gedolah ba’am); (3) your two sons are dead (gam shnei vanekha metu); (4) the ark of God has been captured (va-aron ha-elohim nilkachah). The sequence reserves the worst for last. Gam (“also/even”) before the sons’ death adds incremental weight. The ark news comes last — the disaster Eli feared most. Nilkachah here uses the feminine suffix because aron (ark) is occasionally treated as grammatically feminine, or the suffix may refer to the battle event.
4.18: When he mentioned the ark of God (kehicir et-aron ha-elohim), Eli fell backward (vayipol me-achore) from his seat by the gate, his neck broke (vattishshaver maphreqeto) and he died. Maphreq (neck/nape) appears only here and in this form in the Hebrew Bible; it may refer to the cervical vertebra or the neck joint. The verb shavar (break) is standard. The notation “for the man was old and heavy (kaved ha-ish)” provides physical causation. Kaved (heavy/weighty) is used both literally (physical weight) and metaphorically in 1 Sam for the weight of honor/glory. Eli’s physical heaviness mirrors the weightiness of what has just been lost. He judged Israel forty years (arba’im shanah) — a round number suggesting a generation of leadership.
4.19: Phinehas’ wife, pregnant and near term (harah la-lut laledeth), heard the reports and went into labor (vattikhraf vateled). The verb karaf (crouch/squat down) is the giving-birth posture — the same root appears in the description of Deborah’s battle victory where enemies fell, bowed, lay still (Judg 5:27). The birth pains (tzir’oteha) came upon her. Tzir is the standard term for labor pains but also appears metaphorically for the convulsive distress of divine judgment (Isa 13:8; 26:17; Jer 22:23). In context, the birth and the national catastrophe are linguistically merged.
4.20: At the moment of her death (ule-et mutah), the women attending her (ha-nitzavot aleha) said: “Do not be afraid (al-tiri’i), for you have given birth to a son.” The standard birth-reassurance formula appears here with tragic irony: the formula “do not be afraid, for you have a son” (cf. the angel’s words to Hagar in Gen 21:17, to Manoah’s wife in Judg 13:3) typically signals rescue and hope. Here the mother “did not answer or take notice (lo anah ve-lo samah libbo)” — she is beyond consolation. The birth of a son cannot compensate for the triple loss she has absorbed.
4.22: She repeated: galah kavod me-Yisra’el (“the glory has departed from Israel”). The word kavod (glory, weight, honor) is one of the most theologically freighted terms in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH’s kavod is his manifest presence (Exod 16:10; 24:16; 40:34; Ezek 1:28). The name Ichabod (ikavod) is typically parsed as i-kavod (no glory) or ai kavod (where is the glory?). The repetition of the departure formula in vv. 21-22 marks it as a climactic theological statement, not merely a personal naming. The departure of the ark = the departure of YHWH’s kavod. Ezekiel’s vision of the glory departing from the temple (Ezek 10-11) is the precise structural parallel to this moment.

5.3: Dagon falls on his face before the Ark: wayyipōl dāgôn lᵉpānāyw ʾarṣāh — 'Dagon fell on his face to the ground' in the classic prostration posture of a vassal before a superior. Dāgôn is the chief deity of the Philistine cities, attested in texts from Ugarit (as Baal's father), Mari, and the Tell Dan stele context. The name is disputed — possibly from dāg (fish) or dāgān (grain). The prostration of the Philistine deity before YHWH's Ark is the text's argument: YHWH's sovereign presence does not require Israelite compliance to dominate rival deities; even in captivity, the covenant God of Israel reduces the most powerful Philistine god to prostration. The theme recurs in Acts 5 (Ananias and Sapphira falling before the Spirit's judgment) and Phil 2:10 (every knee bowing at the name of Jesus).
5.4: The second morning: Dagon's head and both hands are severed and lie on the threshold — only dāgôn dāgôn, his torso, remains. The amputation of head and hands is the fate of defeated warriors (cf. 1 Sam 17:51 — David cuts off Goliath's head; 31:9 — the Philistines cut off Saul's head). Dagon is thus treated as a defeated enemy-soldier, his power symbolically neutralized. The word miptān (threshold) marks the place as the boundary where the Dagon cult's authority ended; the narrator notes that the Philistine priests 'do not tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod to this day' — a surviving cultic taboo preserving the memory of this defeat embedded in Philistine practice itself.
5.6: The yāḏ YHWH — 'hand of YHWH' — is the theological category for direct divine action. The phrase appears six times in the Ark Narrative (5:6, 7, 9, 11; 6:3, 5), transforming the entire Philistine ordeal into a demonstration of YHWH's sovereign intervention without Israelite agency. Yāḏ YHWH is the Exodus vocabulary: the ten plagues are the work of YHWH's 'mighty hand and outstretched arm' (Deut 26:8). The Philistine ordeal is a mini-Exodus in reverse — YHWH operates in Philistine territory with the same plagues-and-forced-release pattern: tumors and mice parallel the Exodus plagues; the gold offerings parallel the despoliation of Egypt; the Ark's return on a cart parallels Israel's departure from Egypt. The Philistine priests make this explicit in 6:6: 'Why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts?'
5.1: The Philistines brought (vayyiqchu) the ark from Ebenezer to Ashdod. Ashdod was the most important of the five Philistine cities (Gaza, Gath, Ashkelon, Ekron, Ashdod), located on the coastal plain approximately 50 km south of Joppa. The Philistines appear to have had a central sanctuary there for Dagon. The movement of captured enemy cult objects to the victor’s temple was standard ANE practice: the Marduk statue was famously taken by the Hittites and later by the Babylonians; the Assyrians catalogued divine statues among their war trophies. The Philistines are following standard protocol.
5.2: The Philistines placed (vayatzigu) the ark beside Dagon (etzel Dagon). The verb natzag (hiphil: set up, place upright) is used for statues being set up. Dagon is a grain deity known from Ugaritic texts and widely worshiped in Canaan and Philistia (Judg 16:23: Samson is brought to Dagon’s temple in Gaza). The placement beside Dagon implies acknowledgment of YHWH as a subordinate deity — a trophy god next to the main god. The ark is being treated as the divine equivalent of a captured general’s standard.
5.5: The threshold-avoidance etiology: al-ken lo yidrekhu kohanei Dagon ve-khol ha-ba’im el-beit Dagon al-miphtan Dagon be-Ashdod ad ha-yom hazeh. The miphtan (threshold) of Dagon’s house became a no-step zone because the head and hands of Dagon had fallen there. Threshold theology is well attested in the ANE: thresholds were liminal spaces associated with protective spirits (lamassu in Mesopotamia). The etiology uses the standard formula al-ken...ad ha-yom hazeh (“therefore...to this day”) to connect ancient event to current practice — the same formula in Josh 4:9; 7:26; 8:28, etc.
5.7: Vayir’u anshe-Ashdod ki-chen (“when the men of Ashdod saw what was happening” — literally “that it was so”). The pattern of divine plagues driving the Philistines to transfer the ark echoes the Exodus plagues that drove the Egyptians to release Israel. Philistine counsel to send away the ark mirrors Pharaoh’s successive capitulations. The phrase lo yeshev aron elohei Yisra’el ittanu (“the ark of the God of Israel must not stay with us”) acknowledges that the continuing presence of YHWH’s ark is incompatible with Philistine wellbeing — a remarkable admission.
5.8: The five lords of the Philistines (sarnei Pelishtim) are called to deliberate. Seren (lord/ruler of a Philistine city) is a technical term that appears only in 1 Samuel and Judges in the Hebrew Bible; it may be cognate with Greek tyrannos (tyrant/ruler) or with a Philistine root. The term consistently designates the political leadership of the pentapolis (Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron). The deliberative process — gathering all the lords to decide what to do with the ark — reflects the confederacy structure of Philistine governance.
5.9: Yad YHWH (“the hand of the LORD”) is the primary divine action idiom across the Exodus narrative and is used consistently for divine judgment in 1 Samuel (vv. 6, 7, 9, 11; 7:13). The phrase mehumah gedolah me’od (“a very great panic/confusion”) — mehumah is divine-warrior panic terminology; YHWH sends mehumah on enemies (Deut 7:23; 1 Sam 7:10; 14:20). The tumors (Hebrew ophalim, elsewhere techorim — the two terms are used interchangeably; some manuscripts read one for the other) may correspond to bubonic plague symptoms: swollen lymph nodes (buboes). The Septuagint adds a reference to mice at this point, filling in the narrative gap.
5.10: Transfer to Ekron: vayishlecho et-aron ha-elohim Eqron. Ekron was the northernmost Philistine city, closest to Israelite territory. The Ekronites’ outcry — hesevu et-aron elohei Yisra’el eilai lahamiteni ve-et-ammi (“They have brought the ark of the God of Israel to us to kill us and our people”) — recognizes that the ark’s presence is lethal. The progression Ashdod → Gath → Ekron traces the arc of Philistine decision-making and the unstoppable force of divine judgment driving them toward return of the ark.
5.11: The Ekronites sent and gathered all the lords of the Philistines: shallechu et-aron elohei Yisra’el ve-yashuv limqomo (“Send away the ark of the God of Israel, and let it return to its own place”). Lashuv limqomo (return to its place) — the vocabulary of return to proper location is used in the priestly legislation for objects returning to their proper owners (Lev 25:10: ve-shavtem ish el-achuzzato, each returning to his possession). The ark has a proper place and belongs there; the Philistines can only hold it temporarily. Mehumah-mawet (“deadly panic”) is an intensified compound: the panic that causes/leads to death.
5.12: Those who did not die were struck with tumors (vayukku ba-ophalim). Vata’al shav’at ha-ir ha-shamayim (“and the cry of the city went up to heaven”). The phrase ta’alah shav’atam (their cry going up to heaven) echoes the cry of the Israelites in Egypt (Exod 2:23: vata’al shav’atam el ha-elohim) — but here it is the cry of YHWH’s enemies under his judgment rather than YHWH’s people under oppression. The Ark Narrative uses the Exodus vocabulary to describe a reverse-Exodus: YHWH fighting against the oppressor from within the oppressor’s territory.

6.6: The Philistine priests' reference to Egypt: lāmmāh tᵉḵabbəḏû ʾet lᵉḇaḇḵem kaʾăšer kibbəḏû miṣrayim wəparʿōh ʾet libbām — 'Why do you harden your hearts (kāḇad = to make heavy) as Egypt and Pharaoh hardened their hearts?' The Exodus vocabulary is deliberately employed: the verb for 'harden' here is kāḇad (to be heavy), the same root as kāḇôḏ (glory). The Philistine priests have theological knowledge of the Exodus — a remarkable detail that positions the Ark Narrative as an extension of YHWH's sovereignty beyond Israel's borders. Pharaoh hardened his heart (kāḇad) and was destroyed; the Philistines are urged to learn from that precedent rather than repeat it. The same vocabulary of 'hardening' recurs in Mark 6:52 (the disciples' hardened hearts after the feeding) and Heb 3:8 (the warning not to harden hearts as in the wilderness).
6.19: YHWH strikes the men of Beth-shemesh for looking into the Ark — kî rāʾû bǎʾărôn YHWH, 'for they looked into the ark of YHWH.' Beth-shemesh (bêt šemeš, 'house of the sun') is a sun-cult city; the men treat the Ark with the same casual curiosity appropriate to a cult object that could be inspected. The holiness of the Ark is absolute — it cannot be looked into any more than YHWH's face can be seen directly (Exod 33:20). The number struck (50,070 in the MT, probably a textual corruption) is less important than the theological point: proximity to divine holiness without covenant preparation is lethal. This is the theological ground of the NT's 'our God is a consuming fire' (Heb 12:29) and of the NT's emphasis on Christ as the mediator who makes approach to the holy God possible (Heb 10:19-22 — 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus').
6.1: Varon YHWH beshteh Pelishtim shivah chadashim (“the ark of the LORD was in Philistine territory for seven months”). The seven-month figure establishes the duration of the ordeal. Seven is the standard period of completeness/fullness in the Hebrew Bible (seven days, seven years, etc.). The ark’s seven-month captivity is a period of divine demonstration — sufficient to establish unambiguously that YHWH’s power operates independently of Israel’s military fortunes. The date formula (seven months) also allows the narrative to move efficiently from the disaster to the return without detailed chronological tracking.
6.2: The Philistines called their priests and diviners (kohanav ve-qosemav). Qosem (diviner) is from qasam (to divine/practice divination) — condemned in Israel (Deut 18:10, 14) but standard for Philistine religious specialists. The priests (kohanim) and diviners represent religious expertise; they are consulted because the ark’s return requires ritual precision. The question posed: mah na’aseh l’aron YHWH, hodi’enu bameh neshalchennu limqomo (“tell us how we should send it back to its place”). The request for instruction (hodi’enu, hiphil imperative of yada’) frames the consultation as technical religious inquiry.
6.3: The priests’ counsel: do not send it empty-handed (al-tishlechu oto reqam) but with a guilt offering (asham). The asham in Mosaic law is the reparation offering for inadvertent trespass against holy things (Lev 5:14-6:7): it involves restitution plus 20% to the wronged party. Here Philistine priests apply an analogous logic: an asham must accompany the ark because YHWH has been wronged by its capture and detention. Az yerapheh lakhem (“then you will be healed”) promises healing upon proper reparation. Venodah lakhem lammah lo-tasur yado mikem (“you will understand why his hand has not turned away from you”) — the reparation offering will clarify the causal relationship between offense and punishment.
6.4: Five golden tumors (ophalei zahav) and five golden mice (akhbarei zahav) corresponding to the five Philistine lords and cities. The principle of symbolic equivalence underlies the offering: the images represent the specific afflictions so that the deity can “take back” or release the curse through its representative symbol. This is consistent with Mesopotamian ritual practice of creating effigies of afflictions (Akkadian maqlû and shurpu texts describe burning effigies to remove curses). The mice (akhbar) are typically identified as field mice or rats, associated with plague transmission in many ancient sources and filling in the plague-detail suppressed in the Hebrew text of ch. 5.
6.5: Va’asitem tzalmei ophalekhem ve-tzalmei akhbareikhem hamshichitim et-ha-aretz (“make images of your tumors and images of the mice that are ravaging your land”). The word tzelem (image/likeness) is the same word used in Gen 1:26-27 for humanity as tzelem elohim (image of God) and for the condemned images of Num 33:52. Its use for plague-effigies is ironic: the Philistine ritual inverts the concept of tzelem from being-in-the-image-of to representing-a-plague-image. Vetenu likvodr elohei Yisra’el (“give glory to the God of Israel”) — the Philistine priests counsel acknowledging YHWH’s kavod as the condition of release. Directly after the Ichabod-departure theology, the narrative shows YHWH’s kavod being acknowledged even by his enemies.
6.7: The test setup for the ark’s return: a new cart (agalah chadashah) and two nursing cows (parot alot asher lo-alah aleihen ol — “milk cows that have never been yoked”). The agalah chadashah (new/unused cart) echoes the requirement for dedicated sacred objects to be new and previously unconsecrated. The un-yoked cows are the ANE equivalent of an untouched/pure instrument: an animal that has never served any human purpose cannot have been ritually contaminated. The calves are to be taken away from the cows (ve-et banim hashevu me-achareihem ha-bayitah) — this is the critical test-condition, since cows would instinctively follow their calves rather than an unknown route.
6.8: The ark on the cart with the asham offerings in a box (aron) beside it. The ark and the guilt-offering box travel together — the offering accompanies the one to whom it is addressed. Veshalchtem oto vehalachu (“send it off and let it go”) — the double imperative/jussive form expresses the combination of human action (send it off) and resulting independent motion (let it go on its own). The test’s logic: if the cows go of their own accord to Israelite territory against their natural maternal instinct, that is a sign of divine direction.
6.9: The interpretive key: im derekh gvulah ya’aleh bet-shemesh (“if it goes up toward its own territory, to Beth-shemesh”) — then YHWH did this. If not: hayah lanu miqreh hu (“it happened to us by chance”). The word miqreh (chance/accident) appears in Eccl 2:14-15; 3:19; 9:2-3 as a philosophically charged term for events that happen to all without apparent cause. The Philistines deploy it as the counter-hypothesis to divine action: either YHWH did this, or it was random (miqreh). The narrative implicitly refutes the miqreh hypothesis: the cows go straight to Beth-shemesh (v. 12), mooing as they go (against natural behavior), without deviation.
6.10: Vaya’asu ha-anashim ken (“the men did so”). The report of compliance is terse — a single clause. The detailed instructions of vv. 7-9 are executed without comment. The narrative interest is in the result rather than the execution. Vayiqchu shetei farot alot — the noun parah (cow) used with alot (nursing/suckling) specifies lactating cows that would be under strong instinctive pull toward their confined calves.
6.11: The ark placed on the cart, the chest with the golden mice and golden tumor-images beside it. The itemization confirms the instructions were followed precisely: the narrative validates obedience. The two-item cargo — ark and asham-chest — corresponds to the two elements of the offering: the divine presence (ark) and the reparation offering (chest). The pairing mirrors the Levitical pattern of presenting both the object of worship and the accompanying offering.
6.12: Vayyisharnah ha-parot baderekh al derekh bet-shemesh (“the cows went straight in the direction of Beth-shemesh along one road”). The verb yashar (go straight, go directly) is a loaded term in Hebrew: ha-yashar be-einei YHWH (“what is right/straight in YHWH’s eyes”) is the covenantal standard of behavior. The cows behave with the moral and directional rectitude of creatures responding to YHWH’s guidance. They did not turn to the right or left (lo saru yamin u-smol) — the idiom for covenantal fidelity (Deut 5:32; Josh 1:7). Veyigalu sarnei Pelishtim achareihem (“the Philistine lords followed them as far as the border”): the lords serve as witnesses to YHWH’s sign.
6.13: The Beth-shemeshites were reaping the wheat harvest (qotzerim qatzir-chittim ba-emeq) when they saw the ark. Qatzir chittim (wheat harvest) occurs in late spring (May-June), providing a seasonal marker. The sight of the ark prompted rejoicing (vayismechu lir’ot). Beth-shemesh was a Levitical city (Josh 21:16), which explains both the presence of qualified handlers (Levites, v. 15) and the enthusiastic reception.
6.14: The cart stopped (vataamod) at the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh. There was a large stone there (ve-sham aven gedolah); they split the wood of the cart (vayebaqe’u et atsei ha-agalah) and offered the cows as a burnt offering (olah laYHWH). The use of the cart wood and the cows as immediate offering material is an act of improvised sanctification: the materials that served as the sacred transport become the fuel and substance of the first offering upon return. The economy of consecration: nothing used in YHWH’s service is diverted to profane use.
6.15: The Levites took down the ark and the chest and set them on the large stone (al-ha-aven ha-gedolah). The large stone functions as a temporary altar. Vaya’alu olot vayizbechu zevachim bayyom hahu laYHWH (“offered burnt offerings and made sacrifices to the LORD that day”). The dual offering — olah (wholly burned, expressing total dedication) and zevach (shared meal offering) — marks the occasion as a full covenantal celebration of the ark’s return.
6.16: Va-yir’u chamishah sarnei Pelishtim vayyashubu Eqron bayyom hahu (“when the five lords of the Philistines saw it, they returned to Ekron that day”). The lords who followed as witnesses now depart as witnesses: having confirmed the sign (the cows went straight to Beth-shemesh), their role in the narrative is complete. Their return to Ekron closes the loop of 5:10-6:16.
6.17: The enumeration of the five golden tumors: Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron — the complete pentapolis. Ophalei zahav asher heshibu Pelishtim asham laYHWH (“the golden tumors that the Philistines returned as a guilt offering to the LORD”). The use of asham for the Philistine offering is the narrator’s validation of the Philistine priests’ ritual theology: what they called an asham functions as one. YHWH can receive acknowledgment of wrong even from those outside the covenant.
6.18: The golden mice corresponding to all Philistine cities — fortified cities and unwalled villages (me-ir mivtzar ve-ad kopher ha-parazi). The geographical completeness (fortified to unfortified, city to hamlet) parallels the theological comprehensiveness of the plague: no Philistine settlement was immune. Vaha’aven ha-gedolah asher hinichu aleha et-aron YHWH (“the large stone on which they set down the ark of the LORD is a witness to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh”). The etiological formula ad ha-yom hazeh (to this day) again anchors the narrative event in a physical landmark.
6.20: Mi yukhal la-amod lifnei YHWH ha-elohim ha-qadosh hazeh (“who is able to stand before the LORD, this holy God?”). The question is the Beth-shemeshite response to the deaths among them for looking into the ark. La-amod lifnei YHWH (to stand before YHWH) is covenant-service vocabulary: the priest who “stands before YHWH” (Deut 10:8; 18:7) to minister. The deaths demonstrate that standing before YHWH’s holiness requires qualification. The theological question — who can stand before this holy God? — is ultimately answered by Ps 24:3-4 (the one with clean hands and a pure heart) and definitively by Heb 4:14-16 (the great high priest who enables us to “approach the throne of grace with confidence”).
6.21: The Beth-shemeshites sent to Kiriath-jearim: heshibu Pelishtim et-aron YHWH (“the Philistines have returned the ark of the LORD”). Redu, ha’alu oto eleikhem (“come down and take it up to your place”). The directional verbs are geographically precise: Kiriath-jearim was in the Judean highlands above Beth-shemesh (going up = going to higher ground). The ark will remain at Kiriath-jearim for twenty years (7:2) before David retrieves it (2 Sam 6). The ark’s long residence there, largely forgotten until David, represents the interval between the Ark Narrative’s conclusion and the Davidic era.

7.3: Samuel's call to national repentance uses the technical vocabulary of covenant renewal: hāšîḇû ʾet lᵉḇaḇḵem ʾel YHWH wəʿiḇdûhû lᵉḇaḏô — 'Return your hearts to YHWH and serve him alone.' The verbs šûḇ (return/repent) and ʿāḇaḏ (serve/worship) are the covenant's core demands. The call to remove the bəʿālîm and ʿaštārôt — the plural of Baal and Astarte, the dominant Canaanite male and female deities — is the standard formula for covenant renewal (cf. Josh 24:14; Judg 10:16). Lᵉḇaḏô (him alone) is the exclusivity principle of the Shema: YHWH's covenant requires singular devotion, not mere priority. Jesus cites the Shema's lᵉḇaḏô principle directly: 'Worship the Lord your God and serve him only' (Matt 4:10, citing Deut 6:13).
7.12: Samuel erects the memorial stone and names it ʾeḇen hāʿēzer — 'stone of help': wayyiqrāʾ ʾet šəmāh ʾeḇen hāʿēzer lēʾmōr ʿad hēnnāh ʿăzārānû YHWH — 'he called its name Stone of Help, saying, “Until now YHWH has helped us.”' This is a counter-naming: in 4:1 Israel was defeated at a place that would become Ebenezer (named before the battle), a name that now takes on a new meaning. Samuel plants a covenant-memory marker in the landscape — YHWH's faithfulness made permanent in geography. The ʿad hēnnāh ('until now') phrase is the covenant retrospect formula: all of YHWH's past faithfulness concentrates in this moment. The stone of help is the OT equivalent of the NT's 'He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion' (Phil 1:6) — divine faithfulness marked and remembered.
7.1: Vayavo’u anshe Qiryat-ye’arim (“the men of Kiriath-jearim came”). Kiriath-jearim (city of forests) was on the Judah-Benjamin border, identified with modern Abu Ghosh. The ark was placed in the house of Abinadab (beit Avinadav) on the hill (bagiv’ah). Eleazar his son (Et-El’azar beno) was consecrated (qiddeshu oto) to guard (lishmor) the ark. The consecration verb qadash (piel: to set apart for holy purpose) designates Eleazar as the ark’s dedicated guardian. The informal consecration at a private home rather than at the tabernacle reflects the post-Shiloh disruption of the central sanctuary.
7.2: Vayehi miyor’adi ha-aron beQiryat-ye’arim vayirbu ha-yamim vayihyu esrim shanah (“from the day the ark remained in Kiriath-jearim a long time passed — twenty years”). The phrase vayirbu ha-yamim (the days multiplied/accumulated) expresses the long duration colloquially before the specific figure twenty years is given. Vayinahu khol-beit Yisra’el acharei YHWH (“all the house of Israel mourned and yearned after the LORD”). The verb nachah (niphal: to lament, mourn; or to be drawn toward) is textually debated. Some read it as “lamented” (mourning the ark’s absence); others as “was drawn toward/longed for” YHWH. Either reading captures the spiritual hunger that precedes Samuel’s national revival call in v. 3.
7.4: The people obeyed: vayasiru benei Yisra’el et-ha-b’alim ve-et-ha-ashtarot (“the Israelites put away the Baals and the Ashtaroth”). The Baals (ha-b’alim) and Ashtaroth (ha-ashtarot) are collective terms for the Canaanite storm deity and fertility goddess respectively. B’al (master/husband/owner) was the chief storm deity; Astarte/Asherah was his consort and fertility goddess. These are the standard idolatrous alternatives to YHWH throughout Judges and Samuel. Vaya’avdu et YHWH levado (“and served the LORD alone”): levado (alone/only) is the monotheistic dedication term, echoing the Shema’s YHWH echad.
7.5: Samuel’s call to assemble at Mizpah (qibetzu et-khol Yisra’el ha-Mitzpah). Mizpah was a significant cultic and judicial gathering place in Benjamin (Judg 20:1; 21:1, 8; later a prominent site in the period of the judges). Samuel’s declaration: ve-etpallel ba’adkem el YHWH (“I will intercede for you before the LORD”). The verb palal (hitpael: intercede/pray on behalf of) identifies Samuel’s intercessory role. Moses was the paradigmatic intercessor (Exod 32:11-13; Num 14:13-19); Samuel stands in the same tradition. 1 Sam 12:23: “far be it from me to sin against the LORD by ceasing to pray for you.”
7.6: At Mizpah: they drew water and poured it out before YHWH (vayyishavu mayim vayishpechu lifnei YHWH). The water-pouring rite is unique here — not prescribed in the Mosaic legislation. It appears to be a penitential rite signifying the outpouring of soul/spirit (cf. Hannah’s “pouring out my soul” at 1:15; Lam 2:19). They fasted (vayatzumu) and confessed: chatanu laYHWH (“we have sinned against the LORD”). The three-part penitential sequence — water-pouring, fasting, verbal confession — anticipates the formal confession liturgy of Neh 9 and Dan 9. Vayishpot Shemu’el et-benei Yisra’el ba-Mitzpah: Samuel’s role as judge at Mizpah is given in the same verse as the national assembly — the revival and the judicial function are simultaneous.
7.7: The Philistines heard about the assembly and marched up (vaya’alu sarnei Pelishtim el Yisra’el). Vayishme’u benei Yisra’el vayire’u mipnei Pelishtim (“the Israelites, hearing this, were afraid of the Philistines”). The juxtaposition is pointed: Israel just assembled for national repentance and immediately faces military threat. The fear (yare’) is genuine — the word is standard military fear vocabulary. The newly repentant community must now discover whether YHWH’s response to repentance extends to military deliverance.
7.8: The people’s request to Samuel: al-tachrash mimmennu mizzaqeq el YHWH elohenu (“do not stop crying out to the LORD our God for us”). The verb charash (be silent, refrain) negated: “do not be silent.” Zaqaq is unusual — the MT has mizzaqeq el YHWH; some read mize’oq (from crying out). Either way, the request is for unceasing intercession. Veyoshi’enu miyad Pelishtim (“that he may save us from the hand of the Philistines”): the request combines intercession and expected deliverance — the same connection Samuel will make in 12:23.
7.9: Samuel offered a nursing lamb (taleh chalav echad) as a whole burnt offering (olah kalil laYHWH). Taleh (nursing lamb) is a tender designation — a young lamb still dependent on its mother. Olah kalil is the wholly-consumed offering; kalil (complete/total) emphasizes that nothing is retained by the offerer. Vayizaq Shemu’el el YHWH ba’ad Yisra’el (“Samuel cried out to the LORD on Israel’s behalf”). The double action — offering and intercession simultaneously — defines the prophetic-priestly function Samuel exercises: the offering is the altar-expression; the cry is the verbal expression of the same petition. Vaya’anehu YHWH: “the LORD answered him” — the terse divine response confirms the intercession’s efficacy.
7.10: As Samuel was offering the olah, the Philistines advanced to attack. YHWH ra’am (thundered) with qol gadol (a great voice/thunder) against the Philistines and vayyahamem (threw them into panic/confusion). Ra’am (thunder) is the theophanic weapon of YHWH: Ps 18:13-14; 29:3-9 describe YHWH’s voice as thunder that splits oaks and shakes the wilderness. Hamam (confuse/panic) is the divine-warrior verb from the Exodus and conquest: Exod 14:24 (“YHWH looked down and threw the Egyptians into confusion”); Josh 10:10 (at Gibeon). The combination of thunder-theophany and hamam-panic is a signature formula for YHWH fighting on Israel’s behalf.
7.11: Israel pursued the Philistines from Mizpah and struck them down as far as Beth-car (ad-tachat bet-kar). Beth-car is unidentified; the name may mean “house of the lamb/young ram.” The pursuit pattern (enemy routed, Israel pursues) is standard conquest/battle narrative: Josh 10:10; Judg 4:16. The geographical terminus marks the completeness of the victory.
7.13: Vayikannu ha-Pelishtim velo yasafu od lavo bigevul Yisra’el (“the Philistines were brought low and did not again enter Israelite territory”). Kana’ (niphal: be subdued/humbled) is the term for YHWH’s humbling of Israel’s enemies after divine-warrior victories (Judg 8:28; 11:33). The clause vatehi yad YHWH ba-Pelishtim kol yemei Shemu’el (“the hand of the LORD was against the Philistines throughout Samuel’s lifetime”) is a retrospective summary that frames Samuel’s entire judgeship as characterized by this divine restraint on Philistia — a marked contrast with the chaos of the Ark Narrative period.
7.14: The territorial restoration: cities “from Ekron to Gath” (meEqron ve-ad Gath) were returned to Israel. Ve-et gevulotehen hitzil Yisra’el miyad Pelishtim (“Israel recovered their surrounding territories from Philistine control”). Natsal (hiphil: rescue/deliver) is the standard deliverance verb of the Judges-Samuel era (cf. 4:3; 7:8). Vayehi shalom bein Yisra’el ubein ha-Emori (“there was also peace between Israel and the Amorites”). The Amorites (a blanket term for pre-Israelite Canaanite peoples) are mentioned here as a separate peace agreement, suggesting the Philistine defeat also stabilized relations with the inland populations.
7.15: Vayishpot Shemu’el et Yisra’el kol yemei chayyav (“Samuel judged Israel throughout his lifetime”). The lifelong judicial tenure contrasts with the sequential judges of the book of Judges who arose, delivered, and died. Samuel’s continuous tenure makes him transitional: he is the last of the judges and the inaugurator of the monarchic period. The shafat (judge) function in the Deuteronomistic framework includes both military deliverance and juridical administration; Samuel performs both.
7.16: Samuel’s annual circuit: halachu mide-shanah beshanah vesavar Beit-el ve-ha-Gilgal ha-Mitzpah (“each year he made a circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah”). The three sites are major cultic-judicial centers: Bethel (patriarchal altar, Gen 12:8; 28:19), Gilgal (first Israelite camp in Canaan, Josh 4:19-20), Mizpah (assembly site, 7:5-11). Samuel’s circuit institutionalizes itinerant justice, making the judicial system accessible rather than centralized. The circuit judge as travelling administrator of justice anticipates the later administrative structures of Jehoshaphat (2 Chr 19:5-11).
7.17: Samuel’s home base was Ramah (uve-Ramah ki-sham beito), where he lived, judged Israel (shafat et Yisra’el), and built an altar to YHWH (vayiven sham mizbeach laYHWH). The altar at Ramah is the only altar Samuel builds; its construction at his home suggests a semi-permanent local sanctuary. Pre-Solomonic altars at local sites were not prohibited; the centralization legislation of Deut 12 was not yet fully operative (or was routinely set aside in this period). The altar functions as the visible marker of Samuel’s priestly-prophetic office at Ramah.

8.5: Israel's demand: śîmāh lānû melek lᵉšāpəṭēnû kəḵol haggôyim — 'give us a king to judge us like all the nations.' The phrase kəḵol haggôyim (like all the nations) is the demand's theological indictment: Israel's covenant identity is precisely its differentiation from the nations. Deut 17:14-20 anticipated this moment and regulated kingship — kings were permitted but must not multiply horses (military alliance with Egypt), wives (foreign treaties), or silver and gold (autonomous wealth). The demand here goes beyond the Deuteronomic provision: they want a king not to embody covenant rule but to replace YHWH as the source of security and governance. Lᵉšāpəṭēnû (to judge us) — the same verb šāpaṭ used for the judges — signals that they want a permanent institutional judge, not the crisis-charismatic judges YHWH has been providing.
8.7: YHWH's verdict to Samuel: kî lōʾ ʾōtəḵā māʾāsû kî ʾōtî māʾāsû mimᵉlōḵ ʿălêhem — 'for it is not you they have rejected, but me they have rejected from being king over them.' The verb māʾas (to reject, despise) is the same word used in 15:23 and 15:26 when YHWH rejects Saul. The rejection is mutual and sequential: Israel rejects YHWH as king; YHWH will later reject Saul as the king Israel chose. The theological structure is precise: melek ʿălêhem (king over them) — YHWH's kingship is the real category at stake. The entire book of 1 Samuel is framed by two rejections: Israel rejects YHWH's kingship in ch8; YHWH rejects Saul's kingship in ch15. Between them is the question of what kind of king YHWH will provide — which chs 16-31 and the entire Davidic tradition answer.
8.9: Samuel is commanded to warn Israel of the mišpaṭ hammelek — 'the manner/justice of the king.' The word mišpaṭ normally refers to covenant justice — YHWH's justice, the standard by which leaders are to be measured. Its use here for royal taxation and conscription is ironic: the 'justice' of a human king is coercive extraction, the opposite of the mišpaṭ YHWH's covenant produces. The list (vv11-17): sons taken for the chariot corps and harvest work, daughters for perfumers and cooks, fields and vineyards taken, a ten-percent tithe to royal servants — is the exact catalog of Solomonic administrative excess documented in 1 Kgs 4-5. The warning Samuel gives is the structural analysis of kingship that will be precisely fulfilled in the Solomonic monarchy.
8.1: Vayehi ki-zaqen Shemu’el vayasem et-banav shoftim le-Yisra’el (“when Samuel grew old, he appointed his sons as judges over Israel”). The appointment of sons as successors repeats the pattern of Eli (corrupt sons). Yasem (hiphil of sum: to set/appoint) is the standard appointment verb. The dynastic impulse — appointing family members rather than gifted successors — is identified as a structural vulnerability of the pre-monarchic arrangement. Samuel’s sons will fail as judges; this failure becomes the proximate cause of the monarchic demand.
8.2: The firstborn was named Joel (Yo’el, “YHWH is God”) and the second Abijah (Aviyah, “YHWH is my father”). Both names are theophoric — containing the divine name. The irony: sons with names declaring YHWH’s sovereignty and fatherhood will repudiate both. They served as judges at Beersheba (ba-Beer-sheva’) — the southernmost significant site, far from their father’s home circuit (Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah).
8.3: Velo halekhu vanav bidrachav vayyitu achar ha-batza’ vayiqechu shochad vayyattu mishpat (“his sons did not follow his example — they turned to greed, accepted bribes, and twisted justice”). The three-part corruption catalog: batza’ (unjust gain/greed), shochad (bribe), and nattah mishpat (perverting justice). Batza’ is from baza’ (to cut off, plunder) — exploiting the judicial position for financial benefit. Shochad is specifically bribery in judicial contexts (condemned in Exod 23:8; Deut 16:19; 27:25; Isa 1:23; Ezek 22:12). Nattah mishpat (turn/bend justice) is the standard idiom for perverting legal outcomes (Exod 23:6; Deut 16:19).
8.4: Vayit’qabetzu kol ziqnei Yisra’el vayavo’u el Shemu’el ha-Ramathah (“all the elders of Israel assembled and came to Samuel at Ramah”). The gathering of ziqnei Yisra’el (elders of Israel) is the constitutional procedure for national decision-making. The elders represent the tribal-clan leadership structure that predates the monarchy. Their coming to Samuel at Ramah echoes the earlier scene of Hannah coming to the sanctuary for divine intervention — a structural parallel that highlights how far Israel has drifted from Hannah’s posture of dependence.
8.6: Vayera’ ha-davar be-einei Shemu’el ka-asher ameru tenu-lanu melekh leshoftenu (“the request displeased Samuel when they said, ‘Give us a king to judge us’”). The phrase vayera’ be-einav (it was evil/displeasing in his eyes) uses the standard Deuteronomistic evaluative formula. Ka-asher ameru tenu-lanu melekh leshoftenu — the infinitive leshoftenu (to judge us) qualifies the request: they want the king specifically in the judicial-governance role that Samuel has occupied. Vayitpallel Shemu’el el YHWH (“Samuel prayed to the LORD”): prayer is Samuel’s first response, consistent with his character throughout.
8.8: YHWH’s explanation of the request: kekhal-ha-ma’asim asher asu meha-yom asher he’elitim mi-Mitzrayim ve-ad ha-yom hazeh vaya’azvuni vaya’avdu elohim acherim (“this is consistent with everything they have done since the day I brought them up out of Egypt... forsaking me and serving other gods”). The phrase ken hemmah osim gam-lakh (“now they are doing the same to you”) places Samuel’s personal rejection in the context of Israel’s long history of rejecting YHWH. This contextualizes the request as apostasy-pattern rather than merely pragmatic politics.
8.10: Vayidabber Shemu’el et kol divrei YHWH el ha-am hasha’alim me-ito melekh (“Samuel reported all the LORD’s words to the people who had asked him for a king”). Hasha’alim (those who asked/requested) uses the same sha’al root as the name Samuel and the name Saul. The verb echoes throughout the chapter: the people sha’al for a king; YHWH says give them what they sha’al for; the king who results will be named Sha’ul. The entire king-request is lexically bound to the asking/requesting vocabulary.
8.11: The mishpat ha-melekh (“the law/practice of the king”) begins here. Mishpat can mean both “legal right” (what the king has the legal right to do) or “customary practice” (what kings normally do). The ambiguity is intentional: this is both what the king will do and what he has the right to do under royal custom. The ANE parallels are striking: Mesopotamian royal inscriptions detail the king’s rights of corvée labor, military conscription, and taxation of produce. Samuel’s speech echoes these real royal prerogatives. Yiqach et-beneikhem (“he will take your sons”): the fourfold yiqach (he will take) in vv. 11-17 creates a rhetorical hammer of royal appropriation.
8.12: The military and agricultural drafts: commanders (sarim) over thousands and fifties, sons for plowing (lachrosh charish), harvesting (liqtzor qatziro), and weapons-making (la’asot kelei milchamto ukeli rikkvo, “his war equipment and chariot gear”). The military hierarchy (commanders of thousands and fifties, alfav va-chamishav) is the standard Israelite military unit organization (Exod 18:21; Deut 1:15). The royal economy requires both fighting capacity and agricultural production to sustain it.
8.13: Daughters taken to serve as raqqachot ve-tabbachot ve-ophot (perfumers, cooks, bakers). The three roles are women’s palace crafts — raqqach (perfumer) appears in Exod 30:25, 35 for the sacred anointing oil and incense makers. The palace economy requires female specialists in aromatic preparation, food preparation, and baking. The inclusion of daughters alongside sons in the royal drafts equalizes the exploitation across gender.
8.14: Ve-et sadoteikhem ve-kharmakhem ve-zeitei teivkhem ha-tovim yiqach (“he will seize your best fields, vineyards, and olive groves”). The three agricultural assets — grain fields, vineyards, olive groves — are the complete Mediterranean agricultural economy (also the three commodities of Deut 8:8’s seven-species abundance). Ha-tovim (the best/finest) — the king takes not a portion but the best portion. The distribution to his officials (ve-natan la-avedav) turns the royal appropriation into a patronage system.
8.15: Ve-zar’eikhem ve-kharmakhem ya’aser (“he will tithe your grain and your vineyards”). The royal ma’aser (tithe) is set at one-tenth — the same rate as the Levitical tithe (Num 18:21-24). This is deliberate: the royal tithe competes with YHWH’s tithe for the same agricultural produce. The monarch becomes a tithe-collector for his own officers (sarisav ulevadav), parallel to YHWH’s tithe supporting the Levites. The rhetorical point: the king takes from Israel what belongs to YHWH and directs it to his own household.
8.16: Ve-et-avdeikhem ve-et-amhotekhem ve-et-bachurekhem ha-tovim (“he will take your male and female servants, your finest young men”). The addition of donkeys (ve-et-chamorekhem) to servants creates a rhetorical descent: human servants and draft animals are equivalently absorbed into the royal service economy. Ve-asah l’malachto (“put them to work for himself”) — the royal monopolization of labor is total: the agricultural, domestic, and animal resources of the people become the king’s productive capital.
8.17: Tzone’ikhem ya’aser ve-atem tihyu lo la-avadim (“he will take a tenth of your flocks, and you will become his subjects”). The climax: from taking portions of produce and labor, the king ends by taking a tenth of the flocks and reducing the people to avadim (servants/slaves). The word avadim (servants) is loaded: it is the term for Israel in Egyptian bondage (Exod 1:13-14: vaya’avidu Mitzrayim et benei Yisra’el be-pharekh, the Egyptians enslaved them with hard labor). The monarchy Samuel warns against will reproduce the slavery of Egypt.
8.18: Uzzeqaqtem bayyom hahu mipnei malkkhem (“in that day you will cry out because of the king you have chosen for yourselves”). Zeqaq (cry out) is the verb of distress used for Israel’s cry in Egypt (Exod 2:23; 14:10) and echoed throughout Samuel (7:8-9). The bitter irony: Israel wanted a king to stop crying out under Philistine oppression; the king will cause them to cry out instead. Ve-lo ya’aneh YHWH etkhem bayyom hahu (“but the LORD will not answer you”) — the standard divine-response formula negated. YHWH’s refusal to answer the cry they create for themselves is the consequence of their rejection of his kingship.
8.19: Vayema’anu ha-am lishmo’a be-qol Shemu’el vayomru lo ki im-melekh yihyeh alenu (“but the people refused to listen to Samuel and said, ‘No! We will have a king over us’”). Ma’en (refuse, niphal of ma’an) is the refusal verb of Pharaoh (Exod 7:14; 8:2; 9:2; 10:3-4): ma’en... leshalach (“refuses to let go”). Israel’s refusal to listen to Samuel mirrors Pharaoh’s refusal to listen to Moses. The people who were freed from Pharaoh’s ma’en now themselves ma’en to follow YHWH.
8.20: Vehayinu gam-anachnu ke-khol ha-goyim (“so that we may be like all the other nations”). This is the theological heart of the chapter: Israel’s desire to be ke-khol ha-goyim (like all the nations). The Sinai covenant established Israel as YHWH’s distinctive (segullah) people, a kingdom of priests, a holy nation (Exod 19:5-6) — not like the nations. The request to be ke-khol ha-goyim is the formal rejection of the covenant’s distinctiveness. The king they want: to “rule us, lead us out, and fight our battles” (ushefatanu u-vatza’ lefaneinu ve-nilcham et-milchamoteinu) — the military leadership function they attribute to the king is precisely what YHWH provided as divine warrior throughout the judges period.
8.21: Samuel heard all the words (kol divrei ha-am) and repeated them before YHWH (vayedabberem be-oznei YHWH). The verb dabar be-oznei (speak in the ears of) is used for official transmission of important communication (Exod 17:14; Gen 50:4). Samuel’s role here is as messenger relaying the people’s formal position to YHWH — the intercessory function in reverse: rather than carrying YHWH’s word to the people (cf. 8:10), he carries the people’s word to YHWH.
8.22: YHWH’s final word: shema bequolam (“listen to their voice”) and vehemlekhta lahem melekh (“give them a king”). Divine consent given to human desire does not constitute divine approval: this is the grammar of judgment through permission (cf. Rom 1:24, 26, 28: God “gave them over”). YHWH grants the monarchy knowing it will ultimately fail and lead to the captivity. Samuel’s final instruction — lekhu ish le-iro (“each of you, return to your town”) — disperses the assembly without ceremony. The momentous decision to establish a monarchy is registered in the departure formula of dismissed troops.
