13.8: Saul waits the seven days Samuel commanded (cf. 10:8) then acts alone: wayyitʾappaq šāʾûl wayyaʿal hāʿōlāh — 'Saul forced himself and offered the burnt offering.' The reflexive hitpael of ʾāpas — 'he forced himself' — is Saul's own explanation (v12). This is the Hebrew grammar of self-justification: Saul acts against his conscience, against the prophetic command, against the covenantal boundary, and the syntax marks this as a psychological act of self-overriding. The sacrifice itself is not sinful in kind — burnt offerings are the proper response to military crisis — but this one is offered by someone who has no authority to offer it: the king is not the priest. The boundary between royal and priestly office that Saul crosses is the same boundary that will later kill Uzziah when he enters the temple to burn incense (2 Chr 26:16-21).
13.12: Saul's self-defense: wāʾōmar ʿattāh yērᵉdû pəlištîm ʾēlay hagilgālāh wûpənê YHWH lōʾ ḥillôtî — ‘I said, “Now the Philistines will come down against me at Gilgal, and the face of YHWH I have not entreated,” so I forced myself and offered the burnt offering.' The phrase ḥillôtî ʾet pənê YHWH (to entreat the face of YHWH) is the technical formula for seeking the divine oracle before battle (cf. Judg 1:1). Saul's problem is not that he failed to seek YHWH's face but that he sought it through an unauthorized action. The syntax of the excuse — 'I forced myself' — is the grammar of all rationalized disobedience: the imperative is real (seek YHWH before battle), the reasoning is plausible (Samuel is late), the act is catastrophic.
13.13: Samuel's verdict employs the vocabulary of divine rejection: nibbāltā — 'you have acted foolishly' — from the root nābal, the same root as Nabal the fool of ch25. Saul's foolish act is not primarily tactical but theological: lōʾ šāmartā ʾet miṣwat YHWH ʾelōhêḵā ʾăšer ṣiwwāḵ — 'you have not kept the commandment of YHWH your God which he commanded you.' The vocabulary is Deuteronomic: šāmar miṣwāh (to keep a commandment) is the covenant-loyalty formula that runs through Deut 4-11. Samuel's words make explicit that Saul's failure is not military or strategic but covenantal — the category of failure that will define the entire Saul narrative through ch15.
13.14: The most theologically dense verse in the Saul narrative: biqēš YHWH lô ʾîš kilᵉḇābô — 'YHWH has sought for himself a man according to his own heart.' The phrase kilᵉḇābô (after his heart/according to his heart) is unique in the OT as a description of the king YHWH seeks. Lēḇāḇ (heart) in Hebrew anthropology is the seat of will, understanding, and intention — the inner life that YHWH sees when he looks past appearance (16:7). The verb biqēš (to seek, search) suggests active divine initiative: YHWH has been looking, has found his candidate. Paul cites this verse in his Antioch synagogue sermon (Acts 13:22) as the theological basis for David's appointment — and by implication for Christ's: the king YHWH sought is the one whose heart is wholly aligned with YHWH's purposes, the antitype of both Saul and David, ultimately fulfilled in Christ who 'always does the things that are pleasing' to the Father (John 8:29).
13.1: ben-šānāh šāʾûl bemalekô — 'Saul was ___ year old when he became king.' The MT is famously defective: ben-šānāh šāʾûl bemalekô literally reads 'a son of one year in his becoming king' — the numeral for Saul's age is missing from the Hebrew text. The LXX omits the verse entirely; Acts 13:21 states he reigned 40 years. Ancient scribal transmission simply lost the number. The phrase ûštayim šānîm mālaḵ ʿal-yisrāʾēl ('two years he reigned over Israel') has also been debated — whether this means Saul reigned only two years before his dynasty was rejected (cf. v14 — 'your kingdom will not continue') or whether two years elapsed before the events of ch13.
13.2: vayyiḇḥar-lô šāʾûl šĕlōšet ʾălāfîm miyyisrāʾēl — 'Saul chose 3,000 men from Israel.' The standing army: ʾalpayim ʿim-šāʾûl bemikĕmāš ûbĕhar bêt-ʾēl (2,000 with Saul at Michmash and the hill country of Bethel) and weʾelep ʿim-yôhānāṯān baggiḇʿāh ḇinyāmîn (1,000 with Jonathan at Gibeah of Benjamin). The geographic split — Saul at Michmash in the central highlands, Jonathan at Gibeah — established a two-pronged defensive position. Mikĕmāš (H4363 — the location of the narrow pass through which the Philistines would later advance) was the strategic choke point controlling movement between the coastal plain and the hill country. The rest of Israel was sent home (weʾet-yeter hāʿām šillaḥ ʾîš leʾohālāyw).
13.3: vayyakh yôhānāṯān ʾet-nĕtsîḇ pĕlishtîm ʾăšer begeḇaʿ — 'Jonathan struck the Philistine garrison at Geba.' The verb nākhāh (H5221 — to strike, smite) signals a military engagement that Jonathan initiated without being explicitly commanded. The nĕtsîḇ (H5333 — garrison, prefect, administrative post) at Geba was a Philistine control point in Israelite territory — a symbol of colonial subjugation. Saul's response: yiqqāʿ haššôpār bekol-hāʾārets (the trumpet was blown throughout the land) and the declaration yišmĕʿû hāʿiḇrîm (let the Hebrews hear). The term ʿiḇrîm (Hebrews — H5680) may have been the Philistine designation for Israel, possibly connected to the Hapiru/Apiru of Amarna correspondence. Saul's trumpet call mobilized Israel while simultaneously triggering the Philistine counter-response.
13.4: wĕkhol-yisrāʾēl šāmĕʿû lēʾmōr hikkāh šāʾûl ʾet-nĕtsîḇ pĕlishtîm — 'All Israel heard: Saul has struck the Philistine garrison.' The announcement attributed the victory to Saul (hikkāh šāʾûl) rather than Jonathan — a detail the narrative preserves without explicit comment, though the attentive reader notes Jonathan's actual deed (v3). The consequence: wegam niḇʾaš yisrāʾēl bapĕlishtîm (Israel had become odious to the Philistines — H887 bāʾaš, to stink, be loathsome). The vocabulary of military-political odium (bāʾaš) appears again in 2 Sam 10:6 when Israel becomes odious to the Ammonites. Jonathan's raid triggered a crisis that required Israel to respond as a national force rather than as isolated villages under Philistine control.
13.5: ûpĕlishtîm neʾespû lehillāḥem beyisrāʾēl — 'The Philistines assembled to fight Israel.' The Philistine force: šlōšîm ʾelep rekeb ûšēšet ʾălāfîm pārāšîm (30,000 chariots and 6,000 horsemen — numbers that may reflect hyperbole or textual transmission issues; some MSS read 3,000 chariots). The weʿam keḥôl ʾăšer ʿal-šĕpat hayyām lārōḇ (people as numerous as the sand on the seashore) is the standard expression for uncountable military force (cf. Josh 11:4; Judg 7:12). Their camp at mikĕmāš (Michmash) placed them directly across from Saul at Gibeah — a classic battle setup in the narrow pass terrain that would favor the Philistines' superior iron weapons.
13.6: weʾîš yisrāʾēl rāʾû kî ṣar-lô kî naggaś hāʿām — 'The men of Israel saw they were in distress, for the people were hard pressed.' Ṣar (H6887 — tight, distressed, pressed) describes the military crisis vividly: Israel was squeezed between superior Philistine forces. The response: vayyitḥabbĕʾû hāʿām bemĕʿārôt ûḇaśśîḥîm ûḇaṣṣĕlāʿîm ûḇaṣṣĕrîḥîm ûḇabbōrôt (the people hid themselves in caves, thickets, clefts, holes, and cisterns). The five-fold list of hiding places catalogues the landscape of the central Benjamite highlands — limestone caves, rocky outcrops, cistern shafts. Maḥpēlāh-style hiding in caves echoes the Gibeah situation in Judg 20-21, the same terrain. The list accumulates to create the picture of a people in total rout.
13.7: weʿiḇrîm ʿāḇrû ʾet-hayyardēn ʾerets gāḏ wegilaʿd — 'Some Hebrews crossed the Jordan to the land of Gad and Gilead.' The flight across the Jordan to the trans-Jordanian safety of gāḏ wegilaʿd (Gad and Gilead) indicates how complete the collapse of morale was: not just hiding but abandoning the homeland entirely. Weʾepes hāʿām ʾăšer baggâl lăhăl nāḥarĕdû ʾaḥărāyw (those who remained with Saul in Gilgal trembled after him) — the verb ḥārēd (H2729 — to tremble, be terrified) is the word for the terror of divine judgment (Isa 66:2) here applied to Israel's response to Saul's inadequate leadership. The gap between military crisis and spiritual faithfulness would be exposed in the following episode.
13.9: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl haggîšû ʾēlay hāʿōlāh wehaššĕlāmîm — 'Saul said: Bring me the burnt offering and the peace offerings.' The usurpation: Saul commanded the liturgical offerings (hāʿōlāh and haššĕlāmîm) that belonged exclusively to the priest Samuel (1 Sam 10:8: 'you shall go down before me to Gilgal... I will come down to you to offer burnt offerings'). Vayyaʿal hāʿōlāh (and he offered the burnt offering) — the verb ʿālāh in the causative/Hiphil would mean 'he caused to be offered'; some read it as Saul literally offering the sacrifice himself. The specific identification of the sacrifice — ʿōlāh (burnt offering ascending to YHWH) and šĕlāmîm (peace/fellowship offering) — indicates Saul attempted a full liturgical service, not a casual act.
13.10: vayhî kkallōtô lehaqrîḇ hāʿōlāh wĕhinnēh šĕmûʾēl bāʾ — 'Just as he finished offering the burnt offering, behold, Samuel came.' The temporal precision — kkallōtô (as he was finishing — H3617 from H3615 kālāh, to complete, finish) — is the narrator's irony: Samuel arrived at the moment of transgression's completion. The word hinnēh (behold — the particle of sudden, unexpected appearance) signals narrative surprise. Samuel's arrival was not accidental but divinely timed: YHWH allowed the transgression to occur and then sent the prophet to announce the consequences. The timing preserved both the historical record of Saul's failure and the theological precision of divine response.
13.11: vayyōʾmer šĕmûʾēl māh-ʿāśîtā — 'Samuel said: What have you done?' The prophetic confrontation formula (māh-ʿāśîtā — what have you done?) appears at key covenant-breach moments: Gen 3:13 (to Eve), Gen 4:10 (to Cain), Gen 12:18 (to Abraham in Egypt), 1 Sam 13:11 (to Saul), 2 Sam 12:21 (to David). It is not a genuine question seeking information but a covenant-prosecution formula demanding accountability. Saul's response (v11-12) is the politician's classic self-justification: kî rāʾîtî (because I saw), weʾattāh lōʾ-bāʾtā (and you did not come), wayyeʾāsĕpû pĕlishtîm (the Philistines assembled), waʾăḥallāh nĕpî YHWH (I thought I must propitiate YHWH). Four separate excuses in one sentence.
13.15: vayyāqom šĕmûʾēl vayyaʿal miggilgāl giḇĕʿat binyāmîn — 'Samuel arose and went up from Gilgal to Gibeah of Benjamin.' The departure (vayyāqom... vayyaʿal) of Samuel is abrupt — no farewell, no reconciliation. Samuel went to Gibeah while Saul mustered (vayyispōr šāʾûl ʾet-hāʿām hannimtsāʾîm ʿimmô) the people who remained. The count was keštayyim ʾălāfîm ʿimmô (about 600 men) — from the original 3,000 (v2), two-thirds had deserted. The narrative tracks the collapse of Saul's army numerically: 3,000 → 600 = an 80% desertion rate. This decimation was the practical consequence of Samuel's departure and YHWH's removal of divine support from Saul's campaign.
13.16: wĕšāʾûl wĕyôhānāṯān bĕnô wĕhāʿām hannimtsāʾ ʿimmāhem — 'Saul and Jonathan his son and the people who remained with them were staying in Geba of Benjamin.' The garrison at geḇaʿ binyāmîn (Geba of Benjamin — H1390, H1144) faced the Philistine camp opposite at Michmash. The specific topographic detail — the two opposing positions separated by the narrow Michmash Pass — sets the scene for Jonathan's bold raid in ch14. The word yōšĕḇîm (they were sitting/staying — H3427 from yāšaḇ) suggests a defensive encampment rather than active engagement: Saul was holding position, not attacking. The initiative had passed from Israel to the Philistines.
13.17: vayyētsēʾ hamaššḥît mimmaḥănēh pĕlishtîm šelōšāh rāʾšîm — 'The raiding parties came out of the Philistine camp in three columns.' Hamaššḥît (H7843 the one who destroys/devastates — from the same root as šāḥat, to corrupt/destroy) describes a professional raiding force. The three columns fanned out in three directions: rōʾš ʾeḥad pānāh ʾel-derek ʿophrāh ʾel-ʾerets šûʿāl (one column toward Ophrah and the land of Shual — northeast), weʾeḥad pānāh derek bêt-ḥôrôn (one toward Beth-horon — northwest, controlling the ascent to Jerusalem), weʾeḥad pānāh derek haggĕḇûl hanhōlēʾ derek haggāy ṣĕḇōʿîm midĕḇārāh (one toward the border overlooking the valley of Zeboim toward the wilderness — southeast). The three-column strategy was designed to prevent any Israelite concentration for counter-attack.
13.18: weʾeḥad pānāh derek bêt-ḥôrôn — 'And one column went toward Beth-horon.' The geographical precision of the three raiding directions reveals the strategic purpose: total encirclement of the Israelite defensive position at Geba/Gibeah. Bêt-ḥôrôn (H1032 — the upper and lower Beth-horon controlled the main ascent from the coastal plain to the hill country via the Aijalon Valley — Josh 10:10-11; 1 Kgs 9:17), ʾophrāh weʾerets šûʿāl (Ophrah and the land of Shual — north of Michmash toward the Negev of the Benjamin hills), and the Zeboim valley wilderness — together these covered all approaches to the Israelite position. The Philistines were systematically disrupting Israelite agriculture and supply lines.
13.19: weḥārāš lōʾ yimmātsēʾ bĕkhol ʾerets yisrāʾēl — 'No craftsman could be found in all the land of Israel.' The economic-military control of iron: the Philistines (pĕlishtîm) had deliberately removed all metalworkers (ḥārāš — H2796, craftsman, smith, engraver) from Israel to prevent weapon production. Kî ʾāmĕrû pĕlishtîm pen-yaʿăśû hāʿiḇrîm ḥereb ʾô ḥănît (for the Philistines said, 'Lest the Hebrews make a sword or spear'). This is archaeological context confirmed by excavations: iron technology was introduced to the Levant by the Sea Peoples (Philistines) around 1200 BCE and initially monopolized. Iron smelting required significant capital and technical knowledge — the Philistines had it; the hill-country Israelites did not. The verse is a brilliant military-economic explanation for Israel's vulnerability.
13.20: vayyērĕdû kol-yisrāʾēl happĕlishtîm lelatēš ʾîš ʾet-malsĕḇô — 'All Israel went down to the Philistines to sharpen every man his plowshare.' Israel's agricultural tools — malsēb (H4281 — plowshare), ʾēt (H855 — mattock, hoe), garzen (H1631 — axe), maḥărēšāh (H4281 — plowshare variant) — were all iron implements requiring Philistine smiths to sharpen. The twice-annual sharpening (lilatēš — H3913, to sharpen, whet) for pre-harvest plowing made Israel economically dependent on the Philistine metalworking industry. The payment (ûpatsîr hayyāh pim lapĕlishtîm — a pim was about 7.8 grams of silver, two-thirds of a shekel) was a form of regular tribute extraction. The pim (H6344) appears as a weight-stone inscription in Iron Age Hebrew archaeology.
13.21: wĕhāyĕṯāh happeṣîrāh pîm lapĕlishtîm — 'The charge was a pim for the Philistines.' The economic precision is remarkable: the Chronicler preserved the actual price (pim — a weight unit = 2/3 shekel, confirmed by archaeological finds of inscribed stone weights at multiple Israelite sites). For sharpening hammaḥărēšōt weʾet-haqqardummōt (the plowshares and the axes) and ûlĕhatstsîḇ haddarbon (to set/fix the ox-goads — H1861 dorban). The ox-goad (darban/dorban) was an iron-tipped wooden staff for directing draft animals — even this agricultural tool required the Philistine iron-workers. The economic dependency extended to every agricultural implement in Israelite farming life.
13.22: wĕhāyāh bĕyôm milḥemet lōʾ-nimtsāʾ ḥereb wĕḥănît bĕyad kol-hāʿām — 'On the day of battle, neither sword nor spear was found in the hand of any of the people.' The military consequence of the economic dependency: on the day of the Michmash battle (beyôm milḥemet), no Israelite soldier had an iron weapon — neither ḥereb (H2719 — sword, the primary close-combat weapon) nor ḥănît (H2595 — spear, the primary distance weapon). The only exceptions: raq lĕšāʾûl ûlîhônāṯān bĕnô nimtsāʾ (only Saul and Jonathan his son had them). This detail explains why Jonathan's raid required extraordinary courage and divine assistance: he fought with iron against iron-equipped Philistines while the rest of Israel fought with agricultural implements.
13.23: vayyētsēʾ matsaḇ pĕlishtîm ʾel-maʿăḇar mikĕmāš — 'The Philistine garrison went out to the pass of Michmash.' Maʿăḇar mikĕmāš (the pass of Michmash — H4569, H4363) was the narrow ravine (Wadi Suweinit) between Michmash (north) and Geba (south). The pass was strategically critical — control of this narrow defile controlled movement between the Philistine coastal cities and the Israelite hill country. The Philistine garrison at the pass was simultaneously an offensive position (controlling Israelite movement) and a target (its isolation made it vulnerable to a surprise flanking attack — exactly what Jonathan would exploit in ch14). The verse sets the scene for the next chapter's raid.

14.6: Jonathan's battle-speech to his armor-bearer is one of the OT's most compressed statements of active faith: ûlᵉyôhônātān kî ʾên laYHWH māḵôr lᵉhôšîaʿ bᵉraḇ ʾô ḇimʿāṭ — 'for nothing prevents YHWH from saving whether by many or by few.' The verb māḵôr (hindrance, restraint) is rare; the theological claim is radical: divine saving action is not constrained by military arithmetic. This anticipates Gideon's 300 (Judg 7), David's single stone against Goliath, and Paul's 'when I am weak, then I am strong' (2 Cor 12:10). Jonathan acts from the epistemological inversion that characterizes biblical faith: the normal ratio of forces is irrelevant when YHWH acts. His father Saul, by contrast, is paralyzed by numbers — waiting under a pomegranate tree while his kingdom evaporates (v2).
14.12: The armor-bearer's response — ʿăśēh kol ʾăšer bilᵉḇāḇeḵā nəṭēh lāḵ hinnᵉnî ʿimmᵉḵā kilᵉḇāḇeḵā — 'Do all that is in your heart; here I am with you according to your heart' — uses the lēḇāḇ vocabulary of 13:14: the man after the heart, the heart-aligned companion. The phrase hinnᵉnî ʿimmᵉḵā (here I am with you) echoes the divine commissioning formula (ʾehyeh ʿimmāḵ, 'I will be with you') given to Moses, Gideon, and the judges. Jonathan and his armor-bearer form a two-man unit operating in the lēḇāḇ-mode that defines the coming Davidic king — not Saul's paralyzed majority but Jonathan's faithful two.
14.15: The divine panic at Michmash is described as ḥardat ʾelōhîm — 'the trembling of God' — which even the ground participates in: wattirʿaš hāʾāreṣ, 'the earth shook.' The ḥardat ʾelōhîm is the supernatural terror that YHWH sends before his people to disable the enemy (cf. Exod 23:27 — 'I will send my terror before you'; Josh 2:9 — Rahab's report that the terror of Israel has fallen on the Canaanites). It is theophanic panic — the fear that precedes YHWH's presence in battle. The Michmash earthquake is also the beginning of a typological pattern: the earth shakes when YHWH acts decisively for his people (cf. Matt 27:51; 28:2 — the earthquake at the crucifixion and resurrection).
14.24: Saul's oath: ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yōʾkal leḥem ʿad hāʿereb wənikqamtî mēʾōyᵉḇay — 'Cursed is the man who eats bread until evening when I have avenged myself on my enemies.' The word ʾārûr (cursed) is covenant-curse vocabulary, drawing from the Deuteronomic blessings and curses (Deut 27-28). Saul invokes the covenant-curse mechanism to bind his own army, making food-restraint into a military-religious obligation. The result is the people's exhaustion (v31) and the near-death of Jonathan. The rash oath (ʾālāh) that nearly kills his own son is a pattern that Jephthah's daughter represents in the worst possible form (Judg 11) — covenant-curse language deployed without divine authorization, generating tragic consequence. Saul's oath is a smaller-scale Jephthah, with the people ultimately rescuing Jonathan where no one rescued Jephthah's daughter.
14.45: The people's covenant-rescue of Jonathan: ḥālîlāh kî ʾim yāmût yôhônātān ʾăšer ʿāśāh hayyəšûʿāh haggəḏôlāh hazzōʾt bəyiśrāʾēl — 'Far be it! Jonathan shall not die, for he has worked this great salvation (yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh) in Israel.' The phrase yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh (great salvation) is the same formula used by Samson after his jawbone victory (Judg 15:18) and the language from which yēšûaʿ (Jesus, 'salvation') derives its meaning. The people redeem Jonathan from Saul's death sentence — a son rescued from death by the intervention of the covenant community. The irony is double: the son who worked the salvation that neither Saul nor the army could accomplish is nearly killed by his own father's oath, and is only saved by the same people he delivered.
14.1: vayhî hayyôm vayyōʾmer yônāṯān ben-šāʾûl ʾel-hannāʿar nōśēʾ kelāyw — 'One day Jonathan son of Saul said to his armor-bearer.' The casual vayhî hayyôm (it happened one day — the idiom for a significant day in Israelite narrative, cf. 2 Kgs 4:8,11,18) introduces an act of spontaneous faithfulness. Jonathan's plan was told only to his armor-bearer (hannāʿar nōśēʾ kelāyw — the young man carrying his weapons), not to his father Saul. The word nāʿar (H5288 — young man, servant, armor-bearer) designates the intimate military companion. The secrecy from Saul was deliberate — Jonathan knew his father would not sanction the raid, or perhaps that Saul's fearfulness would prevent any initiative.
14.2: wĕšāʾûl yôšēb biḵrēh haggibĕʿāh taḥat harimmôn ʾăšer bemigĕrôn — 'Saul was sitting under the pomegranate tree at Migron on the outskirts of Gibeah.' The contrast is deliberate: while Jonathan was about to act, Saul was yôšēb (sitting — H3427, the posture of inaction). Harimmôn ʾăšer bemigĕrôn (the pomegranate at Migron — possibly near Gibeah, cf. Isa 10:28) was his post. His entourage: keššēš meʾôt ʾîš (about 600 men) and the priests with the ephod (wahăhiyyāh ʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕpānāyw — Ahijah son of Ahitub was wearing the ephod). The ephod present but unused — Saul had the oracle apparatus but did not consult it, while Jonathan would act on raw faith.
14.3: waʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕpānāyw — 'Ahijah son of Ahitub... was wearing the ephod.' The priestly genealogy — ʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕnê-pînĕḥās ben-ʿēlî kōhēn YHWH bĕšîlôh (son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod, son of Phinehas son of Eli, the priest of YHWH at Shiloh) — connects the current priesthood directly to the disgraced Elide line. The ʾîkāḇôḏ (H350 — Ichabod, 'where is the glory?') reference recalls the moment in 1 Sam 4:21-22 when the glory departed from Israel with the ark's capture. The priesthood present with Saul was from the lineage of Eli's failed sons — the contrast with Samuel's prophetic authority was stark. Weʾāmrû yisrāʾēl (the people did not know Jonathan had gone).
14.4: ûḇên hammaʿbĕrôt ʾăšer biqqēš yônāṯān laʿăḇōr ʿal matsaḇ pĕlishtîm — 'Between the passes through which Jonathan sought to cross to the Philistine garrison.' The geographic precision identifies the specific terrain feature Jonathan would use: šēn hassela miḥṣāḏ ûšēn hassela miḥṣāḇ (a sharp rocky crag on one side, a sharp rocky crag on the other side). The word šēn (H8127 — tooth, sharp point) vividly describes the two precipitous cliffs flanking the Michmash ravine. Their names: bôtsēts (H949 — shining/gleaming crag, the one on the north) and seneh (H5573 — thornbush crag, the one on the south — possibly named after its vegetation). The topography of Wadi Suweinit, identified by Albright and confirmed by survey, matches this description precisely.
14.5: hassēn miṣṣāpôn lenokah mikĕmāš wehaʾaḥēr mittēmān lenokah geḇaʿ — 'The one crag on the north opposite Michmash, the other on the south opposite Geba.' The verse is a pure topographic orientation: Seneh crag was on the south (near Geba, where Jonathan's group was), and Bozez crag was on the north (near Michmash, where the Philistine garrison stood). For Jonathan to reach the Philistine position, he had to cross the ravine (ascending the Seneh crag from the south) in full view of the enemy. The tactical absurdity of the plan — climbing a cliff face toward an armed garrison — was precisely the kind of action that could only be undertaken in faith that YHWH would act.
14.7: vayyōʾmer lô nōśēʾ kelāyw ʿăśēh kol-ʾăšer belĕḇāḇĕkhā nĕṭēh-lākhā — 'His armor-bearer said to him: Do all that is in your heart; turn to where you will.' The armor-bearer's response is one of the Bible's finest examples of loyal courage: hinnĕhāh ʾimĕkhā kĕliḇĕḇĕkhā (here I am with you, as is your heart). The phrase kĕliḇĕḇĕkhā (as your heart — complete identification with the commander's purpose) anticipates David's mighty men who shared his heart's vision. The armor-bearer did not ask for a battle plan, tactical analysis, or divine confirmation: he committed himself fully to Jonathan's leadership. This unnamed young man modeled the faith that Jonathan himself embodied — willingness to act when outcomes are uncertain but the commander is trustworthy.
14.8: vayyōʾmer yônāṯān hinnēh ʾănaḥnû ʿōḇrîm ʾel-hāʾănāšîm — 'Jonathan said: We are crossing over to those men.' The plan with its sign: ûnigĕlînû ʾălêhem (we will reveal ourselves to them). The two possible outcomes: (1) if the Philistines say dommû ʿad higgiʿănû ʾălêkem (stay where you are until we come to you) → that is YHWH's sign that defeat will come on them; (2) if they say ʿălû ʿālênû (come up to us) → that is YHWH's sign that He has given them into Israel's hand. The sign was clever: either outcome was usable as divine guidance. The willingness of the Philistines to invite Jonathan up (v12) fulfilled the positive sign precisely — their taunting confidence became their death warrant.
14.9: ʾim-kōh yōmĕrû ʾēlênû dommû ʿaḏ higĕʿānû ʾălêkem — 'If they say: Wait until we come to you.' The negative sign: dommû (H1826 from H1826 — be still, wait) was the dismissive command of a superior to an inferior who must wait permission to approach. This response would indicate that YHWH had not given the Philistines into Israel's hand — Jonathan's plan would not proceed. The grammar of the oracle-by-sign reflects the binary question format of Urim and Thummim divination (cf. 1 Sam 23:11-12 where David asks YHWH two yes/no questions). Jonathan's improvised oracle used the enemy's own words as divine speech — a creative appropriation of the theophanic oracle tradition without access to the formal instruments (the ephod was with Saul, unused, v3).
14.10: weʾim-kōh yōmĕrû ʿălû ʿālênû weʿālînû — 'If they say: Come up to us — then we will go up.' The positive sign: the Philistines' invitation to come up (ʿălû ʿālênû) would be the sign that YHWH had given them into Israel's hand (kî-nĕtānām YHWH bĕyādēnû). The divine irony: the Philistines' contemptuous taunt (they saw only two Israelites — v12 says 'the Hebrews are coming out of their holes') became the oracle that sealed their own doom. Jonathan interpreted their words as YHWH's speech — a reading that requires deep faith in divine sovereignty over the enemy's mouth. The fulfillment: the Philistines said exactly the expected words, and Jonathan's faith was vindicated.
14.11: vayyiggālû šĕnêhem ʾel-matsaḇ pĕlishtîm — 'Both of them revealed themselves to the Philistine garrison.' The verb gālāh (H1540 — to reveal, expose, uncover) in the reflexive/Niphal means they deliberately showed themselves — stepped out from concealment into the enemy's view. The Philistine response confirmed the sign: hinnēh ʿiḇrîm yōtsĕʾîm min-haḥăḥārîm ʾăšer hiṯḥabbĕʾû šām (look, Hebrews coming out of the holes where they were hiding themselves). The contemptuous reference to the holes (ḥăḥārîm — cf. 13:6 where Israel hid in holes) shows the Philistines' complete disdain for the two Israelites. Their contempt made them careless — and carefulness was the only thing that might have stopped Jonathan.
14.13: vayyaʿal yônāṯān ʿal-yādāyw weʿal-ragĕlāyw — 'Jonathan climbed up on his hands and feet.' The physical description — hands and feet (yadāyw weragĕlāyw) — portrays Jonathan literally crawling up the cliff face. This is combat reporting of exceptional realism: the great warrior in undignified, vulnerable posture, ascending a rock face under enemy observation. Wenōśēʾ kelāyw ʾaḥărāyw (his armor-bearer after him) followed the same route. Then the climactic sequence: vayyippĕlû lifnê yônāṯān wenōśēʾ kelāyw mĕmôtēt ʾaḥărāyw — the Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer killed behind him. The two-man assault became a one-sided rout: Jonathan struck down the enemy, his companion finished those who fell.
14.14: vayhî hammakkāh hāriʾšônāh ʾăšer hikkāh yônāṯān weʾet-nōśēʾ kelāyw — 'That first strike by Jonathan and his armor-bearer was about twenty men.' The precision: kĕʿeśrîm ʾîš kĕḥătsiʾ maʿănāh ʾăšer-yeḥrōš-śādeh (about twenty men within half a furrow of land a team plows in a day). The agricultural measurement — half a furrow (ḥătsiʾ maʿănāh) of land plowed in a day — is an unusual military measurement unit, suggesting this was a relatively compact area of a terraced hillside. Twenty trained warriors killed in a confined space by two men: the casualty rate was extraordinary and could only be explained by divine intervention. The scale of the immediate kill prepared for the larger supernatural intervention that followed.
14.16: vayyirʾû haṣṣopîm lĕšāʾûl begibĕʿat binyāmîn — 'The watchmen of Saul at Gibeah of Benjamin saw.' Haṣṣopîm (H6822 — the lookouts, watchmen) at Saul's position observed the Philistine camp dissolving: wehinnēh hehāmôn nāmôg vayyēlek wahālōm (the multitude was melting and going here and there). The word hāmôn (H1995 — multitude, tumult, roar) for the Philistine camp captures both the numbers and the noise. The verb nāmôg (H4127 — to melt, dissolve — used for the melting of wax, Ps 68:2; the dissolving of the Canaanites' courage, Josh 2:24) describes the Philistine rout as a supernatural liquefying of their military resolve.
14.17: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl lapĕqûḏû nāʾ ûrʾû mî hālaḵ mē ʿimmānû — 'Saul said: Count now and see who has gone from us.' The forensic procedure: pāqaḏ (H6485 — to muster, count, inspect) was the standard military census verb (Num 1:3; 1 Sam 11:8). Only when the rout was already visible did Saul investigate — his leadership pattern was reactive rather than proactive. The discovery: wayyipqĕḏû wehinnēh ʾên yônāṯān weʾet-nōśēʾ kelāyw (when they counted, Jonathan and his armor-bearer were not there). Jonathan's unauthorized raid had already won the battle that Saul was still trying to understand.
14.18: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl laʾăḥiyyāh haggîšāh ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm — 'Saul said to Ahijah: Bring the ark of God.' The MT reads 'bring the ark of God' but the LXX reads 'bring the ephod' (consistent with v3 — Ahijah wore the ephod, not the ark). The ephod reading is likely original: the ark was at Kiriath-jearim (1 Sam 7:1), not with Saul's army. The oracle consultation: Saul finally sought YHWH's guidance after the battle had already begun to turn. The phrase kî hāyāh ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm bayyôm hahûʾ ûḇnê yisrāʾēl (for the ark of God was there that day with the children of Israel) suggests the consultation was triggered by the observed rout. But even this delayed consultation would be interrupted (v19: 'withdraw your hand').
14.19: vayhî ʿad-dabbēr šāʾûl ʾel-hakkōhēn wehahhāmôn ʾăšer-bemahănēh pĕlishtîm — 'While Saul was speaking to the priest, the tumult in the Philistine camp increased.' Wayhî ʾad dabbēr (while speaking/as speaking — the temporal conjunction marking interruption) sets up the contrast: Saul consulting the priest, but the battle going on without him. Wayyāsep wĕhālaḵ (increased and spread). Saul's command: ʾesōp yādêkhā (withdraw your hand — stop the consultation) suggests that Saul decided to skip the oracle and act immediately. The irony: Saul abandoned the divine oracle at exactly the moment he should have used it — his impulsive decision-making versus the patient inquiry that Samuel would have required.
14.20: vayyizzāʿēq šāʾûl wekhol-hāʿām ʾăšer ʿimmô vayyābōʾû ʿad-hammilḥāmāh — 'Saul and all the people with him rallied and came to the battle.' The Hebrew zaʿaq (H2199 — to cry out, muster with a shout) indicates the battle-cry that mobilized Saul's 600-man force. Wehinnēh hāyĕṯāh ḥereḇ ʾîš berēʿēhû mĕhûmāh gĕḏôlāh meʾōd (every man's sword was against his fellow in very great confusion). The mĕhûmāh (H4103 — panic, confusion, tumult — specifically the supernatural terror YHWH sends into armies, Deut 7:23; 2 Chr 15:5; Zech 14:13) was the divine weapon: YHWH caused the Philistines to attack each other. This was the same divine confusion that struck the Midianites for Gideon (Judg 7:22) — YHWH's standard military intervention method when Israel was outnumbered.
14.21: wĕhāʿiḇrîm hāyû lappĕlishtîm kĕʾetmôl šilšôm — 'The Hebrews who had been with the Philistines previously.' The sociological detail is striking: some Israelites (ʿiḇrîm — Hebrews) had been serving with the Philistine army (hāyû lappĕlishtîm — they were among/for the Philistines) in the period before the battle. This reflects the reality of Israel's subjugation: some had gone over to the Philistine side, perhaps as mercenaries, laborers, or under compulsion. When they saw the divine rout (vayyāsēbbû gam-hēmmāh lihyôt ʿim-yisrāʾēl — they too turned to be with Israel), they switched sides. The battle had a conversion effect: Israelite collaborators with the enemy became fighters for their own people.
14.22: wĕkhol ʾîš yisrāʾēl hammiṯḥabbĕʾîm bĕhar ʾeprayim — 'All the men of Israel who had hidden in the hill country of Ephraim.' The reference back to the hiding episode of 13:6 creates a narrative arc: the Israelites who had hidden in caves and thickets now heard that the Philistines had fled (vayyišmĕʿû kî-nāsû pĕlishtîm vayyirdĕpû gam-hēmmāh ʾaḥărêhem — they heard the Philistines fled and they also pursued). The transformation from hiding to pursuing is the measure of the divine intervention: the same people who had been paralyzed by fear were now aggressive fighters. YHWH's battle had converted a demoralized refuge-seeking people into a victorious pursuing army.
14.23: vayyôšaʿ YHWH bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet-yisrāʾēl — 'YHWH saved Israel that day.' The theological summary with the divine subject: vayyôšaʿ YHWH (YHWH saved/delivered — H3467 from yāšaʿ, the root of yĕšûʿāh, salvation). The battle was attributed explicitly to divine action, not to Jonathan's military prowess. Wehammaḥălôqâ ʿāḇrāh ʾet-bêt ʾāwen (the battle passed beyond Beth-aven). The geographical movement of the battle — from Michmash northward past Beth-aven (possibly near Bethel or Ai) — shows the scope of the Philistine rout. The divine name as subject of yāšaʿ recurs in Israel's battle theology (Exod 14:30; Judg 7:7; 2 Kgs 19:34-35), always asserting that military victories were covenant acts, not human achievements.
14.25: wĕkhol-ʾerets bāʾû bayaʿar wayhî ḏĕḇaš ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh — 'All the people came to the forest and there was honey on the ground.' The honey episode: yaʿar (H3293 — forest) with dĕḇaš (H1706 — honey, wild honey from forest bees). The honey flowing on the ground (ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh) suggests a large hive or possibly honeycomb that had fallen from trees. The Philistine territory in the Michmash highlands was known for forest coverage. The contrast with Saul's oath (v24: ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yōkhal leḥem ʿad-hāʿereḇ — 'cursed is the man who eats food until evening') was total: YHWH was providing abundant food to the soldiers precisely when Saul had prohibited eating — YHWH's provision contradicted Saul's oath.
14.26: vayyāḇōʾ hāʿām ʾel-hayyaʿar wehinnēh hēlēkh ḏāḇaš welōʾ-šāmah yādô ʾîš ʾel-pîw — 'When the people came to the forest, there was honey dripping, but no one put his hand to his mouth.' The visual poignancy: the honey was dripping (hēlēkh ḏāḇaš — flowing honey), visible and accessible, and yet the people's hands would not reach their mouths because of Saul's oath (kî-yārēʾ hāʿām ʾet-haššĕḇûʿāh — the people feared the oath). The word yārēʾ (H3372 — to fear, reverence) for the people's response to Saul's oath shows they took it seriously, even as the narrator implies the oath was foolish. The soldiers were exhausted from battle (v31: they were faint) yet denied the energy-giving food that YHWH had placed before them.
14.27: weʾet-ʾêyn-hayyaʿar weḏĕḇaš-ḥāyāh yônāṯān lōʾ šāmaʿ — 'Jonathan had not heard his father command the people with the oath.' The key narrative detail: Jonathan did not know about the oath (lōʾ šāmaʿ — he had not heard). He therefore reached out with the tip of his staff (wayyišlaḥ qētsēh hammattēh ʾăšer bĕyādô), dipped it in a honeycomb (wayyiṭbĕlēhû beyaʿar haddĕḇāš), ate (vayyāšeb yādô ʾel-pîw), and his eyes brightened (vattoʾōrnāh ʿênāyw). The brightening of eyes (ʾôr ʿênayyim — H215, H5869, to give light to the eyes) is a physiological effect of blood sugar restoration but also an idiom for gaining clarity and renewed energy. Jonathan's innocent transgression of an unknowingly unjust oath set the stage for the theological crisis that would follow.
14.28: vayyaʿan ʾîš mēhāʿām vayyōʾmer hiššābîaʿ hēšbîaʿ ʾāḇîkhā — 'One of the people answered: Your father strictly bound the people with an oath.' The doubled infinitive absolute (hiššābîaʿ hēšbîaʿ — strictly swore/bound) intensifies the obligation. The informant's tone is neutral — neither accusatory nor apologetic. Jonathan's response (v29-30) is the critical theological evaluation: ʿākhar ʾāḇî ʾet-hāʾārets (my father has troubled the land). The verb ʿāḵar (H5916 — to trouble, disturb, bring disaster upon — the same word for Achan who troubled Israel, Josh 7:25; for Jephthah, Judg 11:35) is a severe judgment word. Jonathan attributed the battle's incomplete victory directly to Saul's oath: Israel was weakened when it should have been strengthened by the available food.
14.29: vayyōʾmer yônāṯān ʿākhar ʾāḇî ʾet-hāʾārets — 'Jonathan said: My father has brought trouble on the land.' The critique is remarkable — a son explicitly judging his father's military-theological decision as harmful. Jonathan's argument: rĕʾû nāʾ kî ʾōrû ʿênay kî ṭāʿamtî mĕʿaṭ ḏĕḇaš hazzeh (see how my eyes are brightened because I tasted a little of this honey). The empirical argument: the food improved his capacity and energy. The implication: an entire army fed would have been exponentially more effective in battle. Kî ʿal ʾap kî-lōʾ hārĕḇāh hayyôm hāʿām miśśelal ʾōyĕḇāyw (how much greater if the people had freely eaten from their enemies' spoil today!).
14.30: ʾap kî lūʾ ʾākhal ʾākhal hayyôm hāʿām miśśelal ʾōyĕḇāyw — 'How much better if the people had freely eaten from their enemies' spoil today.' The hypothetical (lûʾ — would that, if only — the particle of unfulfilled wish) expresses Jonathan's military assessment: a fed army would have killed more Philistines. Kî ʿattāh lōʾ-rāḇāh hammakkāh bapĕlishtîm (now the defeat of the Philistines has not been great). The victory was partial when it could have been total: the Philistines escaped rather than being destroyed. Saul's oath had limited what YHWH had begun through Jonathan — a military judgment delivered by the very man whose faith had initiated the battle.
14.31: vayyakkû bayyôm hahûʾ bapĕlishtîm mimmikĕmāš ʾayyālônāh vayyāʿap hāʿām meʾōd — 'They struck the Philistines that day from Michmash to Aijalon, and the people were very faint.' The battle line ran from Michmash (the starting point) to Aijalon (H357 — the valley where Joshua commanded the sun to stand still, Josh 10:12) — approximately 30 km of pursuit. The exhaustion was real: vayyāʿap hāʿām meʾōd (the people were very faint — H5774, H3966; yāʿēp means to be weary, exhausted, to fly/rush in exhaustion). The faintness proved Jonathan's point: denied food during a day of intense combat over 30 km, the soldiers were physically depleted when they should have been energized by the abundant honey YHWH had provided.
14.32: vayyāʿaš hāʿām ʾel-hašālāl vayyiqqĕḥû tsōʾn ûḇāqār ûḇnê bāqār vayyišḥăṭû ʾartsāh — 'The people pounced on the spoil and took sheep, oxen, and calves and slaughtered them on the ground.' Vayyāʿaš (H5860 — to rush/fly at, pounce on — from the root of a bird swooping on prey) captures the urgency of the starving soldiers' response. The critical violation: vayyōkhal hāʿām ʿal-haddām (the people ate with the blood) — blood still in the meat — violating the covenant law (Lev 19:26; Deut 12:16,23; Gen 9:4). The soldiers' hunger, created by Saul's oath, led directly to their breaking the blood prohibition. One foolish oath cascaded into a second transgression. Saul's orders had made his people sin — the opposite of a king's purpose.
14.33: vayyaggidû lĕšāʾûl lēʾmōr hinnēh hāʿām ḥōṭĕʾîm laYHWH lĕʾkhōl ʿal-haddām — 'They told Saul: The people are sinning against YHWH by eating with the blood.' The report reached Saul: ḥōṭĕʾîm laYHWH (sinning against YHWH — H2398 from H2399 ḥēṭ, sin/missing the mark). Saul's response was a practical corrective: bigĕdû ûgĕlēlû ʾēlay ʿettāh ʾeḇen gĕḏôlāh (roll a large stone to me right away). By providing a raised surface for proper slaughtering (with blood drainage), Saul tried to stop the sin mid-act. He appointed overseers (šōṭĕrîm) to ensure the animals were slaughtered properly. But the cause of the violation — his own oath — went unaddressed. The king managed the symptom while ignoring the disease.
14.34: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl pōtsû ḇāʿām waʾămartem lāhem haggîšû ʾēlay ʾîš šôrô ûmān ʾîš śēh — 'Saul said: Disperse among the people and say to them: Bring me now every man his ox and his sheep.' The ad hoc slaughterhouse: a large stone (ʾeḇen gĕḏôlāh) as the slaughtering platform, with overseers to ensure proper blood drainage. Vayyiḇīʾû kol-hāʿām ʾîš šôrô bĕyādô hallaylāh (all the people brought, each man his ox, that night). The practical emergency sacrifice allowed the soldiers to eat lawfully. But the Chronicler's note — vayyišḥāṭ šāmmāh (they slaughtered there) — shows Saul building his first altar. The altar-building was improvised rather than ordained: Saul reacting to a crisis he created rather than acting from covenant initiative.
14.35: vayyiḇen šāʾûl mizbēaḥ laYHWH ʾōtô heḥēl liḇnôt mizbēaḥ laYHWH — 'Saul built an altar to YHWH; it was the first altar he had built to YHWH.' The note ʾōtô heḥēl (it was the beginning/this was the first) is both a chronological note and a theological assessment: Saul's first altar was built in response to a crisis he caused. Compare David's altar-building in 2 Sam 24 — also in response to a crisis, but motivated by intercession for the people. Saul's altar was motivated by damage control. The verb bānāh (H1129 — to build) for altar construction anticipates Solomon's temple-building — but Saul's altar was ad hoc, not divinely commissioned. The contrast with Samuel's carefully maintained altar at Ramah (7:17) is implicit.
14.36: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl nērdāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm laylāh — 'Saul said: Let us go down after the Philistines by night.' Saul's plan: a night pursuit (laylāh) to continue the offensive before the Philistines could regroup. The people's readiness: kōl-hayyāšār beʿênêkhā ʾăśēh (do what is good in your eyes). The priest's intervention: hannigĕšāh hălōm ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm (let us approach God here first — H5066). This anonymous priest's caution — consult YHWH before a night attack — represented the proper protocol. Saul finally agreed to consult. But the oracle was silent: welōʾ ʿānāhû YHWH bayyôm hahûʾ (YHWH did not answer him that day). Divine silence was itself a message: someone in the camp had violated a covenant term.
14.37: wayyišʾal šāʾûl beʾĕlōhîm haʾēredāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm — 'Saul inquired of God: Shall I go down after the Philistines?' The formal inquiry followed binary oracle format: haʾēredāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm hatittĕnēm bĕyad yisrāʾēl (shall I go down after the Philistines? will you give them into Israel's hand?). The silence (welōʾ ʿānāhû bayyôm hahûʾ) was interpreted correctly: something was wrong. VayyōʾmerʾelʾYHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾēl hāḇāh tāmîm (Saul said: Come near, all you leaders of the people; know and see how this sin has occurred today). The investigation began with the assumption that the sin was a human breach — likely the oath violation Saul had created, now to be identified through lot-casting.
14.38: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl iggĕšû hălōm kōl pinnôt hāʿām — 'Saul said: Come here, all you leaders of the people.' Pinnôt hāʿām (H6438 — corners/heads of the people, i.e., the leaders) were assembled for the investigation. The command: wĕdiʿû ûrĕʾû bāmmāh hāyĕṯāh haḥaṭṭāʾt hazzōʾt hayyôm (know and see in what this sin has occurred today). The lot-casting format for identifying hidden sin (cf. Josh 7:14-18 — the Achan procedure) required eliminating groups systematically. Saul's self-confident investigation was ironic: he was about to uncover the deed of the son whose heroism had won the day, not knowing that his own oath-creation was the root cause of the crisis.
14.39: kî-ḥay YHWH hamōšîaʿ ʾet-yisrāʾēl — 'As YHWH lives, who saves Israel.' The divine oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives — H2416, H3068) was the strongest possible assertion. Saul swore that even if the violator was his own son Jonathan (kî bĕyônāṯān bĕnî yihyeh), he would die (mōt yāmût). The word hamōšîaʿ (who saves — H3467, the participle from yāšaʿ) is the same root as yĕšûʿāh and Yēšûaʿ/Joshua/Jesus. Saul swore by 'YHWH the Savior' that he would execute the hero who had just saved Israel — the theological irony of using YHWH's saving character as warranty for a death sentence on YHWH's instrument of salvation.
14.40: vayyōʾmer ʾel-kol-yisrāʾēl ʾattem tihyû lĕʾāḥāḏ wĕʾănî wĕyônāṯān bĕnî nīhyeh lĕʾāḥāḏ — 'You be on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on the other side.' The lot-casting procedure divided the community: people versus royal family. Vayyōmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-šāʾûl hakkōḇ ʾet-ʾăšer-ṭôḇ bĕʿênêkhā (the people said to Saul: Do what seems good in your eyes). Their compliance was passive — neither endorsing nor opposing Saul's investigation. The lot fell on the royal family, then further to Jonathan. The lot-casting (gôrāl — H1486, the divination by sacred lots) was understood as YHWH's judgment — its results were authoritative and binding, not subject to appeal.
14.41: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾēl hāḇāh tāmîm — 'Saul said to YHWH God of Israel: Give Urim.' The Urim-Thummim oracle: tāmîm (H8550 — Thummim, completeness, perfection) paired with ûrîm (H224 — Urim, lights, illumination) was the priestly binary oracle system. The specific query structure: 'If the guilt is in me or in Jonathan my son, give Urim; if the guilt is in the people, give Thummim.' The lot fell on Jonathan (vayyillākēd yônāṯān wĕšāʾûl — both were singled out first, then Jonathan alone). The oracle's precision confirmed Jonathan's deed — not as moral guilt (he acted in ignorance) but as the technical trigger that required resolution under Saul's oath.
14.42: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl hapîlû bênî ûḇên yônāṯān bĕnî — 'Saul said: Cast the lot between me and Jonathan my son.' The final casting narrowed to the two — father and son. Vayyillākēd yônāṯān (Jonathan was taken/caught). The word lākaḏ (H3920 — to be caught, trapped, taken — used for animals caught in traps, Ps 9:15; Jer 50:24) applied to Jonathan creates bitter irony: the hero who had 'caught' the Philistines by surprise was now himself 'caught' by his father's oath. The oracle identified the transgressor without judgment of intent — Saul's theological framework required execution regardless of ignorance.
14.43: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl ʾel-yônāṯān haggidāh-lî māh-ʿāśîtā — 'Saul said to Jonathan: Tell me what you have done.' Jonathan's confession was direct and undefended: ṭāʿōm ṭāʿamtî bekṣēh hammaṭṭeh ʾăšer bĕyādî mĕʿaṭ dĕḇaš hinnennî ʾāmût (I tasted a little honey with the tip of the staff I had in my hand. I am ready to die). The doubled infinitive absolute ṭāʿōm ṭāʿamtî (I surely tasted) acknowledges the act without minimizing it. Hinnennî ʾāmût (here I am; I will die) is Jonathan's acceptance of the oath's consequences, even knowing his innocence. His acceptance was not resignation but royal dignity — he had acted rightly, the oracle had spoken, the oath was binding. His courage in battle and his courage in judgment were of the same character.
14.44: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl kōh yaʿăśeh ʾĕlōhîm wĕkhōh yōsîp kî-mōt tāmût yônāṯān — 'Saul said: May God do thus and more also; you shall surely die, Jonathan.' The execution formula kōh yaʿăśeh ʾĕlōhîm wĕkhōh yōsîp (God do so and more so — the standard Hebrew oath formula invoking divine punishment) was followed by kî-mōt tāmût (for you shall surely die — the doubled infinitive absolute signaling certainty). Saul's rigidity is the narrative's darkest moment: the king prepared to execute the hero whose faith had won the day, because of an oath the hero had not heard. The people's rescue of Jonathan (v45) would demonstrate that Israel's moral judgment was sounder than Saul's legal formalism.
14.46: vayyaʿal šāʾûl mē ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm wayyaʿălû pĕlishtîm limĕqômām — 'Saul withdrew from pursuing the Philistines, and the Philistines went to their own place.' The battle's incomplete conclusion: because of the oath crisis and Jonathan's near-execution, the pursuit was abandoned. The Philistines escaped to their territory (limĕqômām). The verb ʿālāh (to go up — H5927) for the Philistines 'going up to their place' may indicate they retreated to the coastal plain, lower in elevation than the Michmash highlands. The battle that Jonathan had initiated with extraordinary faith ended inconclusively — Saul's oath had truncated what YHWH had begun. The victory was real but not decisive; the Philistine threat would persist.
14.47: wĕšāʾûl laḵaḏ hammĕlûkhāh ʿal-yisrāʾēl — 'Saul took the kingship over Israel.' The summary of Saul's wars functions as a regal report, the kind preserved in the royal annals. The enemies he fought: môʾāḇ ûḇnê ʿammôn wĕʾeḏôm ûmalkê tsôḇāh ûḇapĕlishtîm (Moab, Ammonites, Edom, the kings of Zobah, and the Philistines). Ûḇĕkhol ʾăšer yipneh yarîšaʿ (wherever he turned he was victorious — literally 'he acted wickedly/was successful'). The difficult phrase yarîšaʿ (H7561 — a Hiphil form meaning 'he caused to be condemned/attacked'?) is usually translated as 'was victorious.' The catalog of enemies shows Saul's military range — multiple fronts simultaneously — even as the theological assessment of his reign was already compromised.
14.48: vayyaʿas ḥayil vayyakh ʾet-ʿămālēq vayyatssēl ʾet-yisrāʾēl miyaḏ šōsēhû — 'He gathered an army and struck the Amalekites and delivered Israel from those who plundered them.' Vayyaʿas ḥayil (he did valiantly/gathered a force — H2428 from H2428 ḥāyil, strength, army, wealth) summarizes Saul's military achievement. The Amalekite victory (vayyakh ʾet-ʿămālēq) is mentioned here briefly; the full account in ch15 will show the obedience failure that cost Saul the kingdom. But here the summary is positive: Saul did deliver Israel (vayyatssēl ʾet-yisrāʾēl) from her plunderers. The tension in the Saul narrative is that he was a genuine military leader whose accomplishments were real, but whose spiritual failures were equally real and ultimately disqualifying.
14.49: wayhî bĕnê-šāʾûl yônāṯān weyišwî ûmalki-šûaʿ — 'The sons of Saul were Jonathan, Ishvi, and Malchishua.' The family record preserves three sons (yônāṯān weyišwî ûmalki-šûaʿ) and two daughters (mêraḇ haggĕḏôlāh ûmîkhal haqqĕṭannāh — Merab the elder and Michal the younger). Michal would become David's wife (1 Sam 18:20-28); Merab was promised to David but given to Adriel (1 Sam 18:17-19). Išwî (H3440) is sometimes identified with Abinadab who died with Jonathan at Gilboa (1 Sam 31:2). The family genealogy establishes the full context of Saul's household — the connections through which David's path to the throne would run.
14.50: wĕšem ʾēšet šāʾûl ʾăḥînōʿam bat-ʾăḥîmāʿats — 'The name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam daughter of Ahimaaz.' The royal household women are identified by patronymic (bat-ʾăḥîmāʿats — daughter of Ahimaaz). The commander of Saul's army: wĕšem śar-ṣĕḇāʾô ʾăḇnēr ben-nēr dôḏ šāʾûl (Abner son of Ner, Saul's uncle, was commander of his army). Abner would become a significant figure in the David-Saul succession struggle (2 Sam 2-3). His relationship to Saul — dôḏ (uncle) — placed him in the innermost royal circle. The information about Ner (Abner's father) and Kish (Saul's father) being sons of Abiel establishes the wider Benjamite clan network within which Saul's house operated.
14.51: wĕqîš ʾăḇî-šāʾûl wĕnēr ʾăḇî-ʾăḇnēr ben-ʾăḇîʾēl — 'Kish was the father of Saul, and Ner the father of Abner, son of Abiel.' The genealogical note connects the two branches of Saul's family: Kish (Saul's father) and Ner (Abner's father) were both sons of Abiel (ben-ʾăḇîʾēl). This means Saul and Abner were first cousins (not uncle-nephew as v50 implies — the word dôḏ can mean both). The genealogy validates Abner's military appointment: he was royal family, not merely a talented outsider. His loyalty to the house of Saul (2 Sam 2:8-9 — placing Ish-bosheth on the throne) was kinship loyalty as much as political calculation.
14.52: wattihî hammilḥāmāh ḥăzāqāh ʿal-pĕlishtîm kōl-yĕmê šāʾûl — 'There was fierce war against the Philistines all the days of Saul.' The summary verse frames Saul's entire reign: chronic war (milḥāmāh ḥăzāqāh — fierce/hard war) against the Philistines was the defining context. The military personnel policy: wĕkhol-ʾîš gibbôr ʾăšer rāʾāh šāʾûl (every capable warrior that Saul saw), vayyeʾespēhû ʾēlāyw (he gathered him to himself). Saul's standing army recruitment created the military context into which David would enter: 1 Sam 16:21 — 'David came to Saul and entered his service.' The final verse of ch14 is the hinge between the Saul cycle and the David cycle: the chronic war that Saul could not win would be the context in which YHWH raised up his chosen king.

9.1: The chapter opens with a genealogy reaching back five generations — Kish son of Abiel son of Zeror son of Bechorath son of Aphiah — before landing on the key descriptor: gibbôr ḥayil, a man of standing/valor. In narrative, genealogies signal that a new major figure is entering the story. The Benjaminite lineage is theologically loaded: Benjamin was the youngest son of Jacob, the tribe nearly exterminated in the Gibeah atrocity (Judg 20), and yet the tribe that will produce Israel's first king.
9.2: Saul's introduction is dominated by physical description: bāḥûr wāṭôḇ — choice and good, i.e., a fine young man — and above all his height, for from his shoulders upward he was taller than all the people. The narrative underscores external appearance, which foreshadows the irony to come: YHWH sees differently (16:7). Tall stature was the ANE mark of royal fitness; Saul looks like a king, which is precisely what Israel will point to when affirming him (10:24).
9.3: The narrative mechanism that will end with a king being anointed begins with ătōnôt ʾăḇad — donkeys have strayed. Kish sends Saul with a servant to find them. The Hebrew ʾāḇad (strayed/perished) is the same root used for lost sheep and lost children; the narrator's choice of this verb begins the providential-search motif. Israel's future king is on a mundane agricultural errand, unaware that YHWH has already told Samuel to expect him.
9.4: The search route — hill country of Ephraim, land of Shalisha, land of Shaalim, land of Benjamin — covers a wide arc of the central highlands without success. The repeated wəlōʾ māṣāʾû (and they did not find them) drives the narrative tension: the more thoroughly the search fails, the more completely Saul is displaced from his starting point. The geography situates the action in the territory between Gibeah (Saul's home) and Ramah (Samuel's base).
9.5: Saul shows practical wisdom — 'Let us turn back, lest my father stop worrying about the donkeys and worry about us instead.' The inversion is gently ironic: Saul is worried about causing his father anxiety, while YHWH has already arranged the meeting ahead of them. Saul's practical reasoning, pointing homeward, is what YHWH's providence overrides through the servant's suggestion in v6.
9.6: The servant knows of a 'man of God' (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm) in the city — a man of distinction whose every word comes true (ḵol ʾăšer yədabbēr bōʾ yāḇōʾ). This phrase defines the true prophet: words that reliably correspond to reality. The servant's faith, not Saul's initiative, propels the story forward. Samuel has not been named yet — the reader, who already knows Samuel from chs 1-8, recognizes who is being described before the characters do.
9.7: Saul's concern is pragmatic: 'If we go, what shall we bring the man? The bread in our sacks is gone, and there is no gift to bring to the man of God.' The ANE practice of consulting a prophet came with a gift — a fee for prophetic services. Saul's worry reveals he understands the protocol. The problem of having nothing to offer will be resolved by the servant's shekel (v8), but it introduces the theme of what one brings before a holy man.
9.8: The servant produces a quarter shekel of silver — enough for a gift. The denominations mentioned here are small; this is not a wealthy consultation. The servant's creative solution moves the narrative: the gift question resolved, the men turn toward the city. Throughout the chapter, the servant functions as the wiser and more spiritually alert companion — he knows about the prophet, has the silver, and suggests the plan.
9.9: The narrator inserts an editorial gloss: 'Formerly in Israel, anyone who went to inquire of God said, Come, let us go to the rōʾeh (seer); for the one who is today called a nāḇîʾ (prophet) was formerly called a rōʾeh.' This is a rare instance of the narrative's own historical self-awareness — the writer knows the story is ancient and glosses the archaic title for later readers. The two terms rōʾeh and nāḇîʾ reflect different emphases: rōʾeh stresses the visionary-perceptive function; nāḇîʾ stresses the spokesperson function. Both apply to Samuel.
9.10: They go up toward the city. The spatial movement — 'going up' (ʿālāh) — is standard for approaching a higher town, but also carries the cultic connotation of ascending to YHWH (cf. the Psalms of Ascent). They agree to go to the seer. The narrative pacing here is deliberate: each small decision — the servant's knowledge, the gift, the agreement to go — tightens the net of providence without any character realizing it.
9.11: At the city's edge they meet young women going out to draw water and ask about the seer. The question is practical, but the women's detailed answer (vv12-13) provides Saul with a precise window: Samuel is here today for the people's sacrifice at the high place, the people won't eat until he blesses it, and he was expected today. The women's knowledge of Samuel's schedule indicates his public visibility and his cultic role presiding over communal sacrificial meals.
9.12: The women's reply — 'He is ahead of you; hurry now, for he has come to the city today because the people have a sacrifice today at the high place' — establishes the cultic context. Bāmāh (high place) in this period is not yet condemned as idolatrous; the critique of Solomonic-era high places in Kings does not apply here. Samuel legitimately presides over sacrificial worship at a local high place before the Jerusalem temple exists.
9.13: The women specify the timing: the people will not eat until Samuel arrives because he must bless the sacrifice first. This detail places Samuel in the role of sacrificial officiant, a function that will later belong exclusively to priests. In the pre-monarchic and early monarchic period, the boundaries between prophet, priest, and judge overlap; Samuel holds all three functions simultaneously. The instruction to 'go up at once' gives the scene urgency.
9.14: As Saul and his servant enter the city, Samuel is coming out toward them on his way up to the high place. The meeting is exactly timed — they arrive at the gate precisely as Samuel exits. The narrative makes no comment; the perfection of the timing is left for the reader to register as the working of divine providence already announced in v15. No one has had to arrange this meeting; YHWH's prior word to Samuel simply proves accurate.
9.15: YHWH's disclosure to Samuel the day before: 'Tomorrow about this time I will send you a man from the land of Benjamin, and you shall anoint him as ruler (nāgîd) over my people Israel.' The term nāgîd is significant — it is not melek (king) but the term for a military/political leader designated by divine appointment. Nāgîd in Samuel-Kings regularly describes the one YHWH has chosen ahead of or instead of hereditary kingship. YHWH will anoint a nāgîd, which Israel will call a melek — the tension between divine designation and popular institution is already embedded in the terminology.
9.16: YHWH's rationale: 'He shall save my people from the hand of the Philistines. For I have seen my people's misery (kî rāʾîtî ʾeṯ-ʿammî), because their outcry has reached me.' The language is virtually identical to Exodus 3:7-9 — YHWH seeing the people's affliction, hearing their cry, coming down to deliver. The Saul anointing is cast in exodus-deliverance language: this is YHWH again responding to Israel's suffering at the hands of oppressors by raising up a deliverer.
9.17: When Samuel sees Saul, YHWH says: 'Here is the man of whom I told you! He it is who shall restrain my people.' Yaʿṣōr bəʿammî — 'restrain/govern my people' — is an unusual phrase, suggesting both military restraint of the enemy and governance of the people. Samuel's recognition is immediate and divine-speech-confirmed: he doesn't need to ask; YHWH narrates in real time as Saul approaches.
9.18: Saul approaches Samuel at the gate and asks: 'Tell me where the seer's house is.' He does not yet know he is speaking to the very man he seeks. Samuel's identity is still concealed from Saul — the reader, with Samuel's privilege, knows what Saul does not. This narrative irony (the one sought speaks to the one seeking) is gentle comedy of errors, soon resolved.
9.19: Samuel reveals himself: 'I am the seer.' The disclosure is immediate and followed by three commands: go up ahead of me, eat with me today, I will send you on your way tomorrow. Then the news: 'As for your donkeys that were lost three days ago, do not be anxious about them, for they have been found.' The lost-donkey plot is resolved in a single verse — it was never the real story. Saul's mission is complete before he knew he had one.
9.20: Samuel's rhetorical question to Saul: 'And for whom is all that is desirable in Israel? Is it not for you and for all your father's house?' The phrase 'all that is desirable in Israel' (ûləmî ḵol-ḥemdaṯ yiśrāʾēl) is deliberately ambiguous — it could refer to the kingship or to some unspecified blessing. Saul does not yet understand. This compression — Samuel knows everything, Saul knows nothing — is the chapter's theological point: YHWH's initiative precedes human comprehension.
9.21: Saul's response enacts the appropriate humility: 'Am I not a Benjaminite, from the least of the tribes of Israel? And is not my clan the humblest of all the clans of the tribe of Benjamin? Why then have you spoken to me in this way?' The echo of Gideon's self-deprecation (Judg 6:15 — 'my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house') establishes Saul in the tradition of the unlikely deliverer chosen from the margins. Benjamin's status as 'least' recalls the near-extinction of the tribe in Judges 20-21.
9.22: Samuel brings Saul and his servant into the hall and seats them at the head of the invited guests — about thirty men. The seating at the head of the feast honors Saul before the assembled community without yet explaining why. The number thirty suggests a significant gathering, possibly clan leaders or representative elders. The meal before the anointing is itself a covenant-sealing gesture: shared table fellowship precedes the formal appointment.
9.23: Samuel commands the cook to bring the portion he had reserved, saying 'Bring the portion I gave you, of which I said to you, Set it aside.' The pre-reserved portion signals advance preparation — Samuel has been expecting this guest. The language is priestly: set-aside portions in cultic meals are portions designated for the officiating priest (cf. Lev 7:34). Samuel is treating Saul's arrival as the fulfillment of a liturgical anticipation.
9.24: The cook brings the leg and what is with it and sets it before Saul. Samuel says: 'See, what was kept is set before you. Eat, because until the appointed time it was kept for you when I said, I have invited the people.' The leg (šôq) is the most honored portion of the sacrificial animal, typically given to the priest (cf. Lev 7:32-33). Saul is given the priestly portion without yet being told why — the meal enacts his elevation before the verbal anointing does.
9.25: After coming down from the high place, Samuel speaks with Saul on the roof (gāg). Rooftop conversations in Hebrew narrative carry intimacy — the roof is liminal, semi-public, a place for private disclosure above the noise of the street. Samuel communicates something on the roof that the narrative does not reproduce. The content is implied: preparation for the anointing, the shape of his calling, what the morning will bring.
9.26: At dawn Samuel calls to Saul on the roof: 'Get up, so that I may send you on your way.' The sending (šālaḥ) echoes the prophetic commissioning vocabulary — Samuel is acting as YHWH's agent dispatching YHWH's appointed. Saul rises and goes out with Samuel. The dawn hour is the hour of prophetic disclosure elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Gen 19:15, 22:3).
9.27: At the edge of town Samuel tells Saul's servant to pass on ahead while Samuel gives Saul YHWH's word. The deliberate separation — 'stand still for a time, so that I may make known to you the word of God' — creates the private space for the anointing of chapter 10. Samuel orchestrates the encounter with precision, ensuring the anointing happens between them alone. The final verse of ch9 is the threshold moment; ch10 opens on the other side of it.

10.1: Samuel takes a flask of oil (pak haššemen), pours it on Saul's head, kisses him, and says: 'Has YHWH not anointed you as ruler (nāgîd) over his heritage (naḥălāṯô)?' The anointing with oil (māšaḥ) is the act that makes Saul māšîaḥ — anointed one. The vocabulary connects to royal and priestly anointing across the Torah and is the root of the later Hebrew māšîaḥ and Greek christos. Samuel's kiss is a covenant gesture — the kiss in the ANE signals the sealing of a relationship, not merely affection. YHWH's heritage is naḥălāh — inheritance/patrimony — Israel seen as YHWH's personal possession.
10.2: The first confirming sign: two men at Rachel's tomb in Benjaminite territory at Zelzah will tell Saul the donkeys have been found and that his father is worried about him. Rachel's tomb (near Bethlehem or at Zelzah in Benjamin — the location is debated) is a patriarchal landmark. The sign is mundane and verifiable — Saul can check it immediately. The three signs move from the mundane (donkeys found) to the sacred (Spirit-filled prophecy), scaling up toward the transformative.
10.3: The second sign: three men going up to God at Bethel, carrying young goats, three loaves of bread, and a skin of wine. One will greet Saul and give him two loaves of bread, which he will accept. Bethel is a pilgrimage site (one of Israel's major sanctuaries before Jerusalem); the three men are on a cultic journey. Saul receives bread — the provision confirms his appointment. The bread gift anticipates the sacrificial meal at Gibeah, where the Spirit-event will occur.
10.4: The men will greet Saul and offer two loaves of bread, which he should accept from them. The dual loaves (šənê leḥem) may echo the double portion of the firstborn or the elevated portion; accepting the gift is an act of receptivity that positions Saul as one who receives provision for the task rather than providing it. The three-sign structure moves incrementally: first domestic news (donkeys), then cultic provision (bread), then prophetic transformation (Spirit).
10.5: The third sign: at the hill of God (giḇʿaṯ hāʾĕlōhîm), where the Philistine garrison is, a band of prophets coming down from the high place with harp, tambourine, flute, and lyre, prophesying. The location is charged: the garrison of the Philistine overlords is at the same place where YHWH's prophetic band operates. The juxtaposition — military occupation and prophetic worship in the same location — is the social landscape of early monarchy.
10.6: 'The Spirit of YHWH will rush upon you (ṣālaḥ), and you will prophesy with them and be turned into another man (wənehepaḵtā ləʾîš ʾaḥēr).' The verb ṣālaḥ denotes a sudden, powerful onset — the same word used when the Spirit came on Samson (Judg 14:6,19; 15:14). The transformation into 'another man' is the most striking phrase: it describes a fundamental reorientation of character, not merely a temporary prophetic episode. This is the OT's language for Spirit-transformation.
10.7: 'When these signs come to you, do what the occasion requires, for God is with you.' The freedom granted here — 'do what the occasion requires' (ʿăśēh-ləḵā ʾăšer timmāṣēʾ yāḏeḵā, lit. 'do what your hand finds') — is a significant latitude for the newly anointed one. Samuel is not micromanaging; he is authorizing Saul to act with royal initiative once the Spirit confirms the appointment. The formula 'God is with you' is the guarantee that grounds the freedom.
10.8: Samuel instructs Saul to go ahead to Gilgal and wait seven days for Samuel to come, offer burnt offerings, and sacrifice peace offerings. This instruction is fateful — Saul's later failure in 13:8-14 occurs precisely when he fails to wait the full seven days. The instruction is both practical (establishing Gilgal as the cultic center for military campaign) and a test: will Saul trust Samuel's timeline when Philistine pressure mounts?
10.9: 'As he turned his back to leave Samuel, God gave him another heart (wəhāpak-lô ʾĕlōhîm lēḇ ʾaḥēr).' The divine transformation happens immediately at Saul's departure — not at some later date. The 'other heart' corresponds to the 'other man' of v6: both describe the Spirit-effected change that accompanies anointing. All the signs came that day, confirming that Samuel's word was accurate and that divine endorsement was underway.
10.10: At Gibeah, the band of prophets meets Saul and the Spirit of God rushes on him (ṣālaḥ), and he prophesies among them. The third sign is fulfilled exactly as Samuel described. Saul is caught up in the prophetic band's Spirit-empowered activity. Prophecy here is not necessarily verbal proclamation; the Hebrew hitnabbēʾ can describe ecstatic behavior, song, and Spirit-inspired movement — a whole-person experience of divine presence.
10.11: Those who had known Saul previously see him prophesying among the prophets and say: 'What has happened to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets (hagam šāʾûl bannəḇîʾîm)?' The question expresses surprise: Saul is not from the prophetic circles, not a trained member of the prophetic band. The Spirit's work exceeds social categories — it falls on a Benjaminite farmer-warrior, not on professional prophets. The question becomes a proverb (see v12).
10.12: A man from that area responds: 'And who is their father?' — a rhetorical question implying the prophets have no single parentage, that the Spirit makes prophets without hereditary line. The saying 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' becomes a proverbial expression in Israel, cited again in 19:24. The proverb encodes the lesson: the Spirit moves sovereignly, outside conventional categories of lineage and training.
10.13: After the prophetic episode, Saul comes to the high place. The transition back to ordinary movement — from ecstatic Spirit experience to walking up to the high place — marks the temporary nature of this initial Spirit-coming. Unlike the anointing's 'other heart' (v9), the prophetic rush was an episode within a larger process of confirmation, not the permanent Spirit-indwelling of ch 16 which is then definitively withdrawn in 16:14.
10.14: Saul's uncle asks him where he went. Saul tells him: he went to look for the donkeys and found them with Samuel. The uncle presses: 'Tell me what Samuel said to you.' The question tests whether Saul will reveal the anointing. His response is instructive.
10.15: 'Please tell me what Samuel said to you.' The uncle's curiosity is genuine but Saul's discretion in response (v16) is notable. The uncle-nephew relationship was significant in Benjaminite tribal structures; Saul's uncle may have been monitoring family developments.
10.16: Saul tells his uncle they found the donkeys — and says nothing about the kingship. 'But about the matter of the kingdom, of which Samuel had spoken, he did not tell him anything.' The restraint is theologically significant: Saul does not yet act on the anointing, does not claim the kingship, does not announce himself. This discretion contrasts with later Saul, who will act precipitously. The concealment preserves YHWH's chosen moment for the public revelation.
10.17: Samuel summons the people to YHWH at Mizpah. Mizpah (meaning 'watchtower') in Benjamin was a major assembly point — used for the anti-Benjamin war (Judg 20-21) and now for the formal installation of the king. Samuel convenes the national assembly as YHWH's prophet, giving the event theocratic legitimacy. The choice of Mizpah connects Saul's installation to the site of earlier national crisis.
10.18: Samuel's preamble rehearses YHWH's saving acts: 'I brought Israel up from Egypt and delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of all the kingdoms that oppressed you.' The covenant-recital formula before the lot-casting reminds Israel whose authority governs even the selection of a king. The king they are about to install is still YHWH's choice — the lot will confirm it — but YHWH's prior claim as deliverer must be acknowledged first.
10.19: 'But today you have rejected your God, who saves you from all your evils and your distresses, and you have said to him, Set a king over us.' Samuel's indictment is sharp: the act of asking for a king, even before a king has done anything wrong, is already a rejection of YHWH as king. The people are not rejecting bad leadership; they are rejecting the covenant structure in which YHWH leads through charismatic judges and prophets. Despite this, YHWH concedes the request.
10.20: The lot-casting by tribes: Samuel presents all the tribes and Benjamin is chosen. The Hebrew lākaḏ (taken/caught by lot) is used for the lot-selection process — the lot 'takes' the tribe, then the clan, then the household, then the man. This sequential narrowing from tribe to individual enacts YHWH's sovereignty at every level of social organization.
10.21: The Matrites are taken from Benjamin, and from the Matrite family Saul son of Kish is taken. But when they seek him, he is not found. Saul hides among the baggage (baḵkēlîm). The image of the king-elect hiding in the luggage is one of the most peculiar in the narrative. Commentators debate whether this is fear, false modesty, or a kind of ritual withdrawal. The hiding emphasizes that Saul is not self-promoting — he must be extracted rather than presenting himself.
10.22: They ask YHWH: 'Is there a man still to come here?' YHWH answers: 'He has hidden himself among the baggage.' Even Saul's hiding place is within YHWH's knowledge. The oracle is laconic — no explanation of why Saul hid — but confirms that YHWH's chosen one and the lot-selection agree. The lot is not mechanical chance; it discloses YHWH's predetermined selection.
10.23: They run and bring Saul out, and he stands among the people taller than all the people from his shoulders upward — the physical description of 9:2 is repeated. The narrative returns to the height marker at the moment of public presentation: this is the external quality Israel had in mind when they asked for a king 'like all the nations.' YHWH grants them a king who fits their category, knowing the deeper failure that will follow.
10.24: Samuel says to all the people: 'Do you see him whom YHWH has chosen? There is none like him among all the people.' And all the people shouted, 'Long live the king!' (yəḥî hammelek). The acclamation formula is the formal public installation. Samuel's rhetorical question — 'Do you see him?' — inverts the seeing-motif: Israel sees the tall external form; YHWH has seen differently. The crowd's unanimous endorsement completes the installation that YHWH's private anointing began.
10.25: Samuel explains to the people the rights and duties of the kingship (mišpaṭ hamməlûḵāh), writes them in a scroll, and places it before YHWH. The written record of constitutional limits on kingship is a remarkable act: the monarchy is inaugurated with a constitutional document from the start. This 'law of the king' may be related to Deuteronomy 17:14-20's royal law. Writing it 'before YHWH' (i.e., deposited at the sanctuary) gives it covenant-treaty status.
10.26: Saul goes home to Gibeah, and with him go the 'sons of valor' (ḥayil) whose hearts God had touched. The emergence of a voluntary honor guard before any formal military appointment is another sign of divine orchestration: the people who attach themselves to Saul are those whose hearts God has disposed toward him. The kingdom forms organically around the anointed one before any institutional structure is in place.
10.27: But some worthless men (bənê bəlîyyaʿal) despise Saul, saying 'How can this man save us?' and bring him no gift. Saul holds his peace. The bənê bəlîyyaʿal — sons of worthlessness/Belial — are the counter-voice in every pivotal moment of the Deuteronomistic History (cf. Judg 19:22; 1 Sam 2:12). Their question — 'How can this man save us?' — is not merely disrespect; it questions the entire enterprise of YHWH's appointed deliverer. Saul's silence in response to mockery contrasts with his later rash reactions.

11.1: Nahash the Ammonite besieges Jabesh-Gilead. Jabesh-Gilead had a complex history with Benjamin: it was the one city that refused to join the anti-Benjamin war (Judg 21:8-9), and its daughters became wives for the Benjaminite survivors. Saul's rescue of Jabesh-Gilead thus has a genealogical dimension — he may be rescuing people connected to his own tribe's survival. Nahash means 'serpent'; the name's ominous resonance with Genesis 3 is not lost in a theologically sensitive reading.
11.2: Nahash's terms are brutal: he will make the treaty only if he may gouge out every man's right eye and bring disgrace on all Israel. The gouging of the right eye — the dominant eye for archery and swordsmanship — would permanently disable the men as soldiers. This is not mere cruelty but systematic military neutralization: a blinded right eye removes the city's capacity for armed resistance. The Ammonite king's confidence in his position is total.
11.3: The elders of Jabesh buy seven days: 'Leave us alone for seven days so we can send messengers throughout Israel. If there is no one to save us, we will surrender to you.' Nahash agrees — a tactical overconfidence, assuming no one can rally quickly enough. The seven-day window is the narrative opening through which YHWH will act. In a culture without rapid communication, seven days barely suffices for messages to travel and forces to assemble.
11.4: When the messengers reach Gibeah of Saul, the people weep. Gibeah is Saul's home city — again the Benjaminite geography creates a personal connection. The messengers have come to the right place without knowing it: the man YHWH has anointed to save Israel is in this very city.
