The people's weeping is the response of those without a deliverer — the extremity of helplessness that YHWH's Spirit will address in v6.
11.5: Saul comes in from the field behind the oxen and asks: 'What is wrong with the people, why are they weeping?' He is still farming — plowing with oxen is significant peasant-king imagery. Saul has not assumed the royal prerogatives of ch 10; he is still a farmer. When they tell him about Jabesh-Gilead, the Spirit intervenes (v6). Saul's return from the field to receive the Spirit parallels Elisha leaving his plowing (1 Kings 19:19-21).
11.6: 'The Spirit of God rushed upon Saul when he heard these words, and his anger burned greatly.' The formula ṣālaḥ rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm — the same construction used in the Samson narratives and promised in 10:6 — here combines Spirit-empowerment with righteous indignation (ʾapô bəʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ). The anger is not personal reaction but Spirit-channeled zeal for YHWH's people. The Spirit's coming on Saul at the national crisis confirms the anointing of ch 10 with its first public demonstration.
11.7: Saul takes a yoke of oxen, cuts them into pieces, and sends the pieces throughout Israel with the message: 'Whoever does not come after Saul and Samuel, so shall it be done to his oxen.' The action echoes Judges 19:29 where the Levite cut the concubine's body into pieces — both invoke the cut-up body as a summons to tribal assembly. Saul's version is an animal, not a human body, but the symbolic grammar is the same. Adding Samuel's name establishes that Saul acts with prophetic authorization, not merely royal command. The fear of YHWH falls on all the people.
11.8: The muster at Bezek yields 300,000 men of Israel and 30,000 men of Judah — the numbers reflect the idealized tribal levy of a united monarchy, not a standing army. The separate count for Judah hints at the north-south distinction that will fracture the kingdom under Rehoboam. Even in Saul's finest hour, the structural fault lines of Israel's eventual division appear in the census data.
11.9: The messengers return to Jabesh-Gilead with the news: 'Tomorrow, by the time the sun is hot, you will have deliverance (təšûʿāh).' The word təšûʿāh is YHWH's salvation-word — the same root as Joshua's and Jesus's names. The men of Jabesh rejoice: 'Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you shall do with us whatever seems good to you.' Their surrender-language is now a trust-language; they are no longer surrendering to an enemy but waiting for a deliverer.
11.10: The men of Jabesh tell Nahash: 'Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you can do with us whatever seems good to you' — the same words to an enemy as in v9 to the deliverer, but with opposite intent. The deception is defensive necessity: they buy the night so Saul can march. The parallel phrasing (kəṭôḇ bəʿêneḵā to both parties) is the narrator's wry double entendre.
11.11: Saul organizes his forces in three companies for a dawn attack and strikes the Ammonites until 'the heat of the day' — fulfilling the timing promised in v9. The three-column attack (a standard ANE tactical formation) routs the Ammonites so completely that those who survive are scattered 'so that no two of them were left together.' The completeness of the victory underscores the Spirit-empowered deliverance; this is not tactical brilliance but theophanic war.
11.12: The people ask Samuel: 'Who said Saul should reign over us? Hand those men over so we can kill them.' The worthless men of 10:27 who despised Saul are now subject to popular vindication. The people's demand that the doubters be executed is understandable — the king they despised has just delivered them — but Saul's response is the chapter's moral climax.
11.13: Saul says: 'Not a man shall be put to death this day, for today YHWH has worked salvation (təšûʿāh) in Israel.' The mercy-on-a-victory-day is remarkable: the day of military triumph, when the king has maximum authority and popular support, is declared a day of clemency rather than vengeance. Saul explicitly grounds this in YHWH's prior act — 'YHWH has worked salvation' — placing the victory outside his own achievement and therefore the right of revenge outside his prerogative. This is Saul at his best.
11.14: Samuel calls all Israel to Gilgal to 'renew the kingdom' (nəḥaddēš hamməlûḵāh). Gilgal was the first campsite after crossing the Jordan (Josh 4-5) — Israel's original covenant-renewal site in the land. The choice of Gilgal for the renewal anchors the new monarchy in the Conquest traditions. The kingship is 'renewed' rather than newly installed, perhaps indicating that the Mizpah installation of ch 10 was conditional pending a victory demonstration.
11.15: They make Saul king before YHWH at Gilgal, offer peace offerings (šəlāmîm), and all Israel rejoices greatly. The peace offerings are the covenantal meal that seals the arrangement — after the burnt offering that consecrates the occasion. Saul's kingship is formally installed amid genuine communal celebration, before YHWH, at the most significant covenant site in the land. The chapter ends at the apex of Saul's legitimacy and popular support — the fall of ch 13 will contrast with this height.

12.1: Samuel's farewell speech begins with a public accountability review: 'I have listened to you in all that you have said to me and have set a king over you.' Samuel acknowledges his compliance with the people's demand — he is not revisiting the question of whether the kingship was right but moving to the terms of accountability that now govern it. The speech functions as a covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) — a juridical form used when YHWH or his representative brings charges against the covenant people.
12.2: 'Now behold, the king walks before you, and I am old and gray, and behold, my sons are with you. I have walked before you from my youth until this day.' Samuel's intergenerational framing — the king is young and beginning, Samuel is old and ending — establishes the transition moment. His sons' presence in the assembly is a loaded reference: they were the corrupt judges of 8:1-3 whose failure precipitated the demand for a king. Samuel does not hide this history.
12.3: 'Here I am; testify against me before YHWH and before his anointed. Whose ox have I taken? Whose donkey have I taken? Whom have I defrauded? Whom have I oppressed? From whose hand have I taken a bribe to blind my eyes with it?' The rhetorical questions constitute a covenant integrity oath — Samuel invokes YHWH and the king as witnesses to his clean record. The specific items (ox, donkey, bribe) correspond to the abuses Samuel will soon describe as the pattern of kings (8:14-17). Samuel's governance was the alternative to what kings do.
12.4: The people respond: 'You have not defrauded us or oppressed us or taken anything from any man's hand.' The unanimous public acquittal is Samuel's covenant credential — he exercised authority without extracting tribute. The contrast with the corrupt sons of 8:3 and the projected abuses of royal government (8:11-18) is pointed. The people's verdict legitimizes Samuel's authority to now speak covenant law to them.
12.5: 'YHWH is witness against you, and his anointed is witness this day, that you have not found anything in my hand.' And they said, 'He is witness.' The double-witness formula (YHWH + the king) satisfies Deuteronomy's requirement for two or three witnesses (Deut 19:15). Samuel has established the court's legitimacy before delivering the covenant address.
12.6: Samuel continues: 'YHWH is witness, who appointed Moses and Aaron and who brought your fathers up from the land of Egypt.' The saving-history section (vv6-13) begins with the Exodus — the founding act of covenant relationship. Samuel's survey will move from Moses/Aaron through the judges to the present, building the case that YHWH has been Israel's constant deliverer while Israel has been a constant covenant-breaker.
12.7: 'Now therefore stand still so that I may plead with you before YHWH concerning all the righteous deeds (ṣidqôṯ) of YHWH that he did for you and for your fathers.' Ṣidqôṯ YHWH — YHWH's righteous acts/saving acts — is a technical term in the Psalms and prophets for YHWH's interventions on behalf of the covenant people. Samuel frames YHWH's history as a record of consistent faithfulness; he will then frame Israel's history as a record of consistent apostasy. The contrast is the foundation of the covenant lawsuit.
12.8: 'When Jacob went into Egypt and your fathers cried out to YHWH, then YHWH sent Moses and Aaron, who brought your fathers out of Egypt and made them dwell in this place.' The saving-history summary compresses Exodus through Settlement into a single verse — the characteristic Deuteronomistic credo form (cf. Deut 26:5-9; Josh 24:2-13). The credo functions as covenant memory: this is what YHWH did, which is why covenant obligation exists.
12.9: But they forgot YHWH their God, and he sold them into the hand of Sisera the commander of Jabin's army and into the hand of the Philistines and into the hand of the king of Moab. The šāḵaḥ (forget) of covenant unfaithfulness is paired with mākar (sold/delivered over) — YHWH's judicial abandonment of the people to their enemies is the consequence of forgetting him. The three oppressors listed (Sisera, Philistines, Moab) cover major episodes of the book of Judges.
12.10: They cried out to YHWH: 'We have sinned, because we have forsaken YHWH and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth. Now deliver us from the hand of our enemies and we will serve you.' The covenant repentance formula is identical to the cycle pattern in Judges (e.g., Judg 10:10-16): acknowledgment of sin, identification of the sin (serving Baals and Ashtaroth), petition for deliverance, pledge of renewed service. Samuel is identifying the present moment as yet another instance of the recurring cycle.
12.11: YHWH sent Jerubbaal (Gideon), Bedan, Jephthah, and Samuel himself to deliver Israel. The list of judges includes 'Bedan' — an otherwise unknown figure; some manuscripts read 'Barak' here (cf. LXX). Samuel's inclusion of himself in the list of deliverers is not self-aggrandizement but covenantal continuity: his office is in the same sequence as the judges, the last expression of the charismatic-deliverer pattern before the monarchy.
12.12: 'And when you saw that Nahash the king of the Ammonites came against you, you said to me, No, but a king shall reign over us, when YHWH your God was your king.' The juxtaposition is sharp: the Ammonite threat (just resolved in ch 11) was the stated reason for demanding a king. But YHWH was already their king, and the judges-pattern of deliverance was still operative. Israel's demand was not practical necessity but an act of covenant rejection.
12.13: 'And now behold the king whom you have chosen, for whom you have asked; behold, YHWH has set a king over you.' The double hinnēh (behold) and the double acknowledgment — you chose/you asked — holds the people accountable while affirming that YHWH has fulfilled the request. The king they wanted is now their king; YHWH has conceded while maintaining the record of the demand's faithlessness. The tension between divine disapproval and divine concession is unresolved — it characterizes the entire monarchic history.
12.14: The conditional covenant formula: 'If you will fear YHWH and serve him and obey his voice and not rebel against the commandment of YHWH, and if both you and the king who reigns over you will follow YHWH your God, it will be well.' The introduction of the king into the covenant formula is the new element: monarchy does not replace the Sinai covenant but is incorporated under it. Both people and king are now equally subject to covenant obligation. The blessing-conditional is open: the monarchy can still succeed if covenant faithfulness is maintained.
12.15: 'But if you will not obey the voice of YHWH and will rebel against the commandment of YHWH, then the hand of YHWH will be against you and your king.' The curse-conditional mirrors the blessing but adds 'and your king' — the monarchy participates in the covenant curse as well as the blessing. This is the theological framework for every subsequent royal evaluation in Samuel-Kings: kings are judged by covenant faithfulness, and the people's fate is tied to their king's obedience.
12.16: 'Now therefore stand and see this great thing that YHWH will do before your eyes.' Samuel invites the assembled Israel to witness a covenant sign. The invitation to 'stand and see' (hiṯyaṣṣəḇû ûrəʾû) is a theophanic summons — the language used when YHWH is about to act visibly. The covenant sign will be thunder and rain (v17-18) — unusual weather at wheat harvest, which occurs in the dry season (May-June) when rain is abnormal in Palestine.
12.17: 'Is it not wheat harvest today? I will call upon YHWH, that he may send thunder and rain. And you shall know and see that your wickedness is great, which you have done in the sight of YHWH, in asking for yourselves a king.' The thunder-at-harvest is a meteorological sign of divine displeasure: wheat harvest in ancient Canaan/Israel falls in May-June during the dry season; unseasonal rain and thunder would be unmistakable as a divine message. Samuel's ability to summon such a sign confirms his prophetic authority.
12.18: Samuel calls on YHWH, and YHWH sends thunder and rain that day. The entire people greatly fears YHWH and Samuel. The combined fear of YHWH and Samuel indicates that the sign achieved its purpose: the people recognize both divine reality and prophetic authority in a single event. The thunder-and-rain sign is the covenant lawsuit's climactic 'exhibit': YHWH testifies in the natural world.
12.19: The people say to Samuel: 'Pray for your servants to YHWH your God, that we may not die, for we have added to all our sins this evil, to ask for ourselves a king.' The combination of awe and self-condemnation is the desired covenant response — they acknowledge the sin, not merely the fear. Their appeal to Samuel's intercession ('pray for your servants') acknowledges that they need a mediator whose relationship with YHWH can withstand their guilt. The phrase 'we have added to all our sins this evil' is covenant self-indictment.
12.20: Samuel's consolation: 'Do not be afraid; you have done all this evil. Yet do not turn aside from following YHWH, but serve YHWH with all your heart.' The pastoral grace of this verse is extraordinary: Samuel does not minimize the sin ('you have done all this evil') but immediately pivots to the path forward. The covenant's conditional threats do not preclude continued covenant relationship; repentance and ongoing service are still available. Fear is not the goal; covenant faithfulness is.
12.21: 'And do not turn aside after empty things (haṯṯōhû) that cannot profit or deliver, for they are empty.' Tōhû — void, emptiness, the word used in Genesis 1:2 for formless creation — describes the idols and political alternatives that Israel might pursue. The contrast between YHWH who delivers and the tōhû-things that cannot is Samuel's final anti-idolatry warning. The word that described pre-creation emptiness describes the religious alternatives to YHWH.
12.22: 'For YHWH will not abandon his people, for his great name's sake, because it has pleased YHWH to make you a people for himself.' The ground of covenant security is entirely YHWH's self-commitment: not Israel's merit, not the monarchy's strength, but YHWH's own name (šəmô haggāḏôl) and his pleasure (hôʾîl YHWH) in making Israel his people. The double grounding — name and pleasure — is the irreversible foundation. YHWH's reputation is at stake in Israel's survival; he cannot abandon his people without abandoning his own identity.
12.23: 'Moreover, as for me, far be it from me that I should sin against YHWH by ceasing to pray for you, and I will instruct you in the good and the right way.' Samuel's pledge of continued intercession is his covenant commitment: even in retirement from the judgeship, he will not withdraw prayer. To 'cease to pray' (ḥāḏal mippallēl) is declared a sin against YHWH — prayer for the people is a covenant obligation, not an optional pastoral extra. Samuel's identity as prophet-intercessor will persist beyond political power.
12.24: 'Only fear YHWH and serve him faithfully with all your heart, for consider what great things he has done for you.' The closing call synthesizes the entire speech: covenant fear, covenant service, whole-hearted devotion, grounded in grateful memory of YHWH's saving acts. The rāʾû (see/consider) returns to the eyewitness theme — they have just seen YHWH act in thunder and rain; let them see the full scope of his saving history and respond accordingly.
12.25: 'But if you still do wickedly, you shall be swept away, both you and your king.' The speech ends with the curse-conditional in its starkest form — 'swept away' (tîssāpû) — and includes the king in the potential catastrophe. The final verse is the prophecy of the monarchy's potential failure before the monarchy has ruled a year. The book of Samuel will take the rest of its pages demonstrating that this warning was not heeded, and that even the best of Israel's kings — David — falls into the wickedness that leads toward the sweep.

15.1: šālaḥ + māšaḥ — šālaḥ (H7971, sent) and māšaḥ (H4886, anoint) together frame the covenant commission: Samuel acted as YHWH's agent, and Saul's kingship is entirely derived authority. The verse grounds the following command in that original commissioning — Saul's failure to obey is therefore a failure of covenant stewardship, not merely military insubordination.
15.2: pāqad (H6485) — "I remember" translates pāqadtî, but the verb's range includes "attend to, muster, call to account." YHWH is not merely recalling history; he is acting on a standing account. The same verb governs covenant blessing and curse (Exod 20:5). ʾăšer śām lô derek bəʿălōtô mimmiṣrāyim — the relative clause grounds the command in the Exodus narrative: Amalek attacked from behind on the road (Exod 17:8-16; Deut 25:17-19), and that covenantal score is now being settled.
15.3: ḥērem (H2763) — the root appears three times in vv3–9 as the pivotal term. ḥāramtāh means "devote to destruction" — the total consecration of enemy spoil to YHWH, leaving nothing for the victor. The ḥērem is not mere slaughter but a ritual act asserting YHWH's sole ownership of the victory. The list ("man and woman, child and nursing infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey") is total; no exception is authorized. Saul's selective application of it is the crux of ch15.
15.4: Verse reports the muster at Telaim (ṭəlāʾîm). The numerical data (200,000 + 10,000 of Judah separately enumerated) reflects the administrative levy structure; Judah's separate count anticipates the coming split. No Hebrew lexical issue; the verse is narrative preparation.
15.5: wayāreb (H693, from ʾārab) — "set an ambush." The same root governs Amalek's original attack (Exod 17:8 context; Deut 25:18 — Amalek "ambushed" Israel on the road). Saul uses Amalek's own tactical signature against them — the narrative symmetry is probably deliberate.
15.6: ḥesed (H2617) — "loyalty" in the MKT. Saul warns the Kenites away because they "showed loyalty (ḥesed) to all Israel when they came up from Egypt." The Kenites' covenant-loyalty is honored; this is the positive use of ḥesed — covenant-faithfulness that earns protection. The contrast with Saul's own covenant-unfaithfulness in what follows is implicit but pointed.
15.7: Geographic extent: "from Havilah to the approach of Shur, which lies east of Egypt." The breadth signals total victory — the same formula appears in Gen 25:18 for Ishmael's territory. The ḥērem command has been executed on the land; only Agag and the best livestock remain.
15.8: wayyiḥyē with Agag — "He captured Agag alive" (ḥay, living). The specific mention of Agag's survival against the ḥērem command signals the violation immediately after the summary of general compliance. wayyaḥărem (he devoted to destruction) in the second half creates the contrast: the rest received the ḥērem; Agag did not.
15.9: ʾāḇāh (H14) — "refused" (lōʾ ʾāḇû, they would not consent/were unwilling). The verb denotes active refusal, not oversight. The translation notes flag ḥāmal (H2550, spared — "to commiserate, have compassion") applied to Agag and the livestock: the emotion that overrode the command was pity for valuable things. miśmān (fatlings) and ṭōḇ (good/best) are the categories spared. What was nimrāṣ (thin, despised) was devoted. The inversion — keeping the good, destroying the inferior — reveals that the ḥērem was treated as a waste-disposal mechanism, not a sacred act.
15.10: Formulaic prophetic reception: wayhî dəḇar YHWH ʾel šəmûʾēl lēʾmōr — "Then the word of the LORD came to Samuel, saying." The same formula governs the commissioning of prophets (1 Kgs 17:2; Jer 1:4). Its appearance here marks Samuel as the covenant mediator who will pronounce the formal verdict.
15.11:
nāḥam (H5162) — the theologically dense verb: "I regret (niḥamtî) that I made Saul king." The Niphal of nāḥam denotes being moved with sorrow to the point of changing one's course. It is used of YHWH relenting from judgment (Exod 32:14; Jon 3:10) and here of regretting a positive act. The same root appears in v29 and v35 with the opposite claim — that YHWH does not nāḥam. The paradox is not a contradiction but a theological calibration: YHWH genuinely responds to the moral reality of human choice (here, v11), yet his ultimate purposes and character are not subject to reversal (v29). Both truths must be held.

wayyiḥar lišmûʾēl — "Samuel was troubled/angry" (root ḥārāh, H2734, to burn/be kindled). The same word used for YHWH's anger in many texts; Samuel's reaction mirrors the divine response. wayyizʿaq ʾel YHWH kol hallaylāh — he "cried out to YHWH all night" — the intensity is extraordinary; prophetic intercession at its most urgent.
15.12: wayyāṣeḇ lô yād — "he set up a monument for himself" (yād literally "hand," but used for a stele or memorial inscription — cf. 2 Sam 18:18, "Absalom's monument"). The self-erection of a monument before any accounting to Samuel or YHWH characterizes Saul's trajectory: victory is converted into personal honor rather than covenant obedience. Carmel-then-Gilgal: the movement from monument-building to the site of sacrifice shows Saul's priorities reversed.
15.13: bārûḵ ʾattāh laYHWH — Saul's greeting formula ("Blessed are you by the LORD") is a covenant-blessing invocation; its use in the context of disobedience is ironic. hăqîmōtî ʾet dəḇar YHWH — "I have carried out the LORD's command" — the perfect hăqîmōtî claims completed, accomplished action. Samuel's subsequent response demolishes this claim with auditory evidence.
15.14: qôl haṣṣōʾn — "the sound/voice of the sheep." The noun qôl (H6963) means both "sound" and "voice." Samuel's question activates the auditory register that will become central in v22: YHWH values šāmaʿ (hearing-obeying) above sacrifice, and here the sound of the livestock testifies against Saul's claim to have heard.
15.15: Saul deflects by identifying agency: "the people brought them." The shift from "I" (v13) to "the people" (v15) is the grammar of blame-shifting. The rationale offered — "to sacrifice to YHWH your God" — is liturgically framed but the possessive "your God" subtly distances Saul from covenant ownership. The best were spared; the rest destroyed: the same vocabulary as v9.
15.16: wayyōʾmer šəmûʾēl ʾel šāʾûl herpē — "Samuel said, Stop" (herpē, from rāpāh, H7503, to let go, desist). The command to be silent before hearing the prophetic word is itself a posture of submission. Saul's immediate "Speak" (daббēr) is formally compliant but the rest of the chapter shows he is not actually prepared to hear.
15.17: hălōʾ ʾim qāṭōn ʾattāh bəʿênêḵā — "when you were small in your own eyes." qāṭōn (H6996, small/young) coupled with bəʿênêḵā (in your eyes/estimation) — self-assessment of insignificance. Samuel's rebuke reactivates the original condition of Saul's appointment: he was chosen precisely when he did not presume on his own importance (9:21). The contrast with the monument at Carmel (v12) is explicit.
15.18: derek (way/mission) + ḥāram — "sent you on a mission (derek) with an explicit command: Go and completely destroy (ḥāram)." The noun derek (H1870) means both road and mission/way of acting; the whole chapter is about whether Saul followed the derek he was given. derek hāʾămālēqî (the way of the Amalekites) — annihilation was the specific, bounded command.
15.19: lāmmāh lōʾ šāmaʿtā bəqôl YHWH — "Why did you not obey (šāmaʿ) the voice of YHWH?" The verb šāmaʿ (H8085) is both "hear" and "obey" — the two are not separable in Hebrew cognitive grammar. To hear YHWH's voice is to act on it; not acting proves not truly hearing. wattaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH — "you did what was evil in the eyes of YHWH" — the evaluative phrase is the narrative's own verdict formula (cf. Judges passim).
15.20: Saul's second self-defense uses šāmaʿtî (perfect: "I did obey/hear") but the verb is employed to mean only partial compliance. His claim to have "completely destroyed" the Amalekites (ḥāramtî ʾet ʾămālēq) is technically false: Agag lives. The speech demonstrates that Saul uses the covenant vocabulary without its substance.
15.21: wayyiqqaḥ hāʿām miššəlāl — "the people took from the plunder." Saul again attributes agency to "the people" rather than himself. rēʾšît haḥērem — "the choicest of what had been devoted" — the phrase uses rēʾšît (H7225, first/best/choicest), the same word for firstfruits offered to YHWH. The irony: the best of the ḥērem was redirected from its dedicated purpose to a competing sacrificial use.
15.22: šāmaʿ / šemen — Samuel's antithetical couplet: hăḥāpēṣ laYHWH bəʿōlôt ûzəḇāḥîm kišmōaʿ bəqôl YHWH — "Does YHWH delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as in hearing/obeying his voice?" The comparison uses kišmōaʿ (a Qal infinitive construct with prefix kaf: "as in hearing"). The rhetorical question expects "no." šāmaʿ is placed against zābaḥ (sacrifice); then the positive formulation: ṭôḇ šəmōaʿ mizzébaḥ (better to hear/obey than sacrifice) and ləhaqšîḇ méḥēleḇ ʾêlîm (to attend/hearken than the fat of rams). haqšêḇ (H7181, Hiphil of qāšaḇ) denotes attentive listening — the posture of the disciple who gives the teacher full hearing. The verse is not anti-sacrifice but rather establishes that sacrifice without obedience is a category error.
15.23: qesem / ʾāwen / tərāpîm — "rebellion is like the sin of divination (qesem); stubbornness is like wickedness (ʾāwen) and idolatry (tərāpîm)." Qesem (H7081) is a technical term for divination/witchcraft — specifically seeking guidance from spirits rather than YHWH. The comparison is structural: Saul's substitution of his own judgment for YHWH's command is functionally the same as consulting a medium. Tərāpîm (H8655) are household/fertility gods; the parallel equates stubbornness-over-YHWH's-command with idolatry. The key verb māʾastāh (Qal perfect, māʾas H3988, to reject/spurn) — "because you rejected the word of YHWH" — then governs the reversal: the same māʾas now applies to Saul: "he has rejected you as king."
15.24: ḥāṭāʾtî (perfect: "I have sinned") — this is Saul's only explicit confession; the motivation he gives is kî yārēʾtî ʾet hāʿām waʾešmaʿ bəqôlām — "because I feared the people and listened to their voice." The verb šāmaʿ bəqôl (heard/obeyed the voice) echoes v22: Saul šāmaʿ bəqôl hāʿām (obeyed the people's voice) but not šāmaʿ bəqôl YHWH (YHWH's voice). The substitution of voices — people over God — is the lexical center of the chapter.
15.25: śāʾ nāʾ lăḥaṭṭāʾtî — "forgive my sin" (nāśāʾ ḥēṭʾ, literally "carry/lift my sin" — the same idiom behind "bearing sins" in atonement theology; cf. Isa 53:12). Saul asks Samuel to be present at worship — the concern for public honor (kāḇōd) becomes explicit in v30.
15.26: lōʾ ʾāšûḇ ʿimmāḵ — "I will not return/go back with you." šûḇ (H7725, to return/turn) carries covenantal weight — the same root governs repentance and return to YHWH throughout the prophetic corpus. Samuel's refusal to "return" with Saul enacts the verdict: the covenant path has been closed. The grounding: kî māʾastāh ʾet dəḇar YHWH (same as v23) — the rejection is mutual in structure.
15.27: kannap məʿîlô — "the edge/hem of his robe." Kānāp (H3671) means both "wing" and "hem/edge" — the same word Ruth uses in 3:9 when she asks Boaz to "spread his garment" over her (invoking the goel). Here the tearing of the kānāp is an acted oracle. Samuel's məʿîl (H4598, robe/mantle) is the priestly robe of office; the tearing of what Saul grasps signals the tearing of what belongs to the anointed.
15.28: qāraʿ YHWH ʾet mamlᵉḵût yiśrāʾēl mēʿāliḵā hayyôm — "The LORD has torn (qāraʿ) the kingdom of Israel from you today." Qāraʿ (H7167, to tear) echoes the torn robe — the acted sign (v27) becomes the verbal verdict (v28). ûnətānāh lərēʿăḵā haṭṭôḇ mimmēḵā — "and given it to your neighbor who is better than you." Ṭôḇ mimmēḵā (better than you) uses the same ṭôḇ that appeared in vv9 and 21 for the livestock Saul preserved. The contrast is sharp: Saul valued ṭôḇ livestock over the command; now a ṭôḇ man replaces him.
15.29: nēṣaḥ yiśrāʾēl — "the Eternal One of Israel" (nēṣaḥ, H5331 — often "victory, glory, perpetuity"; the MKT's "Eternal One" captures the permanence meaning). lōʾ yəšaqqqer — "does not lie/deceive" (šāqar, H8266). wəlōʾ yinnāḥēm — "does not change his mind" (nāḥam again, same root as v11). The apparent contradiction with v11 (YHWH "regretted") is a theological claim, not an error: the nāḥam of v11 is genuine emotional response to a new moral state; the lōʾ yinnāḥēm of v29 asserts that YHWH's ultimate purposes — unlike human kings — are not subject to manipulation or reversal by human pressure. The verse is spoken precisely to Saul, who is about to appeal for public honor (v30).
15.30: kabbédnî nāʾ — "honor me, please" (kāḇēd, H3513, to be heavy/honored). Saul's concern shifts from sin to reputation: nəged ziqqənê ʿammî ûnəged yiśrāʾēl (before the elders of my people and before Israel). The word kāḇōd (honor/glory) governs Saul's final concern; what he fears is loss of face, not loss of covenant standing. Samuel complies — not because the verdict has changed but to prevent disorder.
15.31: The narrative is laconic: wayyiśtaḥû šāʾûl laYHWH — "Saul worshipped the LORD." The single verb records the act without endorsement; Samuel's presence does not restore the relationship. The reader is meant to note the irony: Saul performs the worship he claimed to be preserving the livestock for.
15.32: maʿădannōt — "hesitantly" (MT ʿdnt, variously read as "with careful steps" or "in chains" — the text is difficult; Targum has "in chains," LXX "trembling"). The MKT chooses "hesitantly." Agag's words ʾāḵēn sār mar hammāwet — "surely the bitter agony of death has passed" — are ambiguous: he may believe Samuel intends to spare him (ironic confidence), or he may be expressing relief that his captivity spared him death in battle. Either reading makes his execution in the next verse more abrupt.
15.33: šikkəlāh nashîm ḥarbaḵā — "your sword has made women childless (šikkēl, H7921)." Samuel's pronouncement is lex talionis applied to royal scale: the verb šikkēl (to bereave of children) frames the execution as proportional justice, not arbitrary violence. wayəšassēp šəmûʾēl ʾet ʾăḡāḡ — "Samuel cut Agag down" (šāsap, H8158 — a hapax; probably "to hew to pieces"). The act is described as happening "before YHWH" (lipnê YHWH) at Gilgal — it is a ritual completion of the ḥērem, not a judicial execution.
15.34: The parting of ways: Samuel returns to Ramah; Saul to Gibeah. The geography underscores permanent separation — Samuel never comes to Gibeah (Saul's base), and Saul never comes to Ramah (Samuel's home). The covenant mediator and the failed king now exist in entirely separate spheres.
15.35: nāḥam (third occurrence) — wəNIHYAH niḥam YHWH kî himliḵ ʾet šāʾûl ʿal yiśrāʾēl — "The LORD regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel." The verb closes the chapter as it opened it (v11). Samuel's grief (ʾābal, to mourn) mirrors YHWH's nāḥam; prophet and God share the same emotional response to Saul's failure. The chapter has moved from appointment (v1) to irreversible rejection (v35), and the bookend of nāḥam holds the arc together.

16.1: ʿad māṯay ʾattāh miṯʾabbēl ʾel šāʾûl — "How long will you mourn (ʾābal) for Saul?" The question is not reproach but a call to transition: grief is appropriate, but YHWH has moved on (mēʾastîhû, perfect — the rejection is complete and in the past). mallēʾ qarnəḵā šāmen — "fill your horn with oil" — the horn of anointing oil is the instrument of covenant installation; the command to fill it is the command to begin the new administration. bāḥartî bānāyw — "I have chosen among his sons" — bāḥar (H977, to choose/elect) is the covenant-election verb used of Israel (Deut 7:6) and of David consistently in later texts (Ps 89:3).
16.2: ʾêḵ ʾēlēḵ wəšāmaʿ šāʾûl wəhăraḡānî — "How can I go? If Saul hears (šāmaʿ), he will kill me." Samuel's fear activates the šāmaʿ vocabulary again: Saul's hearing is dangerous. YHWH's answer is a concealment strategy — take a sacrifice. The practical accommodation of prophetic mission to political danger is notable: YHWH does not override the danger but provides a cover.
16.3: ûmāšaḥtāh lî ʾēt ʾăšer ʾōmar ʾēlêḵā — "you are to anoint for me (lî) the one I indicate to you." The emphatic lî (for me / on my behalf) underscores that the anointing is YHWH's act performed through Samuel. The verb ʾāmar (to say/indicate) will be enacted in v12 — YHWH's speech-act identifies the individual.
16.4: wayyeḥerdû ziqnê hāʿîr liqrāʾtô — "the elders of the town came trembling (ḥārad, H2729) to meet him." Ḥārad denotes trembling before what is awesome or dangerous — used of theophany encounters (Exod 19:16) and of the fear provoked by unexpected authority. Samuel's arrival provokes the same awe as a divine appearance; his prophetic office carries that weight.
16.5: hitqaddəšû ûḇāʾtem ʾittî bazzāḇaḥ — "purify yourselves and come with me to the sacrifice." Qāddēš (Hithpael, to consecrate/purify oneself) is the ritual preparation for holy encounter — washing garments and abstaining (cf. Exod 19:10, 14). Samuel "consecrated" (wayyəqaddēš) Jesse and his sons specifically — a particular preparation beyond the communal purification, suggesting the sons are entering an especially sacred role.
16.6: wayyōʾmer šəmûʾēl ʾak nəged YHWH məšîḥô — "Samuel thought: surely YHWH's anointed stands before him." Nāḡad (Niphal, H5048 — stand before, in the presence of) — Samuel's internal judgment uses "before him" (nəgdô) as if YHWH is already indicating Eliab. The assumption is based on physical stature — the same error that originally made Saul plausible (9:2). The word māšîaḥ (H4899, anointed one) here is used prospectively for whoever YHWH will designate; Samuel's pre-emptive use of it for Eliab is immediately corrected.
16.7:
lēḇ (H3820) — the chapter's central verse: kî lōʾ ʾăšer yirʾeh hāʾādām kî hāʾādām yirʾeh lāʿênāyim waYHWH yirʾeh lallēḇ — "for man sees what is visible to the eyes, but YHWH sees the heart (lēḇ)." The antithesis is structural: hāʾādām/YHWH, ʿênāyim (eyes, external) / lēḇ (heart, internal). Lēḇ in Hebrew psychology encompasses intellect, will, and moral orientation — not just emotion. YHWH's perception penetrates to the seat of character; human sight is limited to the external. The principle governs both David's selection and the entire theology of covenantal fidelity: what qualifies for election is not external advantage but the orientation of the inner self toward YHWH.

wayqahàr (Qal imperfect with waw: "and the LORD looked at") — the verb rāʾāh (to see) is used for both sides, but YHWH's seeing penetrates where human seeing cannot. The verse also indirectly critiques Saul's appointment logic: Israel asked for a king who looked the part; YHWH now explicitly overrides that criterion.
16.8: wayqarāʾ yiššay ʾel ʾăḇînādāḇ — Jesse presents seven sons in sequence. The verbal pattern is formulaic: each son passes before Samuel, Samuel says "the LORD has not chosen this one" (lōʾ ḇāḥar YHWH bāzeh), and the sequence continues. The repetition enacts the progressive elimination; each use of bāḥar (choose) anticipates the positive use in v12.
16.9: The same formula as v8 — šammāh (Shammah) passes, and lōʾ ḇāḥar YHWH bāzeh. The sequential rejection of Jesse's sons by name emphasizes that YHWH's election is not predictable by birth order, physical presence, or family standing — the first seven are each individually considered and passed over.
16.10: šibʿāh ḇānāyw — "seven of his sons." Seven is the number of completion; that all seven pass before Samuel without divine selection sets up the reversal: the anointed is outside the expected frame entirely, absent from the formal presentation, tending sheep.
16.11: haqqāṭān — "the youngest" (qāṭān, H6996 — same word used in 15:17 of Saul's original self-assessment: "when you were small in your own eyes"). The inversion is pointed: Saul was small and was made great; David is literally the smallest/youngest and will be chosen. wəhinnēh rōʿeh baṣṣōʾn — "he is tending the sheep (rāʿāh)" — the shepherd-image here is biographical; it will become the dominant metaphor for David's kingship (Ps 78:70-72; 2 Sam 5:2).
16.12: ʾadmōnî ʿim yəpê ʿênāyim wəṭôḇ rōʾî — "ruddy, with beautiful eyes and handsome in appearance." ʾAdmōnî (H132, ruddy/reddish) is mentioned again in 17:42. The physical description comes after the theological principle of v7 — the narrator is not contradicting v7 but signaling that David's appearance is secondary to the divine word that follows. wayōʾmer YHWH qûm mošḥēhû kî zeh hûʾ — "YHWH said: Rise and anoint him, for this is the one" (zeh hûʾ). The divine word is monosyllabic and definitive: "this — he." No explanation of the inner qualities that qualified him; the election is pure divine prerogative.
16.13: rûaḥ YHWH + ṣālaḥ — "the Spirit of the LORD came powerfully upon (ṣālaḥ, H6743) David from that day onward." Ṣālaḥ denotes effective, sudden action — the same root was used of the rûaḥ coming upon Saul (10:6, 10) and Samson (Judg 14:6, 19; 15:14). The force is invasive and transformative. The phrase mēhayyôm hahûʾ wāmāʿlāh (from that day onward) marks a permanent installation, not a transient experience — contrasted with v14 where the rûaḥ "departed (sûr) from Saul." Māšaḥ bəqéreḇ ʾeḥāyw — "anointed in the midst of his brothers" — the private ceremony before witnesses; public installation comes later (2 Sam 2:4; 5:3).
16.14: sûr / rûaḥ rāʿāh — "the Spirit of the LORD departed (sûr, H5493) from Saul, and an evil/harmful spirit from the LORD tormented him." Sûr is the verb of departure/removal — the same root used when the ark was removed (4:21: gālāh kāḇôd, glory departed). The divine empowerment that Saul received at 10:10 is formally withdrawn. Rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH — "a harmful spirit from the LORD" — the text does not identify this as a demonic being in later NT taxonomy; it is an affliction that YHWH permits/sends as part of the judicial withdrawal of protective presence. The juxtaposition of vv13-14 is deliberate: the rûaḥ moves from Saul to David in the same narrative moment.
16.15: Saul's servants explain the situation using the same vocabulary: rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh mǝḇaʿǝtteḵā — "an evil spirit from God is troubling you" (bāʿat, H1204, to terrify/trouble). The servants recognize a theological reality — the spirit is from God — while seeking a practical remedy in music. The therapeutic use of music connects to the tradition of prophetic music (10:5) and the Psalms.
16.16: yôdēaʿ mənaggen — "skilled/knowing in playing" (yādaʿ + nāgan). Yādaʿ (H3045, to know) with a skill denotes professional competence — the person who "knows how" to do something. Nāgan (H5059, to play a stringed instrument) — the lyre/kinnor. The servants propose a musical-therapeutic intervention; the logic connects physical performance to spiritual relief.
16.17: rəʾû nāʾ lî ʾîš mêṭîḇ lənaggen — "find me a man who plays well" (literally "who does good in playing" — mêṭîḇ from ṭôḇ). The word ṭôḇ reappears: the man who is ṭôḇ at music will enter where the man the people wanted (tall, impressive) failed.
16.18: rāʾîtî bēn lǝyiššay bêt hallaḥmî yōdēaʿ nāḡēn — "I know of a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite, skilled in playing." The servant's description of David is striking in its fullness: skilled in music, brave (gibbôr ḥayil), man of war (ʾîš milḥāmāh), prudent in speech (nāḇôn dāḇār), handsome (ʾîš tōʾar) — and above all, waYHWH ʿimmô — "the LORD is with him." The climactic phrase "YHWH is with him" is the theological summary that will recur throughout the David narrative (18:12, 14, 28). In contrast to the rûaḥ having departed from Saul, David's defining characteristic is divine presence.
16.19: šilḥû ʾēlay ʾet dāwid binkā hārōʿeh baṣṣōʾn — "send me your son David, who tends the sheep." The royal summons still identifies David by his occupation — shepherd. The king who will replace Saul enters his orbit as a servant.
16.20: Jesse's gift: bread, wine, a young goat — the standard hospitality offering for a patron. The donkey as carrier signals the gift's weight and distance. David goes with the gift — as servant delivering tribute, not as heir presenting credentials.
16.21: wayʾahāḇēhû šāʾûl məʾōd — "Saul loved him greatly (ʾāhab + məʾōd)." The intensity of the affection is recorded; it will intensify into lethal hatred. wayhî lô nōśēʾ kēlîm — "and became his armor-bearer." The armor-bearer role (nōśēʾ kēlîm) is one of high personal trust — the man who carries and hands the weapons of the king in battle.
16.22: yaqōm nāʾ dāwid lǝpānay — "let David remain in my service" (literally "stand before me" — ʿāmad lǝpānāyw, the idiom of court attendance). Saul requests David specifically, and Jesse complies. The servant of Saul is the chosen of YHWH; the narrative tension of the next fifteen chapters is embedded in this single verse.
16.23: kəšeḇet rûaḥ hāʾĕlōhîm ʾel šāʾûl ... lāqaḥ dāwid ʾet hakkinnôr wəniggēn bəyādô — "whenever the harmful spirit from God came upon Saul, David took the lyre and played." The temporal clause kəšeḇet (whenever/each time) marks a recurring pattern. Kinnôr (H3658, lyre/harp) is the instrument associated with worship and praise throughout the psalmic tradition — David's instrument. The therapeutic sequence: David plays → Saul is relieved → the harmful spirit departs. The verse closes ch16 by showing David already functioning as the agent of Saul's relief — the anointed king ministering to his predecessor who does not yet know who David truly is.

17.4: Goliath is introduced as ʾîš habênayim — 'man of the between-two-spaces' — the technical term for a single combat champion who stands between the two armies to settle the battle by representative duel. The bênayim (the space between) is the no-man's land where the champions meet; the champion's defeat ends the battle. This is the ANE institution of monomachia — individual combat as proxy warfare. The irony of the narrative is that Israel has a champion — YHWH — who dwells in the bênayim between heaven and earth, whose representative David will enter the space as YHWH's proxy. Goliath is the embodiment of all that looks like an unsurmountable adversary: six cubits and a span tall, armored in bronze, with a spear like a weaver's beam. The narrative establishes the appearance of insurmountability before the reversal.
17.26: David's first question — mî haʿārēl happəlištî hazzeh kî ḥērēp maʿarəḵôt ʾelōhîm ḥayyîm — 'Who is this uncircumcised Philistine that he should defy the armies of the living God?' — introduces the key theological term ḥerpāh (reproach, defiance, shame). Goliath's challenge is not merely military; it is a covenant-theological defiance: he stands against ʾelōhîm ḥayyîm (the living God), which David will address directly in v45-46. The term ḥerpāh (shame/reproach) appears in Ps 22:6 — 'I am a reproach (ḥerpāh) to men' — where the Davidic king takes on the shame that Goliath levied. The one who defeats the shame-levying giant in ch17 will himself bear shame in Ps 22 — the trajectory from victorious champion to suffering servant in the Davidic typology.
17.45: David's declaration of battle terms is a liturgical counter to Goliath's military credentials: ʾattāh bāʾ ʾēlay bəḥereḇ ûḇaḥănît ûḇəḵîḏôn wəʾānōḵî bāʾ ʾēleyḵā bəšēm YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt — 'You come to me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come to you in the name of YHWH of armies.' The contrast is between weaponry (ḥereḇ, ḥănît, kîḏôn = sword, spear, javelin — three weapons for Goliath's three-fold armament) and the šēm YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt (the name of YHWH of armies). The name (šēm) is the representative presence of YHWH — invoked in battle as the living divine warrior (cf. Ps 20:5 — 'in the name of our God we will set up our banners'). YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt (YHWH of armies) is the divine warrior title par excellence; David is fighting as YHWH's champion, and the šēm is his weapon.
17.47: David's theological summary of the battle: wəyēḏᵉʿû kol haqqāhāl hazzeh kî lōʾ bəḥereḇ ûḇaḥănît yôšîaʿ YHWH kî laYHWH hammilḥāmāh — 'all this assembly will know that not by sword or spear does YHWH save; for the battle belongs to YHWH.' The phrase laYHWH hammilḥāmāh (to YHWH belongs the battle) is the most compressed statement of holy war theology in the Samuel narratives. It echoes Moses at the Red Sea (Exod 14:13-14 — 'Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH... YHWH will fight for you') and Jehoshaphat's battle prayer (2 Chr 20:15 — 'the battle is not yours but God's'). The purpose of the victory is explicitly missional: 'that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel' (v46) — YHWH's glory among the nations as the goal of the anointed's battle.
17.49: The sling-stone strikes Goliath in the forehead (miṣḥô): wayyitbāʿ hāʾeḇen bᵉmiṣḥô wayyipōl ʿal pānāyw ʾārṣāh — 'the stone sank into his forehead, and he fell on his face to the ground.' The blow to the head is the Gen 3:15 vocabulary enacted: rōʾš (head) — 'he shall crush your head' (yəšûpəḵā rōʾš). The serpent's-head-crushing prophecy finds one of its clearest pre-Christ actualizations here: the seed of the woman (the young shepherd, the least expected champion) strikes the representative of all forces arrayed against YHWH's people through the head. The forehead strike is not incidental but structurally significant — it is the narrative's way of gesturing toward Gen 3:15 while pointing beyond all human champion-combatants to the one who will finally crush the serpent.
17.51: David takes Goliath's own sword and cuts off his head: wayyiqaḥ dāwiḏ ʾet ḥarbô wayyišlᵉpāh mittaʿrāh wayyᵉmōtəṯēhû wayyiḵrat bāh ʾet rōʾšô — 'David took his sword and drew it from its sheath and killed him and cut off his head with it.' The use of the enemy's own weapon to execute the final blow is the book's ironic statement: the armament that was supposed to terrify Israel becomes the instrument of Goliath's destruction. This is the NT pattern for Christ's victory over death: 'that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death' (Heb 2:14) — the weapon of the adversary (death) becomes the mechanism of the adversary's defeat. David takes the giant's head to Jerusalem (v54), presaging the future capital where the Davidic line will reign and ultimately where the serpent's head will be crushed once for all.
17.1: wayyiqbəṣû pəlištîm ʾeṯ-maḥănêhem lammiḥlāmāh — 'gathered their camps/forces for battle.' The term maḥăneh (camp, army) is both the military encampment and the assembled force within it; the MKT 'forces' captures the military sense. Socoh (Khirbet Abbad, c.21km WSW of Bethlehem) is in the Shephelah foothills — the contested buffer zone between Philistia and Judah. The Philistines are staging in Israelite territory, forcing Israel to respond defensively.
17.2: ʾēmeq hāʾēlāh — 'Valley of the Terebinth' (MKT: 'Valley of Elah'). The name derives from the ʾēlāh tree (terebinth or oak). The site is identified with Wadi es-Sant, where a dry streambed provided the smooth stones David would select (v40). The two armies deploy on opposite hills with the valley between — a standard ANE tactical standoff waiting for an individual champion or breakthrough.
17.3: The spatial geometry — Philistines on one hill, Israel on another, haggayʾ bênêhem ('the valley between them') — establishes the impasse that Goliath's challenge is designed to break. In ANE warfare, champion combat was an accepted device for avoiding mass casualties; the text presents this as a genuine Philistine proposal, not mere bravado. The valley functions as a no-man's-land until David crosses it (v48).
17.5: qoḇaʿ nəḥōšeṯ ʿal-rōʾšô wəširiôn qaśqaśîm hûʾ lāḇûš — bronze helmet and scale armor. The širiôn qaśqaśîm ('coat of scale armor,' lit. 'armor of scales') is the laminar bronze plate-suit documented in ANE archaeology and known from Ugaritic and Egyptian texts; the 5,000-shekel weight (~57kg) is plausible for full body-armor. The catalogue of Goliath's equipment (vv5–7) follows the literary convention of ekphrasis — detailed description establishing the champion's invincibility before the reversal.
17.6: kîdôn nəḥōšeṯ bên kētēpāyw — 'a javelin of bronze between his shoulders.' The term kîdôn appears elsewhere as a short lance or dart (Josh 8:18; Job 39:23); its placement 'between his shoulders' (slung on the back) indicates a third projectile weapon in addition to the spear and sword. Goliath is equipped for every combat range: javelin for distance, spear for mid-range, sword for close combat. The asymmetry with David's sling (v40) is the narrative's central irony.
17.7: wəḥēṣ ḥaniṯô kəmənôr ʾōrəgîm — 'the shaft of his spear was like a weaver's beam.' The comparison to a weaver's beam (the horizontal roller on a loom, typically 5–7cm thick) communicates mass rather than size. The iron point weighing 600 shekels (~6.8kg) represents high-grade iron weaponry; the Philistines had metallurgical superiority in iron at this period (1 Sam 13:19–22). The shield-bearer walking ahead completes the picture of a fully-serviced war machine.
17.8: ʿibrîm ʾattem ʿăḇādîm ləšāʾûl — 'you are Hebrews, servants of Saul.' Goliath's use of ʿibrîm ('Hebrews') rather than 'Israel' is the outsider's ethnic label, associated in Samuel with Philistine usage and carrying social-inferior connotations (cf. 1 Sam 4:6, 9; 13:3, 7, 19). The legal framework of the challenge — loser's side becomes servants (ʿăḇādîm) of the winner — formalises the combat as a binding covenant-substitution.
17.9: The conditional structure of the challenge — 'if he can kill me, we serve you; if I kill him, you serve us' — is legally precise. The verb wəʿăḇaḏtem ʾōṯānû ('you shall serve us') carries the full weight of tributary servitude. The Philistine champions's challenge inverts the covenant structure: Israel's God should give them victory; instead they must produce a man who can match Goliath. The narrative will resolve by collapsing the human champion structure through YHWH's intervention via David.
17.10: ḥēraptî ʾeṯ-maʿarəḵôṯ yiśrāʾēl — 'I have defied/taunted the battle lines of Israel.' The verb ḥārap (Piel: to taunt, revile, reproach) recurs as the chapter's theological key: Goliath is not merely challenging individual combat but insulting maʿarəḵôṯ ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm (v26, 'the battle lines of the living God'). David will reframe Goliath's challenge as an insult against YHWH personally (vv45–46), transforming individual combat into a theological contest.
17.11: wayyišməʿ šāʾûl wəkol-yiśrāʾēl ʾeṯ-dib·rê happəlištî hāʾēlleh wayyēḥattû wayyirəʾû məʾōḏ — they were 'dismayed' (ḥāṯaṯ) and 'greatly afraid.' ḥāṯaṯ is the technical term for the psychological collapse of military courage (Deut 1:21; Josh 1:9; Isa 8:9); it is the fear that undoes battle readiness. Saul himself, anointed king to lead Israel's armies (1 Sam 8:20), is paralysed alongside his troops — an indictment of the existing leadership framework.
17.12: The narrator introduces David's genealogy at the moment of Israel's crisis — a narrative device signalling that the solution to the impasse has already been prepared. ʾepraṯî mibbêṯ leḥem yəhûḏāh ('an Ephrathite from Bethlehem of Judah') is the full ethnic-geographical identification; ʾepraṯî designates the Bethlehemite clan (Ruth 1:2; Mic 5:2). Jesse's advanced age and eight sons establishes the family context that will make David's appearance at the front unexpected.
17.13: The three eldest sons — Eliab, Abinadab, Shammah — are named and identified with Saul's army. Their presence at the front and David's absence sets up the contrast: David is not a soldier but a shepherd, which is precisely the background that qualifies him for what follows (v34). The naming of the three eldest also establishes Eliab as the eldest who had seemed to Samuel the obvious choice (16:6–7) but was rejected. His reappearance in v28 will show his character.
17.14: wədāwid hûʾ haqqāṭōn — 'David was the youngest (qāṭōn).' The adjective qāṭōn (small, youngest, least significant) is precisely the category YHWH overturns in 1 Samuel's theology (16:7, 11; cf. 9:21 — Saul's own initial self-deprecation). The youngest son sent to provision his brothers recapitulates the Joseph pattern (Gen 37:13–14) and points toward the reversal the narrative is constructing.
17.15: wədāwid hōlēk wāšāb mēʿal šāʾûl — 'David went back and forth from Saul to tend his father's flock.' The verb hōlēk wāšāb (going and returning) describes a commuter pattern; David has a dual role as court musician (16:18–23) and pastoral shepherd. This verse is sometimes cited in the textual discussion about the apparent narrative tension between ch16 and ch17; the MKT renders it naturally as a continuing pattern of alternating service.
17.16: wayyaškem happəlištî wayyaʿărōḇ baqqer ûḇāʿereḇ — the Philistine 'rose early and presented himself morning and evening for forty days.' The twice-daily challenge ('morning and evening') mirrors the twice-daily tamid offering in the tabernacle (Ex 29:38–42) — a dark parody of liturgical regularity. Forty days is the standard biblical period of testing (Num 13:25; Jonah 3:4; Matt 4:2). The challenge has liturgical rhythm but no covenantal content.
17.17: Jesse's errand — ʾēpāh qālûy ʾeśer leḥem ('an ephah of roasted grain and ten loaves') — is a practical supply run, not a heroic mission. The provisions are standard field rations: roasted grain (qālî) was portable, shelf-stable army food (Ruth 2:14; 2 Sam 17:28). Ten cuts of cheese (ḥaṣar ḥĕlāḇîm) go to the unit commander — small gifts of honour. Jesse's errand positions David at the battle line without any military mandate, making his intervention entirely contingent.
17.18: wəʾeṯ-ʾaḥêkā tifqōḏ ləšālôm wəʾeṯ-ʿarūbbāṯām tiqkāḥ — 'check on your brothers' welfare (šālôm) and bring back some token from them.' The word ʿǎrubbāh (pledge, token — given as security) is unusual here; some read it as 'word/news' (emending slightly). The MKT 'token' captures the sense of tangible evidence that David reached the brothers safely. Jesse's concern is fraternal welfare, not military intelligence.
17.19: The narrative locates Jesse's sons and 'all the men of Israel' in the Valley of Elah 'fighting (nilḥāmîm) the Philistines.' The participle nilḥāmîm is technically a stalemate — they are 'engaged in battle' without resolution. The passive standoff (armies drawn up but no engagement, v21) is where David arrives. The narrator's vocabulary holds two realities: formally there is a war, practically there is a 40-day paralysis.
17.20: wayyaškēm dāwid babbōqer wayyaʿazeḇ ʾeṯ-haṣṣōʾn ʿal yad šômēr — David 'rose early in the morning and left the flock with a keeper.' Early rising (wayyaškēm) is the mark of diligence throughout the Samuel narrative; leaving the sheep properly attended rather than abandoned shows pastoral responsibility. His arrival at the camp happens to coincide with the battle array drawing up (v21) — the narrator frames the coincidence as providential.
17.21: wayyaʿarəḵû yiśrāʾēl ûpəlištîm maʿarāḵāh liḳrāṯ maʿarāḵāh — 'Israel and the Philistines drew up battle formation facing each other.' The repetition of maʿarāḵāh ('battle formation, array, line') frames the scene as pure standoff: two battle lines facing each other with no one willing to move. David arrives precisely at the moment of maximum tension and minimum action — when the impasse is most visible and most humiliating.
17.22: wayyaʿazeḇ dāwid ʾeṯ-kēlāyw ʿal yad šômēr hakkēlîm — 'David left his gear with the keeper of equipment.' The transition from errand-boy to soldier is marked by this administrative detail: David formally checks his supplies with the supply officer before running to the battle line to greet his brothers. The mundane logistics grounds the heroic narrative.
17.23: Goliath's appearance and repetition of his challenge (kaddəḇārîm hāʾēlleh, 'those same words') is the narrative trigger. David hears the challenge for the first time at the same moment Goliath delivers it; the repetition for the reader (hearing it the second time) is David's first encounter. His response will be immediate and theologically formed — unlike the forty-day paralysis of the army.
17.24: wayyānusû mippānāyw wayyirəʾû məʾōḏ — 'they fled from before him and were very afraid.' The verb nûs (to flee, to run away in fear) describes actual retreat from Goliath's advance, not merely standing their ground in fear. This amplifies the impasse: Goliath can actually advance and make Israel run, yet the formal battle has not begun. The daily intimidation has become a rehearsal of defeat.
17.25: wəhāyāh hāʾîš ʾăšer yakkennû yaʿăśernû hammelek ʿāšer gādôl — 'the king will enrich the man who kills him greatly.' The reward structure — wealth (ʿāšer), the king's daughter, and freedom for his father's house from tax/tribute — is the ANE hero's reward. The detail of freedom from ḥopšî ('freed household,' possibly exemption from corvée or tribute) adds legal specificity. David's question about this reward in v26 shows he is listening to the political economy, not just the military challenge.
17.27: The men repeat the reward terms to David: kazzeh yaʿăśeh lāʾîš ʾăšer yakkennû ('this is what will be done for the man who kills him'). The passive construction ('will be done') implies official royal promise; David's inquiry about it in v26 and its repetition here establishes the formal covenantal framework of the reward before David acts. He is not acting from naivety but from full knowledge of the terms.
17.28: wayyiḥar ʾap-ʾĕlîāḇ bədāwid — 'Eliab's anger burned against David.' ḥārāh ʾap (lit. 'the nose burned') is the standard Hebrew idiom for fierce anger. Eliab's accusation — pəḥazûṯ libbəkā ('the presumption/insolence of your heart') — is exactly the false reading: the reader knows David's heart has been examined by YHWH (16:7) and found worthy. The eldest brother's dismissal is the final repetition of the 16:7 theme — human sight judges wrongly.
17.29: māh ʿāśîṯî ʿattāh halôʾ dāḇār hûʾ — 'What have I done now? Is it not just talk/a word?' David's response is understated and rhetorical. dāḇār here means a matter worth inquiring about; the question deflects Eliab's attack without engaging it. The brevity is telling: David doesn't defend himself but moves on (v30). The contrast with Eliab's heated accusation underlines David's equanimity.
17.30: wayyissōḇ mēʾeṣlô ʾel-mul ʾaḥēr — 'he turned away from him to another.' The verb sāḇaḇ (to turn, go around) is physical: David simply walks away from Eliab to ask someone else. The same question produces the same answer; the repetition confirms the reward is publicly known. David's persistence is not stubbornness but methodical information-gathering before action.
17.31: wayiggāḏû liḵənê šāʾûl — 'what David had said was reported to Saul.' The passive report (wayyaggēḏ, Hiphil, 'was told') shifts the narrative momentum: David's words have now reached the king. Saul's summons (wayyišlaḥ lô, 'he sent for him') sets up the confrontation that will expose Saul's paralysis and provide the opportunity for David's intervention. The passive construction distances David from any self-promotion.
17.32: ʾal-yippōl lēḇ-ʾādām ʿālāyw — 'let no man's heart fall/fail because of him.' nāpal lēḇ ('heart falls') is the idiom for loss of courage; David is directly addressing the condition described in v11 (ḥāṯaṯ). His self-identification as 'your servant' (ʿaḇdəḵā) is the appropriate court register; his offer to fight the Philistine frames the action as royal service, not personal glory.
17.33: lōʾ ṯûḵal lāleḵeṯ — 'you are not able to go.' Saul's objection — nəʿar ʾattāh ('you are a young man/boy') and Goliath is a warrior since his youth — names the obvious disproportion. The term nəʿar (youth, boy, servant) can mean anything from infant to military aide; here it emphasises inexperience. Saul's reason is accurate from a purely human perspective; David's response (vv34–37) will reframe the evidence base.
17.34: ḥōrēh ʾet-ṣōʾn ʾāḇîw — 'tending his father's flock.' David begins his argument with pastoral biography, not military credentials. The encounter with lion and bear (ʾaryēh wəhaḏḏōḇ) establishes a track record of individual combat against superior force — precisely the scenario Goliath presents. The key word is wəhiṣṣaḷtî (Hiphil of nāṣal, 'I rescued'), which David will use again of YHWH in v37.
17.35: wəhiḵîṯîhû wəhiṣṣaḷtî miqqî — 'I struck it and rescued [the lamb] from its mouth.' The verbs (nākāh, to strike; nāṣal, to rescue) form David's combat vocabulary: he attacks the predator and recovers what was taken. ûḇəqûmô ʿālay wəheḥezaqtî bizeʿqānô ('when it rose against me, I seized it by its beard') describes hand-to-hand wrestling with a lion — the beard of a lion is not a natural fighting target but demonstrates extreme close-range mastery.
17.36: wəhāyāh hapaləšṯî hāʿārēl hazzeh kəʾaḥaḏ mēhem — 'this uncircumcised Philistine will be like one of them.' David's use of ʿārēl ('uncircumcised') to describe Goliath reframes the combat in covenant terms: circumcision is the sign of the covenant community (Gen 17:11); an uncircumcised man is outside the covenant, therefore outside YHWH's protection. The same word recurs in v26 — David uses it consistently, not as an insult but as a theological categorisation.
17.37: yhwh ʾăšer hiṣṣilani — 'YHWH who rescued me.' David's conclusion draws the explicit theological inference from his biographical evidence: the rescuing agent in the lion-bear encounters was YHWH, not his own skill. yaṣṣilēnî miqqāṯ hapəlištî hazzeh — 'he will rescue me from the hand of this Philistine.' The same verb (nāṣal) links past pastoral rescue to anticipated military rescue; Saul's response ('Go! YHWH be with you') is a blessing that implies his acceptance of David's theological case.
17.38: wayyalbiš šāʾûl ʾeṯ-dāwid maḏ·dāyw — 'Saul dressed David in his own military garments.' The attempt to armour David in Saul's equipment is symbolically charged: Saul is trying to make David a smaller version of himself, a warrior equipped in the same military framework that has failed. qôḇaʿ nəḥōšeṯ ʿal-rōʾšô ('a bronze helmet on his head') echoes Goliath's armour description (v5) — David is being made into a miniature Goliath.
17.39: waylōʾ yāḵōl lahalōḵ kî lōʾ nissāh — 'he could not walk, for he had not tested them.' The verb nāsāh (to test, try, prove) is the same root used for YHWH testing Abraham (Gen 22:1) and Israel's wilderness testing. David had not been tested in this military framework — it is not his. His refusal of the armour and return to his shepherd's staff and sling is the rejection of institutional warfare in favour of the only combat mode he has actually proved.
17.40: wayyiqkāḥ maqqəlô bəyāḏô wayyiḇḥar-lô ḥămîššāh ʾaḇnê-ḥālaqqôṯ min-hannaḥal — 'five smooth stones from the brook.' The detail of five stones (for one shot) has generated much comment; the LXX reads the same. The practical reason is probably the insurance of multiple attempts; the theological point is that one was sufficient (v49). The shepherd's pouch (yalqûṭ hārōʿîm) and sling (qelaʿ) are tools of his real vocation — the ones YHWH has already validated in the field.
17.41: wōlēḵ ûmaggēn lōʾ lippānāyw — Goliath coming forward 'with his shield-bearer walking before him.' The shield-bearer's function is battlefield protection from missiles — exactly what David intends to use. The shield-bearer walks lippānāyw ('before him'), unwittingly positioning himself to screen Goliath from the front while David approaches. The sling projectile from a distance would not be screened by a man-held shield in any case.
17.42: wayyibbet happəlištî wayyirʾeh ʾeṯ-dāwid wayyibzēhû — 'looked... and despised him.' The verb bāzāh (to despise, hold in contempt) is significant: Goliath's evaluation of David follows the same logic Eliab used — looking at outward appearance. naʿar ('youth'), ʾaḏmōnî ('ruddy'), yəpēh marʾeh ('handsome') are the visible markers of inexperience. YHWH had looked at the same outward description in 16:12 and chosen differently.
17.43: hakeleḇ ʾānōḵî kî-ʾattāh bāʾ ʾēlay bammaqqəlôṯ — 'Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks?' The dog-insult is classic ANE combat rhetoric; a keleḇ ('dog') is a contemptible, scavenging animal. Goliath reads the shepherd's staff (maqqēl) as a stick weapon appropriate for a dog-fight, not honourable combat. He curses David 'by his gods' (ʾĕlōhāyw) — invoking Philistine divine authority. The curse by Philistine gods will be answered by YHWH's name (v45).
17.44: ləḵāh ʾēlay wəʾetənāh ʾeṯ-bəśārəḵā — 'come to me, and I will give your flesh.' Goliath's counter-threat is the mirror of the battlefield consecration formula: the loser's flesh is given to scavenging birds and beasts (ʿôp haššāmayim ûlḇehemōṯ haśśāḏeh). David will use the same language about Goliath and the Philistine army in v46 — the verbal duel matches the physical one, with David turning Goliath's threat back on him.
17.46: wənāṯattî ʾeṯ-pigrê maḥănēh pəlištîm hayyôm hazzeh — 'I will give the corpses of the Philistine camp this day.' David's proclamation expands Goliath's individual-combat threat into a battle declaration: the entire army will fall. The purpose clause — wəyēḏəʿû kol-hāʾāreṣ kî yēš ʾĕlōhîm ləyiśrāʾēl ('that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel') — gives the battle cosmic-apologetic significance. Victory will be theological demonstration, not military achievement.
17.48: wayyāqom dāwid wayyāroṣ hammaʿarāḵāh liḳrāṯ happəlištî — 'David ran quickly toward the battle line to meet the Philistine.' The verb rûṣ (to run) is notable: David initiates the movement, running toward Goliath rather than waiting. This is the opposite of Israel's reaction in v24 (nûs, to flee). The contrast in verbs of movement — flee vs. run toward — is the chapter's climactic physical action before the sling-shot.
17.50: wayyeḥezaq dāwid min-happəlištî baqqelaʿ ûḇāʾeḇen — 'David prevailed over the Philistine with sling and stone.' The narrator's summary deliberately notes that David 'struck the Philistine and killed him, and there was no sword in David's hand.' The absence of a sword is the point of the whole sequence: conventional weaponry failed; YHWH's intervention through the weakest instrument succeeded. The sword David uses in v51 is Goliath's own.
17.52: wayyāqumû ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh wayyārîʿû — 'the men of Israel and Judah rose up with a shout.' The explicit distinction 'Israel and Judah' (yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh) is unusual at this period and may reflect the narrator's knowledge of the later division, or a genuine sense of the tribal tension that will surface in Saul's reign. The pursuit from the valley to Gath and Ekron traces the route back into Philistine heartland.
17.53: wayyāšuḇû ḇənê yiśrāʾēl middālēq ʾaḥărê pəlištîm — 'returned from pursuing the Philistines.' The plundering of the Philistine camp (wayyāḇōzzû ʾeṯ-maḥănêhem) follows the pattern of holy-war aftermath; the victor takes the spoil of the fleeing enemy. The military reversal is complete: Israel, paralysed for forty days, now pursues the Philistines to their own cities.
17.54: wayyiqaḥ dāwid ʾeṯ-rōʾš happəlištî wayyəḇîʾēhû yərûšālaim — 'David took the Philistine's head and brought it to Jerusalem.' Jerusalem at this time was still Jebusite (cf. v11's note that it was not Israelite); the placement of Goliath's head there anticipates David's future conquest of the city. The armour is deposited in David's own tent (ʾōhălô), not a royal treasury — David is still a private person at this point.
17.55: wayōʾmer ʾel-ʾaḇnēr śar haṣṣāḇāʾ ben-mî-zeh hannāʿar — 'Whose son is this young man?' Saul's apparent failure to recognise David (cf. 16:14–23) has generated extensive scholarly discussion. The most plausible readings are: (a) the narrative derives from independent traditions; (b) Saul genuinely does not know David's lineage (family name), though he knows the person; (c) the question is formulaic for establishing David's family claim to the king's daughter and tax exemption promised in v25.
17.56: biqqəšû ʾattāh ben-mî-zeh hāʿālem — 'find out whose son this young man is.' ʿālem is a hapax in this form; related to ʿelem (young man of fighting age, cf. 20:22); the MKT 'young man' is apt. The inquiry is delegated to Abner and then resolved by David's own identification (v58). The genealogical question matters legally: the promised rewards require knowing the family lineage.
17.57: wayyiqkāḥ ʾōṯô ʾaḇnēr wayyəḇîʾēhû lippənê šāʾûl ûrōʾš happəlištî bəyāḏô — 'Abner brought him before Saul with the Philistine's head still in his hand.' The detail of David holding Goliath's head during his interview with the king is a powerful image of accomplished fact: the promise has been fulfilled, the evidence is literal and immediate. The scene closes the combat narrative and opens the court narrative.
17.58: ʾanōḵî ben-ʿaḇdəḵā yišay bêṯ hallaḥmî — 'I am the son of your servant Jesse the Bethlehemite.' David's self-identification uses the deferential 'your servant' (ʿaḇdəḵā) for his father, placing himself within the household of a royal servant. The patronymic 'son of Jesse' (ben-yiśay) becomes David's standard identification throughout Samuel and will carry prophetic weight in later passages (cf. Isa 11:1, the 'shoot from the stump of Jesse').

18.1: The soul-binding between Jonathan and David: wᵉnepeš yôhônātān niqšərāh bᵉnepeš dāwiḏ wayyeʾĕhāḇēhû yôhônātān kᵉnapšô — 'and the soul (nepeš) of Jonathan was knit (niqšərāh) to the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul.' The verb qāšar (to bind, knit, tie) is the construction-verb: what happens is an ontological binding of two napšôt. The nepeš in Hebrew is not merely the 'soul' in a Platonic sense but the whole animated person — the breathing, feeling, willing self. Jonathan's self-gift to David (stripping his robe, armor, sword, bow, belt in v4) enacts in action what the soul-binding describes in ontological terms. The love standard — 'as his own soul' (kənapšô) — is the Lev 19:18 standard that Jesus cites as the second great commandment.
18.3: The covenant between Jonathan and David: wayyiḵrot yôhônātān wəḏāwiḏ bərît bəʾahăḇātô ʾōtô kənapšô — 'Jonathan cut a covenant with David, in his love for him as his own soul.' The kārat bərît (cutting a covenant) formula marks this as a formal covenant transaction, not merely friendship. The covenant is motivated by love (ʾahăḇāh) — the word used for covenant-loyalty love throughout the OT (Deut 6:5; 7:9; 1 Kgs 3:3). Jonathan's enactment of covenant love toward David — the crown prince stripping himself of his royal insignia to clothe the shepherd — is the OT's most vivid picture of kenotic self-emptying in covenant: rank, privilege, and weapon transferred to the beloved. The NT pattern is explicit: 'He who had equality with God did not count it a thing to be grasped but emptied himself' (Phil 2:6-7).
18.5: David succeeds everywhere: wayyaśkēl dāwiḏ bᵉḵōl ʾăšer yišlāḥēhû šāʾûl — 'David acted wisely (śākal) in everything Saul sent him to do.' The verb śākal (in the hiph'il: to act wisely, to prosper through prudent action) is the vocabulary of covenant wisdom — the same root as haśkîl in Ps 101:2 and maśkîl in the Psalm superscriptions. The verb combines wisdom, insight, and the resulting success: to act with discernment that leads to divine favor and practical effectiveness. The pattern — the Spirit-anointed son who succeeds in everything — runs from Joseph (Gen 39:3 — 'YHWH caused all he did to succeed') through David to the messianic portrayal of the Servant who 'acts wisely' (yaśkîl) before his suffering (Isa 52:13 — 'my servant shall act wisely / be high and lifted up').
18.12: Saul's fear of David: wayyirāʾ šāʾûl mippənê dāwiḏ kî hāyāh YHWH ʿimmô ûmēʿim šāʾûl sār — 'Saul was afraid of David because YHWH was with him but had departed from Saul.' The structure is a covenant-reversal: YHWH ʿimmô (YHWH was with him) has transferred from Saul to David. The divine presence-formula (YHWH ʿim X) tracks covenant election: it is with Abraham (Gen 21:22), Isaac (Gen 26:28), Jacob (Gen 28:15), Joseph (Gen 39:2), Moses (Exod 3:12), Joshua (Josh 1:5), Gideon (Judg 6:12), and now David. Saul's fear is the inverse of covenant confidence — he sees that the divine presence that underwrites success has left him and rests on his rival. Saul's yārēʾ (fear) of David is the fear that comes from recognizing that YHWH has chosen another.
18.2: lōʾ nəṯānô lāšûḇ bêṯ ʾāḇîw — 'he did not let him return to his father's house.' Saul's retention of David after the battle formalises the transition hinted at in 16:21–22. The verb nāṯan (to give, allow) in the negative implies royal fiat: David is now a court permanent rather than an occasional visitor. This new status simultaneously elevates David and removes him from his father's protection, setting up the later scenes of vulnerability.
18.4: wayyiṯpašəṭ yônāṯān ʾeṯ-hamməʿîl ʾăšer ʿālāyw wayyiṯtənēhû lədāwid — 'Jonathan stripped off the robe he was wearing and gave it to David.' The gift of the crown prince's robe (məʿîl) to David is symbolically loaded: the məʿîl is a garment of royal status (cf. 1 Sam 15:27; 24:5, 11, where it marks Saul's kingship). Jonathan's voluntary divestiture anticipates the transfer of the kingdom and is a de facto act of covenant abdication: 'your kingship will be established and I will be second to you' (23:17).
18.6: wattēṣeʾnāh hanaššîm mikōl ʿārê yiśrāʾēl lāšîr wəhamməḥōlōṯ — 'women came out from all the towns of Israel to sing and to dance.' The women's victory celebration with tambourines (tuppîm), singing, and three-stringed instruments (šālîšîm) follows the Miriam-pattern of Exodus 15 — the women lead the victory song after the decisive battle. The public, communal nature of the celebration makes Saul's jealousy over the song-content all the more politically significant.
18.7: hikkāh šāʾûl ʾăḷāpāyw wədāwid bərəḇābotāyw — 'Saul has struck down his thousands, but David his ten-thousands.' The antiphonal couplet uses the standard Hebrew poetic number escalation: ʾălāpîm (thousands) vs. rəḇābôṯ (ten-thousands). In victory songs this is a device of comparative praise, not arithmetic; the women are singing in Saul's honour of the whole campaign. But Saul will read it as a zero-sum comparison (v8).
18.8: wayyiḥar ləšāʾûl məʾōḏ wayyēraʿ bəʿênāyw haddāḇār hazzeh — 'very angry... displeased him greatly' (lit. 'it was evil in his eyes'). rāʿ bəʿênāyw is the technical formula for moral and political displeasure (cf. 2 Sam 11:27, where YHWH uses the same formula for David's sin). Saul's self-comparison — 'only the kingdom is left for him' (hāmalûḵāh ʿôḏ-lô) — is theologically ironic: this is precisely what Samuel has prophesied (13:14; 15:28). Saul's fear is accurate.
18.9: wayyəhî šāʾûl ʿôyen ʾeṯ-dāwid — 'Saul kept watching David with suspicion/an evil eye.' The phrase ʿôyen ʿayin (lit. 'eye-ing with the eye') implies hostile surveillance; some translations render 'eyed him with jealousy.' The continuous participle marks this as an ongoing attitude that will define the rest of Saul's relationship with David. What begins as jealousy (v8) becomes structural enmity (v29) becomes active murderous intent (19:1).
18.10: wattislaḥ rûaḥ-ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh ʾel-šāʾûl — 'an evil spirit from God rushed upon Saul.' The formulation rûaḥ-ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh ('evil spirit from/of God') repeats the language of 16:14–15 and raises the same theological question: in what sense does YHWH send the evil spirit? The standard reading is that YHWH permits a distressing spirit as part of the judicial abandonment of Saul after his rejection (15:26–28); the spirit is called 'from God' because it operates within YHWH's sovereign governance.
18.11: wayyāṭel šāʾûl ʾeṯ-haḥănîṯ — 'Saul hurled the spear.' The verb ṭûl (to cast, hurl) here is used for the first of three spear-throwing incidents in Samuel (18:11; 19:10; 20:33). The spear (ḥănîṯ) was a royal symbol — carried by Saul as a sceptre-like weapon (1 Sam 22:6; 26:7). Throwing it at David transfers the symbol from dominion to violence; wayyissōḇ dāwid mippānāyw paaʿmayim ('David eluded him twice') — the double escape foreshadows David's repeated deliverance.
18.13: wayyāsirēhû šāʾûl mēʿimmô wayyāśem ʾōṯô lô śar-ʾalep — 'Saul removed him from his personal service and made him a commander of a thousand.' The removal from ʿimmô ('with him,' personal attendance) to field command over a śar-ʾalep ('commander of a thousand,' a standard military unit) is a combination of lateral promotion and distancing. David is simultaneously honoured and removed from the potentially dangerous proximity of the court. The plan backfires: wayyēṣēʾ wayyābōʾ ('he went out and came in') and the people's love for him grows (v16).
18.14: wayhî dāwid maśkîl bəḵōl dərāḵāyw — 'David prospered/acted wisely in all his ways.' śāḵal (Hiphil: to be wise, prudent, to prosper) is a key term in the wisdom tradition; its use here ties David's success to divinely-given wisdom rather than military prowess alone. The causal clause wayhwh ʿimmô ('and YHWH was with him') repeats the theological explanation of David's success that will recur throughout Samuel (cf. 2 Sam 5:10; 7:3).
18.15: wayyirʾ šāʾûl ʾăšer hûʾ maśkîl məʾōḏ — 'Saul saw that he was very successful/wise.' Saul's fear (yārēʾ) is now directed at David's demonstrated competence, not just the women's song. The verb śāḵal recurs from v14: what David is (maśkîl) is exactly what Saul fears. Fear and awe of YHWH's chosen one is a recurring motif; Saul's fear of David is a perverse form of recognising the presence of YHWH with David.
18.16: wəḵol-yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh ʾōhēḇ ʾeṯ-dāwid — 'all Israel and Judah loved David.' The subject 'all Israel and Judah' reappears (cf. v5, 17:52) as the narrative marker of David's national support cutting across the later tribal divide. yōṣēʾ wāḇāʾ lipnêhem ('going out and coming in before them') is the idiom for military leadership (Num 27:17; Deut 31:2); the people love him because he leads from the front. This is precisely what Saul was supposed to do (1 Sam 8:20) and cannot.
18.17: hinnēh bittî hagəḏôlāh mēraḇ — 'here is my eldest daughter Merab.' Saul's offer of Merab fulfils the reward promised in 17:25. raq heyēh-lî libben-ḥayil ('only be a courageous man for me') conditions the marriage on continued military service, effectively using Merab as an incentive to keep David fighting — and potentially dying — against the Philistines. The phrase 'let the hand of the Philistines be against him' (v17b) makes Saul's motive explicit.
18.18: mî ʾānōḵî ûmî ḥayyay — 'who am I, and what is my life?'
