The inclusion of his wives marks this as full domestic transplantation, not just military alliance.
27.4: Saul learns that David has fled to Gath and stops searching (wayyᵉḥaḏal lᵉḇaqqēš ʾōtô). The cessation of Saul’s pursuit achieves what David sought — safety from the immediate threat. Saul’s abandonment of the pursuit is rational: David with six hundred men among the Philistines is beyond practical reach. The irony is that David’s safety comes from throwing himself on the mercy of Israel’s primary enemy — a decision that will create its own moral and political complications (ch29).
27.5: David’s request for a rural town: nittᵉnāh lî māqôm bᵉʾaḥat ʿārê haśśāḏeh wᵉʾēšᵉḇāh šām — give me a place in one of the towns of the field that I may settle there. The request to move out of the capital city (Gath) to a provincial town is politically shrewd: David reduces his visibility within Achish’s court while retaining the alliance. The rhetorical reason given — “why should your servant dwell in the royal city with you?” — is face-saving for Achish while serving David’s strategic purposes.
27.7: David’s time in Philistine territory: wᵉhāyāh mispar hayyāmîm ʾăšer yāšaḇ dāwid biśᵉḏēh pᵉlištîm yāmîm wᵉʾarbāʿāh ḥōḏāšîm — the count of days David lived in Philistine fields was days and four months, i.e., one year and four months. The term yāmîm used here as “a year” (a Hebrew idiom for an indeterminate period typically meaning a year) plus four months gives the total stay. The precision is unusual in narrative — the narrator marks the duration as significant for the chronology of David’s pre-kingship period.
27.8: David’s raiding policy: he raids the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites — peoples who were yōšᵉḇê hāʾāreṣ mēʿôlām (inhabitants of the land from of old). The Geshurites (not the Geshurites of Aram-Geshur) and Girzites are archaic pre-Israelite populations in the Negev approaches; the Amalekites are Israel’s longstanding enemies (cf. 1 Sam 15). David targets populations that are both politically safe to attack (no Philistine allegiance) and theologically legitimate (traditional enemies of Israel), maintaining covenantal consistency even while serving a Philistine overlord.
27.9: David’s total-destruction policy: wᵉhikkāh dāwid ʾet hāʾāreṣ wᵉlōʾ yᵉḥayyeh ʾîš wᵉʾiššāh — David would strike the land and leave alive neither man nor woman. The no-survivors policy is both military — preventing intelligence reports — and consistent with the ḥerem (total destruction) principle applied to Amalekites. The spoil — sheep, oxen, donkeys, camels, clothing — is brought back to Achish. David’s application of ḥerem against Amalekites is ironic given Saul’s failure to do so (ch15), but his motivation here is strategic deception rather than covenantal obedience.
27.10: Achish’s question: ʾal pᵉšaṭtem hayyôm — where have you raided today? David’s deceptive answer claims raids against the Negeb of Judah, the Negeb of the Jerahmeelites, and the Negeb of the Kenites — Israelite-adjacent territories. The deception is strategic: Achish believes David is attacking his own people, making him persona non grata in Israel and permanently dependent on Philistine patronage. David’s multilevel deception — raiding Israel’s enemies while claiming to raid Israel’s allies — is narratively presented as clever survival, not moral exemplar.
27.11: The no-survivors policy serves intelligence security: wᵉdāwid lōʾ yᵉḥayyeh ʾîš wᵉʾiššāh lᵉhāḇîʾ Gat lēʾmōr pen yagîḏû ʿālênû lēʾmōr kōh ʿāśāh dāwid — David left no man or woman alive to bring back to Gath, saying they would tell what David had really done. The narrator explicitly provides David’s reasoning, making the deception transparent to the reader even while Achish remains deceived. The phrase wᵉḵēn miš pāṭô kol hayyāmîm ʾăšer yāšaḇ biśᵉḏēh pᵉlištîm — and this was his practice all the days he lived in Philistine territory — shows the systematic nature of the deception over sixteen months.
27.12: Achish’s complete trust in David: wayyaʾămēn ʾāḵîš bᵉdāwid lēʾmōr haḇʾēš hibʾîš bᵉʿammô bᵉyiśrāʾēl — Achish trusted David, thinking David had made himself utterly repulsive to his own people in Israel. The verb baʾš (to stink, to make oneself odious) is used of social excommunication — the same root used when Israel “became odious” to the Philistines in ch13:4. Achish believes David has permanently burned his bridges with Israel, becoming a “servant forever” (lî lᵉʿeḇed ʿôlām). The irony is complete: the Philistine king trusts the man who has been systematically deceiving him, while believing the man whose loyalty is entirely to YHWH and Israel.

1.1: The story opens with ʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ('a certain man') — the same formula as Judg 13:2 (Manoah's introduction) and Ruth 1:1 (Elimelech's introduction). The formula marks an ordinary Israelite whose family will become the vehicle for YHWH's extraordinary purpose. Rāmātayim (H7436, dual of rāmāh, heights) — two heights; ṣôpîm (watchers/Zuphites). Elkanah (ʾelqānāh H511) means 'God has created/acquired' — a theophoric name with ʾēl and qānāh (the root of Gen 14:19: 'God Most High, creator of heaven and earth'). His four-generation genealogy roots him in the clan of Zuph of Ephraim (the land of Zuph reappears in 1 Sam 9:5).
1.2: The two-wife structure: Ḥannāh (H2584, grace/favor — from ḥēn) and Pĕninnāh (H6444, possibly from pənînîm, corals/rubies — Prov 3:15; 8:11 use the word for what wisdom exceeds in value). ʿāqārāh (H6135, barren) — the theologically loaded term for a woman YHWH has not opened. The OT's barren-matriarch pattern: Sarah (Gen 11:30) → Rebekah (Gen 25:21) → Rachel (Gen 29:31) → Samson's mother (Judg 13:2) → Hannah. Each barrenness is YHWH's withholding before miraculous provision of a child with a covenantal role. The pattern marks Samuel as one in that line before his birth is narrated.
1.3: YHWH Ṣəḇāʾôt (LORD of Armies/Hosts) — this divine title appears with new frequency in Samuel; ṣāḇāʾ (H6635, army/host) denotes earthly armies, the heavenly angelic forces, and the stars (the host of heaven). The title asserts YHWH's military-cosmic sovereignty. Shiloh (šilôh H7887) was the central sanctuary during the judges period, housing the ark (Josh 18:1). Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas are introduced in the same verse as the annual sacrifice — a narrative anticipation that these two are inseparable from the worship context that will expose their corruption.
1.4: The annual sacrifice generates mānōt (portions, H4490, from mānāh H4487, to count/apportion) distributed to family members. The portions represent the offerer's share in the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) eaten as communion with YHWH. Deut 12:11-12 mandates that the whole household — including servants — share in this meal. Elkanah's distribution sets up v5's tension: giving portions to Peninnah and her children (the fruitful wife) while reserving a special portion for Hannah (the barren one who is nevertheless loved) frames the conflict between social fruitfulness and covenantal love.
1.5: Elkanah gives Hannah mānāh ʾaḥat ʾappāyim — a double or choice portion; the exact phrase is unique and debated: 'double portion' (double of what the others received), or 'a portion of two faces/nostrils' (a sighing portion, i.e., accompanied by grief). The theological note — 'because YHWH had closed her womb' (kî-ʾāḥar rāḥam YHWH bĕʿaḏāh) — directly attributes Hannah's barrenness to divine action. This is consistent throughout the OT: YHWH 'closes' (Gen 20:18) and 'opens' (Gen 29:31; 30:22) wombs as sovereign acts. The attribution prevents a purely biological reading and frames Hannah's condition as a theological state awaiting divine resolution.
1.6: Peninnah is called Hannah's ṣārāh (H6869, rival/adversary — the technical term for a co-wife in a polygamous household; Lev 18:18 uses the root to regulate taking 'a woman as a rival to her sister'). Her kaʿas (H3707, provocation/vexing) is directed at Hannah 'because YHWH had closed her womb.' Peninnah weaponizes the evidence of divine action — Hannah's barrenness as visible sign of YHWH's withholding — as the basis for social mockery. YHWH's closing of the womb is thus made the catalyst for the prayer that will become the occasion of YHWH's opening it.
1.7: The pattern repeats annually (šānāh ḇĕšānāh): Hannah weeps and does not eat. Two standard markers of deep mourning in Hebrew culture (cf. David for Bathsheba's first son, 2 Sam 12:16; Ahab's sulking, 1 Kgs 21:4). The sanctuary visit — which Deut 16:11-14 commands as a time of rejoicing — becomes Hannah's annual moment of deepest grief. The gap between what YHWH commands (rejoice at the feast) and what YHWH has given her cause for (grief over barrenness) is the story's emotional architecture. The liturgical feast as the occasion of deepest personal anguish sets up prayer that is prayer indeed — not ritual performance but desperate petition.
1.8: Elkanah's consolation — 'Am I not better to you than ten sons?' — uses the number ten as the superlative-complete number (the ideal family size implying more than enough). His misreading: he interprets Hannah's grief as a relational deficit when it is a theological and social condition (barrenness is a divine closure, a social shame, a missing covenant blessing). What a husband's affection cannot supply — the covenant blessing of children — YHWH alone can give. Elkanah's genuine love and genuine misunderstanding together make Hannah's turn to YHWH in prayer the only remaining recourse.
1.9: Hannah rises (wayyāqom) after eating at Shiloh — textually debated; LXX suggests she rose before eating. mārah lāh (it was bitter to her) uses the root mārar (H4843, to be bitter) — the same root as Naomi's Mara (Ruth 1:20). The bitterness of soul (marāh nāpeš) is a recognized OT psychological-spiritual state (Job 3:20; 7:11). Eli is positioned at the doorpost of the sanctuary (mĕzûzat hêḵal YHWH): the threshold-guardian whose role is to mediate between worshipers and YHWH, but who will in this scene misread the most authentic prayer before him.
1.10: Waṯṯitpallēl ʿal-YHWH — she prayed to YHWH. pālal (H6419, to pray/intercede) in the reflexive Hithpael means to interpose oneself before God — the prayer-form that brings oneself before the divine presence. wāḇāḵōh ṯiḇkeh (weeping she wept) — the cognate infinitive absolute intensifies: her weeping was complete, sustained, total. The combination of prayer and intense weeping characterizes the OT's most urgent intercession (Ezra 10:1; Ps 6:6; Hezekiah in Isa 38:2-3). Hannah's prayer is the OT model for petition that combines explicit request, vow, and complete emotional disclosure before YHWH.
1.11: Hannah's vow (neder H5088) contains: (1) invocation of YHWH Ṣəḇāʾôt; (2) double infinitive absolute 'if you will indeed see' (rāʾōh tirʾeh) — emphatic appeal; (3) the covenant verb zāḵar (remember me — active covenant engagement, not mere recall: Gen 8:1 [Noah], Gen 30:22 [Rachel]); (4) 'not forget your servant'; (5) specific petition: 'give your servant a male child'; (6) reciprocal vow: 'I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life'; (7) Nazirite sign: 'a razor shall not come upon his head.' The Nazirite vow (Num 6:1-21) combined no wine, no razor, no corpse contact — Hannah specifies the razor. The Qumran Samuel text expands the conditions; the LXX adds wine.
1.12: The verb hirḇĕṯāh lĕhitpallēl (she multiplied/continued her prayer) — Eli watches (šōmēr, H8104) from his seat at the doorpost. Eli's position — ʿal-hakkissēʾ ʿal-mĕzûzat hêḵal YHWH (on the seat by the doorpost of the temple of YHWH) — places him at the threshold between outer and inner sanctuary, the liturgical guardian. The hêḵal (H1964) is the main hall of the Shiloh sanctuary, the term used for both the tabernacle's inner structure and later the Solomonic temple. Eli sees lip movement without sound — and misreads it.
1.13: Hannah's prayer is mĕḏabberet ʿal-libbāh (speaking upon her heart) — silent, lip-only prayer without audible voice. This is one of the OT's earliest descriptions of purely mental prayer (as opposed to spoken petition). Qôlāh lōʾ yiššāmēaʿ (her voice was not heard) — the silent petition addressed to YHWH alone, not audible to human witnesses, is a theological statement about the audience of prayer. Eli's misreading of silent devotion as public drunkenness is doubly ironic: the priest who fails to discipline his sons' public sacrilege condemns the woman whose private prayer is exemplary.
1.14: Eli's accusation — ʿaḏ-māṯay tiššəkārîn (how long will you be drunk?) — uses šāḵar (H7937, to be drunk). The irony: Eli's sons are guilty of cultic violations in the same sanctuary (2:13-17), yet the priest accuses the praying woman of drunkenness. His instruction — 'put away your wine' (hāsîrî ʾet-yênēḵ) — reads the external sign (moving lips, no sound) without perceiving the internal reality. This failure of spiritual discernment characterizes Eli throughout: he observes external forms while missing inner realities — the same failure that will make him unable to see YHWH's presence while Samuel receives it in the same room (ch3).
1.15: Hannah's self-defense: 'ʾiššāh qĕšat-rûaḥ ʾānōḵî' — I am a hard/pressed-spirit woman. qāšāh (H7186, hard/severe) applied to rûaḥ: her spirit is under severe pressure. 'I have poured out my soul before YHWH' — šāpaḵ ʾet-napšî lipnê YHWH: the metaphor of pouring out (šāpaḵ, H8210, to pour/spill out) the nepeš (soul/inner self/animating life) before YHWH captures the image of total self-disclosure in prayer. Used in Ps 42:4 ('I pour out my soul') and Lam 2:19 ('Pour out your heart like water before the face of the Lord'). The image treats the person as a vessel whose entire inner contents are emptied before YHWH in prayer.
1.16: 'Do not regard your servant as a daughter of Belial' — al-tittēn ʾet-ʾamaṯḵā lipnê bat-bĕliyyāʿal. bat-bĕliyyāʿal (daughter of worthlessness/Belial) — the feminine form of the descriptor the narrator will apply to Eli's sons in 2:12 (bĕnê bĕliyyāʿal). The irony is sharp: Eli is applying to the praying woman the exact characterization that correctly describes his own sons. Bĕliyyāʿal (H1100): 'without profit/worth' or 'without ascending' — a compound of bĕlî (without) + yaʿal (profit/value). She concludes: 'I have been speaking from the abundance of my complaint and grief' (mērōḇ śîḥî wĕḵaʿăśî) — śîaḥ (H7879, anxious musing/complaint) + kaʿas (H3707, vexation/grief) — the vocabulary of deep grief-prayer.
1.17: Eli's blessing — lĕḵî lĕšālôm (go in peace) — is the standard priestly benediction. 'The God of Israel grant your petition which you have asked of him' (wĕʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yittēn ʾet-šĕlātēḵ ʾăšer šāʾalt) — the verb šāʾal (H7592, to ask/request) appears here for the first time in the chapter and will dominate vv18-28. The blessing is pronounced without Eli knowing the content of the petition — he is endorsing the form (earnest prayer) before knowing the substance. This is the priestly blessing functioning as a genuine covenant channel: the blessing pronounced in YHWH's name has efficacy regardless of the priest's knowledge of its specific application.
1.18: Hannah goes away, eats, and 'her face was no longer sad' (ûpānehā lōʾ-hāyû-lāh ʿôḏ) — before any empirical change in her condition. The change in countenance signals an inner resolution: she has transferred her burden to YHWH in prayer, and the priestly blessing has confirmed YHWH's receipt of it. This is the OT portrait of the faith that receives before the evidence — the peace that precedes the answer (cf. Phil 4:6-7: 'in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God, and the peace of God will guard your hearts'). The mechanism is the same: full petition → transferred burden → peace.
1.19: They rise early (wayyaškimû babboqer) and worship before YHWH — the morning worship follows the prayer-and-blessing sequence. 'And YHWH remembered her' (wayyizkor-āh YHWH) — zāḵar (H2142, to remember) is the covenant verb of divine initiative: YHWH 'remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1), Abraham (Gen 19:29), Rachel (Gen 30:22). YHWH's remembering is not the recovery of forgotten information but the active covenant engagement that initiates fulfillment. The verse's pairing of 'Elkanah knew Hannah his wife' with 'YHWH remembered her' places human and divine action in sequence: the means (normal marital relations) and the agency (YHWH's active covenant remembering) are both present.
1.20: Hannah conceives and bears a son named Šĕmûʾēl (Samuel H8050). The etymology given — 'because I asked him from YHWH' (kî mēYHWH šĕʾiltîw) — is based on šāʾal (to ask). The name's literal elements are more likely šēm (name) + ʾēl (God) = 'his name is God' or 'heard of God' — but the šāʾal etymology fits better the name Šāʾûl (Saul). This is a much-discussed lexical tension: the etymology that belongs to Saul's name is given to Samuel. Some scholars read this as deliberate — the man who embodies YHWH's 'asking' (the one truly given to YHWH) is Samuel, while the king Israel will 'ask for' (šāʾal) in ch8 is Saul. The šāʾal wordplay will saturate the chapter's conclusion.
1.21: Elkanah goes up to make the yearly sacrifice and pay his vow (ʾet-nidĕrô) — a vow not narrated previously, suggesting he also made a commitment at the Shiloh visit. Hannah decides not to go until Samuel is weaned — standard Iron Age practice was nursing for 2-3 years. The specific mention of Elkanah's vow suggests both spouses responded to Hannah's crisis with vows; the narrative focuses on Hannah's because it is the one that directly concerned Samuel's dedication. The transition sets up the tension: Hannah will fulfill her vow when the child is old enough to remain permanently at the sanctuary.
1.22: Hannah's stated intention — 'he may appear before YHWH and remain there forever' (wĕyāšaḇ šām ʿad-ʿôlām) — uses ʿôlām (H5769, forever/indefinitely). The same word governs YHWH's covenant promises (Abrahamic, Davidic). Hannah applies the covenant-permanence vocabulary to her son's service: not a temporary loan but a permanent dedication. Elkanah's response — 'only may YHWH establish his word' (yāqēm YHWH ʾet-dĕḇārô) — implies that YHWH has already spoken a word concerning Samuel (through the priestly blessing, or a direct oracle not narrated), and the proper response to fulfilling the vow is to invoke YHWH's confirmation of his own prior word.
1.23: Elkanah's phrase 'only may YHWH establish his word' — the LXX reads 'establish what proceeds from your mouth' (harmonizing to Hannah's vow), but MT's 'his word' (dĕḇārô) refers to a prior YHWH word about Samuel. If the priestly blessing of v17 is in view, Elkanah is invoking its fulfillment; alternatively, a direct divine communication not narrated here may be implied. Either way, Elkanah frames Hannah's vow-fulfillment within YHWH's own prior speech — the human vow-keeping and divine word-keeping are aligned as a single purpose-event.
1.24: When Samuel is weaned, Hannah brings him to Shiloh with a three-year-old bull (par mĕšulaš), an ephah of flour (ʾêpāh ʾaḥat qemeaḥ), and a skin of wine (nēḇel yāyin). The three-year-old bull is the peak-maturity sacrificial animal — Numbers 15:8-10 prescribes a bull for major vow-fulfillment offerings. The full sacrificial equipment (bull, flour, wine — the components of a grain offering with liquid accompaniment) signals that this is a vow-fulfillment offering of the highest grade. Wĕhannaʿar nāʿar (the boy was young) — the repetition of the naʿar root emphasizes his youth, setting up the contrast between his smallness and the gravity of the covenant act about to occur.
1.25: They slaughter the bull and bring the child to Eli — the sacrifice precedes the presentation. The sequence (sacrifice → bring to Eli → present to YHWH) follows the vow-fulfillment pattern: the sacrifice ratifies the vow; the priest receives the dedication; the child enters permanent sanctuary service. Samuel is being given not as Eli's personal servant but as YHWH's sanctuary servant — the distinction will become critical in ch2 when Samuel's faithful ministry contrasts with Eli's sons' corruption. The scene's formality emphasizes the covenant seriousness of the transfer: this is legally binding dedication, not a temporary lending.
1.26: Hannah's self-identification to Eli — 'I am the woman who was standing here in your presence, praying to YHWH' — uses the oath formula bî ʾăḏōnî ḥay napšĕḵā ('as your soul lives, my lord'). The anamnesis: 'I am the woman' (ʾānî hāʾiššāh) — the woman Eli could not correctly identify when he saw her praying now identifies herself in the light of what YHWH has done. The priest who called her drunk is looking at the answered prayer. The identification without reproach or triumph is itself a form of hesed: she returns in gratitude, not in score-settling.
1.27: 'For this child I prayed, and YHWH has granted me my petition which I asked of him' — wayyittēn YHWH lî ʾet-šĕʾēlātî ʾăšer šāʾaltî mēʿimmô. The verse concentrates the šāʾal vocabulary: šĕʾēlātî (my petition, what-I-asked) and šāʾaltî (I asked). The fulfillment of prayer is expressed as exact correspondence between petition and answer: she asked for this child, YHWH gave precisely what was asked. The verb nātan (gave) — YHWH as the giver who matches his gift to the specific request — is the theology of answered prayer (James 1:5: 'God gives generously to all without finding fault').
1.28: 'Therefore I have lent him to YHWH. As long as he lives, he is lent to YHWH' — wĕgam ʾānōḵî hišʾiltîhû laYHWH ... šāʾûl laYHWH. The final wordplay: hišʾiltîhû (I have asked/lent him out — the Hiphil of šāʾal) and šāʾûl laYHWH (he is asked-for/lent to YHWH — the passive participle). šāʾûl is precisely the name of Saul (the future king Israel will 'ask for' in ch8). Samuel ends ch1 described as šāʾûl laYHWH — the lexical shadow of the coming king falls over the closing of the story of the prophet who will anoint him. The chapter that is about Samuel's naming ends with the word that names Saul.

3.1: 'The word of YHWH was rare (yāqār, H3368) in those days; there was no frequent vision' (ʾên ḥāzôn niphrāṣ). yāqār means precious/rare/scarce — used for precious stones (Prov 3:15; Ps 72:14) and rare events. The scarcity of YHWH's word creates a condition of preciousness-through-absence: rare speech, when it comes, carries extraordinary weight. ḥāzôn (H2377, vision/prophetic revelation) — the technical term for the vision-form of revelation (Isa 1:1; Ezek 1:1). The paired rarity of divine word and prophetic vision describes a period of spiritual drought that simultaneously marks the darkness just before YHWH breaks his silence — and through the most unexpected medium.
3.2: Eli's failing eyesight — hēḥēllû ḵĕhôt lōʾ yûḵal lirʾôṯ (had grown so dim he could not see) — is both literal and theologically resonant. The priest who cannot physically see parallels his inability to see the sins of his sons (2:22-24) and his misreading of Hannah's prayer (1:12-14). The root kāhāh (H3543, to grow dim/weak) that describes his eyesight is the same root used in 3:13 for his failure to 'restrain' (kāhāh) his sons. His physical dimming and ethical weakness share the same verb root, making the connection explicit. The prophet who 'sees' (receives visions) is coming to replace the priest who cannot see.
3.3: The lamp of God (nēr ʾĕlōhîm) had not yet gone out — the perpetually burning lampstand of the tabernacle (Exod 27:20-21; Lev 24:2-4) burning from evening to morning. 'Not yet gone out' situates the scene just before dawn: the end of the night watch when the lamp's oil was nearly exhausted. Samuel lies near the ark of God (ʾăšer šām ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm) — the presence-locus of YHWH in the sanctuary. Proximity to YHWH's dwelling is the narrative setup for the divine speech: YHWH speaks to the one lying nearest to him. The lamp about to go out + the ark where YHWH dwells = the last moment of the old order, immediately before the new prophetic era begins.
3.4: YHWH calls 'Samuel! Samuel!' — the doubled name is the formula of urgent divine address: Abraham, Abraham (Gen 22:11); Moses, Moses (Exod 3:4); Elijah (1 Kgs 19:13); Martha, Martha (Luke 10:41); Saul, Saul (Acts 9:4). The repetition signals a call requiring total attention and complete response — YHWH is summoning a person into a new relationship, not simply conveying information. Samuel's response hinnēnî (H2009, here I am) is the standard formula of covenant availability: Abraham (Gen 22:1), Moses (Exod 3:4), Isaiah (Isa 6:8). The identical response to YHWH and to Eli (vv5-6) shows Samuel has not yet learned to distinguish the source, but his posture of availability is correct.
3.5: Samuel runs to Eli thinking he called. Eli's response — 'I did not call; lie down again' (lōʾ qārāʾṯî šûḇ šĕḵāḇ) — is accurate: he genuinely did not call. The pattern of threefold misidentification (vv5-8) before correct identification is the OT's narration of prophetic formation: the call must be recognized before it can be answered rightly. Samuel's assumption that the voice belongs to Eli is natural (who else would call a child in the sanctuary at night?). The narrative's patience with the three errors reflects the understanding that prophetic formation takes time and correction.
3.6: YHWH calls a second time: 'Samuel!' — and Samuel rises and goes to Eli again. The exact repetition of the call and response sequence with no variation is the narrative's way of showing that the misidentification is genuine, not convenient: Samuel is consistently and sincerely mistaking YHWH's voice for Eli's. The learning process requires the mistake to be repeated until the teacher is able to diagnose the pattern. Eli's second 'I did not call' begins to set up the recognition — he is now hearing the pattern from Samuel's reports that something is happening that is not attributable to him.
3.7: 'Now Samuel did not yet know YHWH' (wĕšĕmûʾēl ṭerem yāḏaʿ ʾet-YHWH) — the same verb yāḏaʿ as 2:12 ('Eli's sons did not know YHWH') but with opposite theological valence. Eli's sons did not know YHWH because of corruption; Samuel does not know him because 'the word of YHWH had not yet been revealed to him' (ṭerem yiggāleh ʾēlāyw dĕḇar-YHWH) — YHWH has not yet initiated the relationship from his side. The not-knowing is circumstantial and about to be remedied by divine revelation. The contrast between the two uses of lōʾ yāḏaʿ within a few verses is the narrator's precise theological characterization: same surface, opposite reality.
3.8: YHWH calls a third time. Eli understands (wayyāḇen, H995 — to discern/perceive; the root of wisdom-discernment, bînāh) that YHWH is calling the boy. This is Eli's last moment of genuine spiritual service: he correctly perceives what is happening and gives Samuel the proper response-formula. The priest who cannot see (v2) and who failed to act against his corrupt sons can still discern a genuine divine call when the pattern makes it undeniable. His instruction is theologically correct: ‘Say, “Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears”’ — he teaches Samuel how to respond to YHWH even though the oracle to follow will pronounce Eli's doom.
3.9: The formula Eli gives: dabbēr YHWH kî šōmēaʿ ʿaḇdeḵā (Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears). šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear/obey) is the covenant verb — Deut 6:4 (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) establishes hearing-that-obeys as the fundamental covenant stance. 'Your servant hears' means 'your servant is in the posture of responsive obedience' — not passive reception but active readiness to respond. The formula models the prophetic stance toward divine speech: total availability, no filtering, responsive action. Samuel will spend his life as a prophet practicing this formula's content.
3.10: YHWH comes and stands (wayyāḇōʾ YHWH wayyiṯyaṣṣēḇ) — theophanic presence language: YHWH does not merely send a voice but takes up a standing position, indicating a personal appearance (cf. Gen 18:2; Exod 34:5; Num 22:22). The doubled name formula ('Samuel! Samuel!') repeats the urgency of the initial call. Samuel's response — dabbēr kî šōmēaʿ ʿaḇdeḵā — follows Eli's instruction exactly but omits the divine name: YHWH is present and speaking; the name is not needed for identification. Samuel has learned to address the voice directly.
3.11: 'Behold, I am about to do something in Israel at which the two ears of everyone who hears it will tingle' (ṣilênāh ʾoznê kol-šōmĕʿô). ṣālan (H6750, to tingle/ring) applied to ears describes news so shocking it creates a physical sensation — used only here, 2 Kgs 21:12 (judgment on Manasseh), and Jer 19:3 (judgment on Jerusalem). The formula marks this as one of the most severe judgment pronouncements in the OT. The ear-tingling is the somatic marker of devastating news — the same kind of news that Paul reserves for the severest covenant warnings (Gal 1:8: 'even if we or an angel from heaven should preach to you a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be accursed').
3.12: 'I will fulfill against Eli all that I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end' (mēhāḵēl wĕʿaḏ-kallōh). The phrase uses ḵālal (begin) and kālāh (complete/finish): from-beginning-to-end, the full oracle will be fulfilled. This total-fulfillment formula is used for YHWH's covenant commitments (both promises and judgments): what is spoken will be completed without remainder. The word of the man of God (2:27-36) is now confirmed by YHWH's direct speech — double confirmation by oracle and divine voice establishes the certainty and scope of what is coming.
3.13: 'I have told him that I am about to punish his house forever, for the iniquity that he knew, because his sons were blaspheming God, and he did not restrain them' (wĕlōʾ kihāh bām). kāhāh (H3543, to restrain/rebuke/dim) — the same root as Eli's dimming eyes. He did not kihah his sons — he did not rebuke with the force of removal from office. The sons' sin: mĕqallĕlîm (H7043, making light/contemptible/cursing) — they made YHWH's name and service contemptible. The combination of knowing the sin (v22: 'Eli heard everything') and not restraining it makes the guilt shared: Eli's passivity becomes complicity in his sons' contempt.
3.14: 'The iniquity of Eli's house shall not be atoned for by sacrifice or offering forever' (ʾim-yitkappēr ʿăwōn bêṯ-ʿēlî bĕzēḵaḥ ûḇĕminḥāh ʿaḏ-ʿôlām). This is a severe covenant declaration: the two primary sacrificial forms — zāḵaḥ (animal sacrifice, H2077) and minḥāh (grain offering, H4503) — cannot provide atonement for this sin. The system that Hophni and Phinehas corrupted cannot serve as the mechanism of their house's restoration. Num 15:30-31 establishes that willful, high-handed rebellion (not unintentional sin) is beyond ordinary sacrificial atonement. The Levitical system's limits are here explicitly stated: some sins require a different remedy — which the rest of Scripture will identify as the sin-bearing that only the sinless can perform.
3.15: Samuel lay until morning and opened the doors of the sanctuary — his regular morning duty, performed without alteration despite the oracle received. He is afraid to tell the vision (mĕhaggîḏ ʾet-hammārʾeh ʾel-ʿēlî). mārʾeh (H4758, vision/appearance) — the vision received in the night encounter. Samuel's fear is appropriate: the oracle is devastating, and it concerns the man who raised him. The tension between loyalty to Eli and fidelity to YHWH's word is the first test of his prophetic formation. The faithful prophet must report YHWH's word even when it is painful — this test precedes every subsequent oracle Samuel will deliver.
3.16: Eli calls 'Samuel, my son!' (šĕmûʾēl bĕnî) — the paternal address invokes the relationship. Samuel responds hinnēnî — the same covenant-availability formula he used with YHWH (v4-8): total responsiveness to both YHWH and to those in authority over him. Eli's request: 'Do not hide it from me' (ʾal-nāʾ tĕḵahēd mimmennî) — kāḥaḏ (H3582, to hide/conceal) inverted: tell me everything, conceal nothing. By explicitly requesting full disclosure, Eli creates the covenant obligation for Samuel to report completely — including the devastating personal dimension of the oracle against Eli's own house.
3.17: Eli adds the self-imprecation oath: 'God do so to you and more also if you hide anything from me' (kōh yāʿăśeh-lĕḵā ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵōh yōsîp̄ ʾim-tĕḵaḥēd mimmennî dāḇār). The standard oath formula (Ruth 3:13; 1 Sam 14:44; 2 Sam 3:9) places divine sanctions on Samuel if he withholds anything. Eli has legally bound Samuel to full disclosure. The one who is about to receive the devastating oracle has himself created the obligation for complete reporting — removing any possibility of Samuel softening or omitting elements 'to spare feelings.' Covenant oaths ensure that YHWH's word is transmitted accurately and completely.
3.18: Samuel reports everything; Eli's response: 'It is YHWH. Let him do what seems good to him' (YHWH hûʾ haṭṭôḇ bĕʿênāyw yaʿăśeh). This is Eli's finest recorded moment and his last significant speech. Submission to YHWH's covenant judgment — without complaint, without self-defense, without seeking to negotiate — is the correct response to a prophetic verdict. The same formula of submission appears in David (2 Sam 10:12; 15:26) and characterizes the person who understands that YHWH's judgments, even when devastating, are within YHWH's sovereign goodness. Eli receives the oracle against his house with theological integrity even as he receives it without the practical action that might have changed it.
3.19: 'YHWH was with Samuel and let none of his words fall to the ground' (wĕlōʾ-hippîl mikkol-dĕḇārāyw ʾārṣāh). The idiom nāpal ʾārṣāh (to fall to the ground) for prophetic words that fail to fulfill — they 'drop' without reaching their target. YHWH's guarantee that none of Samuel's words fall is the Deuteronomic criterion for a true prophet (Deut 18:21-22: if the word does not come to pass, YHWH did not speak it). Samuel's total reliability as a prophetic channel — established from the first oracle — is the foundation for his national authority. Isa 55:11 applies the same principle to YHWH's word itself: 'it shall not return to me empty.'
3.20: 'All Israel from Dan to Beersheba' (mîḏān wĕʿaḏ-bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ) — the standard expression for the full national extent: Dan in upper Galilee (northern limit) to Beersheba in the Negev (southern limit), approximately 150 miles. 'Knew that Samuel was established (nĕʾĕmān, H539, confirmed/faithful) as a prophet (nāḇîʾ, H5030) of YHWH.' nāḇîʾ — the standard term for prophet applied to Samuel here for the first time. nĕʾĕmān — from ʾāman, the root of 'amen' — characterizes Samuel's prophetic reliability: his word is confirmed, trustworthy, faithful. The nation's recognition of his prophetic authority is the public side of YHWH's establishment of him as the covenant's human mouthpiece.
3.21: 'And YHWH continued to appear in Shiloh, for YHWH revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh by the word of YHWH' (wayyōsep̄ YHWH lĕhērāʾôṯ ḇĕšilōh kî niglāh YHWH ʾel-šĕmûʾēl ḇĕšilôh biḏḇar YHWH). Three revelation verbs in one verse: rāʾāh (appear/be seen, H7200), gālāh (reveal/uncover, H1540), dāḇar (speak/word, H1696). The triple characterization — appearing, revealing, speaking — describes the full range of divine self-disclosure. After v1's rare word and absent vision, YHWH now regularly appears at Shiloh through Samuel. The new prophetic era has begun: the drought of divine speech is over; YHWH's word comes now through his established prophet.

19.1: wayyədabbēr šāʾûl ʾel-yônāṯān bənô wəʾel-kol-ʿăḇādāyw ləhāmîṯ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ — the verb hāmît (Hiphil of mûṯ, to cause to die) is official and premeditated, not a heat-of-the-moment impulse. The command goes to Jonathan and all the servants simultaneously — a public execution order. The narrative irony is immediate: Jonathan, the designated heir Saul is trying to protect, will become David’s defender.
19.2: wayyaggēḏ Yônāṯān ləDāwîḏ — “Jonathan told/reported to David.” The verb nāgaḏ (H5046, to declare openly) is the standard word for formal disclosure. Jonathan’s act is not disobedience to his father but an act of covenantal loyalty to David that takes precedence — a conflict of obligations that the narrator presents without condemning Jonathan.
19.3: Jonathan’s plan: waʾănî ʾēṣēʾ wəʿāmaḏtî ʾeṣel-ʾāḇî — “I will go out and stand beside my father.” He positions himself as a visible mediator, physically between Saul and the field where David hides. The phrase wəʾăḏabberāh bəḵā — “I will speak for you” — frames Jonathan as David’s legal advocate (mēlîṣ-like function) before the king.
19.4: Jonathan’s intercession opens with ʾal-yeḥṭāʾ hammelek bəʿaḇdô ḇəDāwîḏ — “let not the king sin against his servant David.” The verb ḥāṭāʾ (to miss the mark/sin) reframes what Saul contemplates as a theological offense, not merely a political or personal action. ʿaḇdô (his servant) appeals to the covenant relationship between king and loyal retainer.
19.5: wayyāśem ʾeṯ-napšô bəḵappô — “he put his life in his hand” — the idiom for risking death (nāpāš as the seat of life, kap as the palm/cupped hand). Jonathan’s advocacy rests on the factual record: David risked his life, YHWH won a great victory (yəšûʿāh gədôlāh), and Saul himself witnessed it. The appeal to eyewitness testimony structures the argument legally.
19.6: wayyišmaʿ šāʾûl bəqôl Yônāṯān — “Saul listened to Jonathan’s voice.” šāmaʿ bəqôl (hear + in the voice of someone) is the biblical idiom for obedient heeding — more than hearing, it means yielding. Saul swears an oath by YHWH: ḥay-YHWH ʾim-yûmāṯ — “as YHWH lives, he shall not die.” The oath formula invokes YHWH as guarantor; its subsequent violation by Saul deepens the portrait of a man who treats YHWH’s name as an instrument.
19.7: Jonathan brings David back to Saul, and David resumes court service. The reconciliation is genuine but temporary — narratively it sets up the relapse of vv.9-10. The wayyəhî šālôm (and there was peace) of the restored situation is fragile; the Hebrew use of the perfect tense signals a completed state that will be undone.
19.8: War resumes; David’s military success is described with wayyak bāhem makkāh gədôlāh (he struck them with a great blow). The formulaic victory phrase (cf. 14:31) frames David’s battlefield role as ongoing — he remains the indispensable warrior even as Saul turns murderous.
19.9: rûaḥ YHWH rāʿāh ʾel-šāʾûl — “an evil spirit from YHWH came to Saul.” The construction is the same as 16:14-16: rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH. Hebrew theology does not assign ultimate causation to any power independent of YHWH; the “evil” spirit is divine in origin. Whether this means YHWH sent it directly or permitted it is a genuine theological debate — but the grammar attributes it to YHWH without hesitation. The result is Saul’s violent episode with the spear.
19.10: wayyəḇaqēš šāʾûl lənakôṯ bəḥaníṯ — “Saul sought to pin David with the spear.” The verb nāḵāh (Hiphil, to strike/pin) with a spear (ḥănîṯ) occurs three times in this narrative cluster (18:11; 19:10; 20:33); each repetition deepens the portrait of Saul’s escalating violence. wayyippaṭēr mippenê šāʾûl — “he escaped from before Saul” — pāṭar (H6452) means to slip away/escape; a different verb from the usual nûs/brāḥ, suggesting a close, narrow escape.
19.11: Saul sends agents (malʾāḵîm) to watch David’s house through the night. The intelligence apparatus of Saul’s court is now deployed against YHWH’s anointed. ləšomrô wəhămîṯô babbōqer — “to watch him and kill him in the morning.” Michal’s warning is the domestic counterpart to Jonathan’s public advocacy — the entire household conspires to protect David.
19.12: wattōreḏ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ bəʿaḏ haḥallôn — “she let David down through the window.” The window-escape is a recurring biblical motif for protected flight: Rahab’s spies (Josh 2:15), Paul in Damascus (2 Cor 11:33). wayyēleḵ wayyānos wayyimmālēṭ — three rapid verbs (he went, he fled, he escaped) marking the urgency and success of the escape.
19.13: tərāpîm (H8655, household idols/teraphim) — Michal places the idol in David’s bed with a cushion of goat hair at its head to mimic a sleeping figure. The tərāpîm were household divine images, associated with family religion and divination (cf. Gen 31:19-35; Ezek 21:21). Their presence in David’s house is notable and unremarked; the narrative assumes their existence without condemnation here, though later reforms will prohibit them (1 Sam 15:23; 2 Kgs 23:24).
19.14: Michal’s lie: ḥôleh hûʾ — “he is sick.” The deception is a protective lie. The verb ḥālāh (H2470, to be sick/weak) uses the same root as the adjective; the simple statement buys time. The messengers accept it and report back without entering.
19.15: Saul escalates: bring him to me in his bed — bammittāh — so I can kill him. The persistence of the murderous intent even after Jonathan’s successful intercession (v6) confirms that Saul’s oath was temporary capitulation, not genuine repentance.
19.16: The deception is discovered: messengers enter and find the tərāpîm and goat hair. The elaborate domestic deception — household idol as body double — has a darkly comic quality; it also demonstrates Michal’s resourcefulness and her readiness to lie to her father to protect her husband.
19.17: Saul’s confrontation with Michal: lāmmāh kākāh rimmîṯinî — “why have you deceived me thus?” rāmāh (H7411, to deceive/betray) is a strong word — the same root used of Laban’s deception of Jacob (Gen 29:25). Michal’s excuse — “he said he would kill me if I didn’t let him go” — is likely a further lie; the narrator records it without endorsing it.
19.18: David’s flight to Samuel at Ramah: wayyaggēḏ lô ʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh lô šāʾûl — “he told him everything Saul had done to him.” The nāgaḏ (declare) vocabulary marks David’s report as an official complaint to his spiritual authority. Naioth (nayyôṯ, perhaps “dwellings/pastures”) in Ramah was the site of Samuel’s prophetic school — David seeks refuge in the prophetic community.
19.19: Saul receives intelligence about David’s location. The wayyuggaḏ ləšāʾûl (it was reported to Saul) uses the same nāgaḏ root — information networks work in both directions. The surveillance state Saul has built turns all social relationships into potential intelligence sources.
19.20: Saul’s first group of agents: wayyarəʾ ʾeṯ-lahăqaṯ hannəḇîʾîm nəḇiʾîm — “they saw the company of prophets prophesying.” lahăqāh (H3862, a company/troop) is a technical term for a prophetic guild or school. rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm came upon the agents, and they too began to prophesy. The irresistible divine Spirit overrides the human mission — YHWH’s protection of David operates by co-opting his pursuers.
19.21: Second and third groups of agents are sent; both are similarly overtaken. The repetition (three groups) emphasizes the completeness of the protection and intensifies the irony: Saul’s military resources are turned into a prophetic choir. The escalation also mirrors the plagues of Egypt — repeated divine resistance to a persistent human will.
19.22: Saul himself goes. wayyišʾal wayyōmer — he asks where Samuel and David are; he is told at Naioth in Ramah. The bôr haggādôl (great cistern) at Sechu marks the journey’s midpoint — Saul is still determined but spatially isolated before the Spirit meets him.
19.23: wayyəhî rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm ʿālāyw gam-hûʾ wayyēleḵ hālôḵ wayyiṯnaббēʾ — “the Spirit of God came upon him too, and he walked along prophesying.” The phrase hālôḵ wayyiṯnaббēʾ — “walking and prophesying” — suggests a continuous ecstatic state during the entire journey, not just upon arrival. The Spirit overrides Saul’s murderous intent before he reaches David.
19.24: wayyipšaṭ gam-hûʾ bəgādāyw wayyiṯnaббēʾ gam-hûʾ lipnê Šəmûʾēl — “he too stripped off his garments and prophesied before Samuel.” The king lies naked (ʿārôm) all day and all night — total loss of royal dignity. ʿal-kēn yōmərû hăgam šāʾûl bannəḇîʾîm — “therefore they say, ‘Is Saul also among the prophets?’” The proverb (first heard in 10:11-12) is given its second occurrence; in context it becomes ironic commentary on a king whose divine anointing has been redirected to protect his prey.

20.1: David’s threefold interrogative — meh-ʿāśîṯî meh-ʿăwōnî ûmeh-ḥaṭṭāʾṯî — “What have I done? What is my iniquity? What is my sin?” The three nouns are a legal formulation of innocence: ʿāwōn (iniquity, moral guilt), ḥēṭʾ (sin, missing the mark), and the general ʿāśāh (wrong done). David presents himself formally as innocent before a court that has condemned him without charge.
20.2: Jonathan’s response appeals to his own position in Saul’s confidence: lōʾ-yōmar-lî ʾāḇî dāḇār gādôl ʾô dāḇār qāṭōn — “my father does nothing great or small without telling me.” The rhetorical point is that Jonathan should know if Saul planned murder. But the dramatic irony is immediate: Jonathan’s trusted position is precisely why Saul hides his real intent from him (v3).
20.3: yāḏaʿ yāḏaʿ ʾāḇîḵā — emphatic construction (absolute infinitive + finite verb): “your father knows, he truly knows.” David sees what Jonathan cannot: that Saul conceals his intention precisely because Jonathan’s loyalty to David is known. The phrase wəḵî-penê-yənāṯān yiḵʾāḇ — “lest Jonathan be grieved” — attributes motive to Saul: his concealment is not consideration but calculation.
20.4: Jonathan’s unconditional offer: kol-ʾăšer-tōmar napšəḵā ʾeʿěśeh-llāḵ — “whatever your soul (nāpāš) asks, I will do for you.” The use of nāpāš (soul/life) in the vow gives it covenantal depth; Jonathan commits the full weight of his life-loyalty to David’s request.
20.5: David’s plan: the new moon festival (hāḥōdeš). Attendance at the royal feast was obligatory for the inner court — absence would immediately be noticed. David uses his absence as the diagnostic: Saul’s reaction will reveal his intentions. ʾēšēḇ ʾeṣel hammeleḵ leʾĕḵôl — “I should sit with the king at the feast” — the festival meal as a political performance of loyalty.
20.6: The alibi: a family sacrifice in Bethlehem, David’s hometown. mizbaḥ-yāmîm — “an annual sacrifice” — a regular family sacrificial occasion (cf. Hannah’s annual journey, 1:3). Jonathan is to present it to Saul on David’s behalf. The alibi is plausible and theologically grounded — family sacrifice was a legitimate obligation.
20.7: ʾim-kōh yōmar ṭôḇ šālôm laʿaḇdəḵā — “if he says ‘good,’ peace to your servant” — the conditional structure maps two outcomes: if Saul accepts the alibi peacefully, David is safe; if he yiḥar-ʾap (becomes angry, lit. “his nose burns”), the intended evil is confirmed. ḥārāh-ʾap is the standard Hebrew idiom for explosive anger, with “nose” as the seat of breath and rage.
20.8: wəʿāśîṯā ḥesed ʿal-ʿaḇdəḵā — “show covenant loyalty to your servant.” ḥesed (H2617) is the key theological term of this passage — the steadfast, covenantal love that binds the two together. David calls Jonathan to fulfill the specific obligations of their covenant (bərît YHWH, covenanted before YHWH, v8b). If David has done iniquity, Jonathan may kill him himself — the offer of self-judgment as a guarantee of good faith.
20.9: Jonathan refuses the conditional execution: ḥālîlāh lāḵ — “far from it for you!” (a strong negative expression). His promise: kî ʾim-yāḏaʿ ʾēḏaʿ — “if I know for certain” — again the absolute infinitive for certainty. Jonathan will not withhold the information; the ḥesed he has pledged includes full disclosure when needed.
20.10: David’s practical question: mî yaggîḏ lî — “who will tell me?” The arrow signal system (vv.20-22) is developed in response to this question. The question reveals the precariousness of the situation: David cannot be at court to receive the information directly; the covenant communication must be covert.
20.11: lekā wənēṣēʾ haśśāḏeh — “come, let us go out to the field.” The two leave the city for a private space. The field (śāḏeh) is the locus of the covenant renewal; away from court politics, the two friends speak freely. The movement from palace to open field mirrors the shift from institutional to personal covenant.
20.12: Jonathan’s covenant oath: YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl kî-ʾeḥqōr ʾeṯ-ʾāḇî — “By YHWH, the God of Israel — when I sound out my father.” ḥāqar (H2713, to search out/probe) is used for careful inquiry, not confrontation. The oath invokes YHWH as guarantor: if the news is good, Jonathan will send it to David; if not, he will send that too.
20.13: kōh-yōsîp YHWH laYônāṯān wəḵōh yōsîp — the double oath formula (may YHWH add/do more to Jonathan and more still) invokes graduated divine punishment if the oath is broken. The commitment is total: Jonathan promises to actively warn David and help him escape. wahayāh YHWH ʿimmāḵ kaʾăšer hāyāh ʿim-ʾāḇî — “may YHWH be with you as he was with my father” — Jonathan explicitly passes the divine favor from Saul to David.
20.14: wəʾim-ʿôdennî ḥay wəʿāśîṯā ʿimmî ḥesed YHWH — “if I am still alive, you shall show me the ḥesed of YHWH.” Jonathan’s request turns the ḥesed covenant around: he now asks David for the divine-quality covenant loyalty that David has been asking of him. ḥesed YHWH (the ḥesed of YHWH) is a higher category than human loyalty — it is the kind of love that YHWH himself shows, now required of humans toward one another.
20.15: wəʾal-takkrēṯ ʾeṯ-ḥasdəḵā mēʿim bêṯî ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “do not cut off your covenant loyalty from my house forever.” kāraṯ ḥesed (cut off loyalty) is the formal termination of covenant obligation. Jonathan looks beyond his own life to his descendants (bêṯî, my house): the covenant is intergenerational. This provision directly establishes the legal basis for David’s care of Mephibosheth in 2 Sam 9.
20.16: wayyikrōṯ Yônāṯān ʿim-bêṯ Dāwîḏ — “Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David.” The formal covenant language (kāraṯ bərît) frames this as a dynastic covenant, not merely a personal friendship — it extends to families and successors. ûḇiqqēš YHWH miyyaḏ ʾōyəḇê Dāwîḏ — “and the LORD will require it from the hand of David’s enemies” — YHWH is invoked as the enforcer of the covenant’s anti-enemy clause.
20.17: wayyôsep Yônāṯān lahaššbîaʿ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ bəʾahabāṯô ʾōṯô kî ʾahabaṯ napšô ʾăhēḇô — “Jonathan caused David to swear again because of his love, for he loved him as his own soul.” ʾahabaṯ napšô — “the love of his soul” — echoes the standard formula for deepest personal attachment; cf. 18:1 where the same phrase first appears. The repetition (three mentions of ʾāhab) reinforces that this covenant rests on genuine love, not political calculation.
20.18: Jonathan outlines the logistical plan: at the new moon feast, David’s seat will be empty (nip̱qaḏtā, you will be missed/your absence will be noticed). The verb pāqaḏ (H6485, to visit/inspect/notice absence) is often used for military musters where an absence is significant; its use here anticipates the formal occasion of the feast.
20.19: The signal location: the stone Ezel (ʾeḇen hāʾāzel, perhaps “the departing stone” or a proper name). David is to hide there on the third day. The specificity of the geographic marker — a known landmark — anchors the covert communication plan in real terrain.
20.20: Jonathan will shoot three arrows toward the stone — the number three creates plausible hunting cover. The signal is embedded in a real action that looks innocent to an observer: a young man practicing archery. The code is embedded in the spatial relationship between arrow and target: near = safe; far = flee.
20.21: ʾim-ʾāmōr ʾōmar lannnaʿar hinnēh haḥiṣṣîm mimmənnaḵā waqqāḥāh — “if I say to the boy, ‘look, the arrows are on this side of you’” — the near-arrow signal means peace. šālôm lāḵ wəʾên dāḇār — “peace to you and there is no thing.” The coded message uses the simplest possible language, interpretable only by those who know the code.
20.22: wəʾim-kōh ʾōmar laʿelem hinnēh haḥiṣṣîm mimmənnaḵā wāhāʾlāh lēḵ kî šillaḥăḵā YHWH — “but if I say, ‘the arrows are farther out,’ then go, for the LORD has sent you away.” The far-arrow signal encodes a theological frame: David’s departure is not a defeat but divine sending (šālaḥ). YHWH himself is the one dismissing David from court — his flight is obedience to divine direction.
20.23: wəhaddāḇār ʾăšer dibbarnû ʾănî wāʾattāh hinnēh YHWH bênenû ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “the matter we have spoken — I and you — the LORD is between us forever.” ʿaḏ-ʿôlām (to eternity) gives the covenant a trans-temporal dimension. YHWH as the standing witness (YHWH bênenû) means the covenant cannot be dissolved by death or changed circumstances.
20.24: wayyissāṯēr Dāwîḏ baśśāḏeh — David hides in the field while the new moon feast proceeds at court without him. The juxtaposition of David concealed in the field and Saul at the feast underlines the divided reality: the anointed king is in hiding while the rejected king dines in state.
20.25: The seating arrangement: Saul against the wall (qîr), Abner beside him, Jonathan opposite, David’s place empty. The court seating was fixed and hierarchical; David’s empty place is a visible absence, a gap in the social body that demands explanation.
20.26: Saul’s first assumption: ritual impurity (ṭāmēʾ hûʾ biltî ṭāhôr). The law required ritual purity for participation in sacred meals; Saul assumes David is unclean rather than absent by choice. The initial charitable interpretation — which Saul himself knows is probably false — keeps the feast proceeding on day one.
20.27: Day two: maddûaʿ lōʾ-bāʾ ben-Yišay gam-timôl gam-hayyôm ʾel-hallāḥem — “why has Jesse’s son not come to the meal, either yesterday or today?” The deliberate naming of David as “son of Jesse” (ben-Yišay) is a rhetorical reduction — denying David his court title, treating him as a mere commoner.
20.28: Jonathan delivers the alibi: David asked permission to go to Bethlehem for a family sacrifice. The simplicity and plausibility of the excuse is the point; Jonathan delivers it straightforwardly without elaboration, making it harder to dismiss.
20.29: yəšallaḥ-nā ʾōṯî — “please let me go.” Jonathan repeats David’s alleged words, emphasizing the family obligation: a brother insisted, a clan sacrifice required attendance. The language of ʾāḥîw ṣiwwāh (my brother commanded me) appeals to kin-obligation — a category Saul himself would recognize as legitimate.
20.30: ben-naʿăwat hammardût — “son of a perverse, rebellious woman.” This is Saul’s insult to Jonathan — one of the most cutting familial insults in the Hebrew Bible. naʿăwāh (perversity) and mardût (rebellion) attack the mother to shame the son. The eruption reveals what Saul has been suppressing: his certainty that Jonathan’s loyalty to David is treason against the royal house.
20.31: kî kol-hayyāmîm ʾăšer ben-Yišay ḥay ʿal-hāʾădāmāh lōʾ tikkôn ʾattāh ûmalkûṯəḵā — “as long as Jesse’s son lives, neither you nor your kingdom will stand.” In his rage Saul involuntarily confirms YHWH’s election of David — his own words are the best testimony to David’s coming kingship. lōʾ tikkôn (will not stand/be established) uses the kûn root of divine establishment — the very word YHWH uses for his covenant (2 Sam 7:16).
20.32: Jonathan’s legal defense: lāmmāh yûmāṯ meh-ʿāśāh — “why should he die? What has he done?” The twice-repeated question is the form of legal defense — demanding specification of the offense before capital punishment. Jonathan makes the procedural argument: no crime has been named.
20.33: wayyāṭēl šāʾûl ʾeṯ-haḥănîṯ ʿālāyw ləhakkōṯô — “Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan to strike him.” The same violent act aimed at David (18:11; 19:10) is now aimed at his own son. The ḥănîṯ (spear) has become the symbol of Saul’s disorder; its deployment against Jonathan confirms that Saul is past reason.
20.34: wayyaqom Yônāṯān mēʿim haššulḥān baḥărî-ʾap — “Jonathan rose from the table in fierce anger.” ḥărî-ʾap (burning of nose = fierce anger) is the same idiom used of YHWH’s wrath. Jonathan is angry and grieved (kî huḵlam Dāwîḏ mēʾēt ʾāḇîw) — the grief is for David’s public shaming, not for his own near-death. The covenant loyalty continues to define Jonathan’s emotional priority.
20.35: Jonathan goes to the field at the appointed time. naʿar qāṭōn (a small boy/young servant) is the instrument of the covert communication — the boy knows only the literal action, not its meaning (v39). The use of an innocent intermediary protects both parties.
20.36: Jonathan shoots the arrow that “flew past the boy” — the far-arrow signal. wayyôreʾ haḥēṣ lahaʿăḇîrô — “he shot the arrow to make it pass beyond.” The deliberate overshoot encodes the warning in a visible physical act that the boy carries out without understanding its significance.
20.37: hēn haḥēṣ mimməḵā wāhāʾlāh — “the arrow is beyond you, isn’t it?” The far-arrow is confirmed. The signal is deployed; the danger is real; David must go. Jonathan’s shouted words to the boy are simultaneously a message to David — the public shout serves a double function.
20.38: maher ḥûšāh ʾal-tāʿmōḏ — “hurry, make haste, don’t stop.” The urgency escalates through three imperatives. The boy gathers the arrows and returns to the city — his errand complete, the drama continuing without his knowledge.
20.39: wəhannnaʿar lōʾ-yāḏaʿ məʾûmāh — “the boy knew nothing.” This detail closes the frame of the covert communication. The narrator emphasizes the boy’s innocence specifically to underline the technical brilliance of the signal: it was invisible as a message to everyone except its two intended recipients.
20.40: Jonathan gives his weapons to the boy and sends him away — creating the private space for the farewell. The handing off of weapons (kēlāyw) is significant: Jonathan has already given David his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt in 18:4; now he removes the remaining implements of court identity.
20.41: wayyippōl ʾappayim ʾarṣāh wayyištaḥû šālōš pəʿāmîm — “he fell with his face to the ground and bowed three times.” The triple prostration is a royal greeting — David addresses Jonathan with formal court honor even in the wilderness. wayyiḇkû ʾîš ʾeṯ-rēʿēhû — “they wept, each with his friend.” The mutual weeping mirrors the earlier ḥesed language: the covenant is felt as grief at separation.
20.42: lēḵ ləšālôm ʾăšer nišbaʿnû šənênû ʾănaḥnû bəšēm YHWH — “go in peace, for we have both sworn in the name of YHWH.” The final word of the farewell grounds the covenant in YHWH’s name. bên yādî ûḇên yāḏəḵā ûḇên zarʿî ûḇên zarʿăḵā ʿaḏ-ʿôlām — “between me and you, between my seed and your seed, forever.” The covenant extends to descendants; Jonathan speaks the terms of what will become the Mephibosheth arrangement.

21.1: David arrives at Nob, the priestly city, alone. wayyeḥaraḏ ʾAḥîmeleḵ liqrāʾṯ Dāwîḏ — “Ahimelech trembled to meet David.” ḥāraḏ (H2729, to tremble/be frightened) signals that the priest senses something wrong in David’s solitary arrival — no royal escort, no explanation. The question “why are you alone, and no one with you?” is professional, not merely curious.
21.2: dāḇār ṣiwwānî hammelek — “the king has commanded me on a matter.” David’s lie protects him at the cost of implicating Ahimelech in an act of treason (aiding an unauthorized person). ûmāqôm muʿad yāḏaʿtî ʾeṯ-hannəʿārîm — “and I have told the young men the appointed place” — David invents a detachment of soldiers waiting elsewhere; the fiction provides cover for the request that follows.
21.3: The request for bread: ḥămišāh-leḥem tēn bəyāḏî ʾô hammimṣāʾ — “give me five loaves in my hand, or whatever is available.” Five loaves is a substantial request; the “or whatever” signals real hunger and urgency. The number five will reappear in the NT feeding narratives (Matt 14:17).
21.4: ʾên-leḥem ḥōl ʾel-taḥaṯ yāḏî kî-ʾim-leḥem qōdeš — “there is no ordinary bread under my hand, but there is holy bread.” The ḥōl vs. qōdeš distinction (profane/ordinary vs. holy/consecrated) is the central tension of the episode. The bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm, Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) was ritually reserved — replaced weekly, eaten by priests only. Ahimelech’s conditional offer — if the men have kept from women — applies Numbers 5 purity logic to the emergency.
21.5: ʾāḵēn ḥullǎlāh libbênûš miṯmôl šilšôm — “indeed, women have been kept from us since yesterday and the day before.” David’s claim of ritual purity for his men is the halachic justification. Jesus will cite this episode (Matt 12:3-4; Mark 2:25-26; Luke 6:3-4) as a precedent that human need can override ceremonial law: David’s act is cited as lawful by the Son of David — an implicit claim that he has authority to interpret Torah.
21.6: wayyittēn-lô hakkōhēn qōdeš — “the priest gave him holy bread.” leḥem happānîm (bread of the Presence/Face) was the weekly bread set out before YHWH, renewed each Sabbath (Lev 24:8); removed bread was hot — just replaced. The hot bread given to David is the bread of the divine presence — the immediate, daily sustenance of YHWH, now given to the fugitive king. The irony: the man anointed to rule is fed by the bread of the Lord’s own table.
21.7: āḏōm ʾeḏōmî — Doeg the Edomite, “detained before YHWH.” ʿāṣûr lipnê YHWH (detained/kept before YHWH) may mean he is undergoing a ritual process (a vow? disease quarantine?). Whatever its cause, his presence at the sanctuary during David’s visit is the fatal detail — he will report to Saul (22:9-10) and massacre the priests. The narrator plants this narrative bomb quietly.
21.8: David asks for a weapon: ḥānîṯ ʾô-ḥereb — “a spear or sword.” kî gam-ḥarbî wəgam-kēlai lōʾ-lāqaḥtî bəyāḏî — “for I have brought neither my sword nor my equipment.” The haste of the flight is emphasized; the king’s champion is weaponless. The absence of David’s own sword sets up the provision of Goliath’s sword — a deeply ironic supply.
21.9: ḥereḇ Golyāṯ happəlištî ʾăšer hikkeṯā bəʿēmeq hāʾēlāh — “the sword of Goliath the Philistine whom you struck in the Valley of Elah — there it is, wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod.” Goliath’s sword is kept as a sacred trophy at the sanctuary. ʾaḥǎrê hāʾēpôḏ (behind the ephod) places it in the innermost zone of the tabernacle precinct. David receives back the sword of his greatest victory as a fugitive at his lowest point — the warrior is re-armed with his own trophy.
21.10: David flees to Achish king of Gath — the homeland of Goliath, carrying Goliath’s sword. The audacity of the move (seeking refuge among the Philistines) is remarkable; the risk is that he will be recognized. wayyāqom wayyibrāḥ bayyôm hahûʾ mippenê šāʾûl — the pace of flight is immediate; “that very day.”
21.11: Achish’s servants identify David: hălôʾ-zeh Dāwîḏ melek hāʾāreṣ — “is this not David the king of the land?” They quote the victory song: “Saul has struck his thousands, and David his ten thousands.” David’s fame has preceded him; the Philistines know his identity and his glory — making Gath the most dangerous possible refuge.
21.12: wayyāśem Dāwîḏ ʾeṯ-hadəḇārîm hāʾēlleh bilibbô — “David took these words to heart.” śûm bilbāb (to set in the heart) marks serious calculation. His fear of Achish is tactical — he recognized the danger and responded with the only tool available: performance.
21.13: wayyəšannēʾ ʾeṯ-ṭaʿmô bəʿênêhem wayyiṯhôlēl bəyādām — “he changed his behavior before them and acted as if insane.” šānāh (H8138, to change) with ṭaʿam (reason/taste/judgment) means deliberately altering his presentation. hiṯhôlēl (Hitpoel of hālal or hôlēl) = to feign madness. He makes marks on the doors and lets saliva run on his beard — both behaviors associated with mental breakdown in the ancient world.
21.14: Achish’s dismissal: rəʾû ʾîš mištaggēaʿ — “look, this man is insane.” šāgaʿ (H7696, to be mad) is the word used for ecstatic prophecy in negative contexts; the boundary between prophetic ecstasy and madness was culturally thin. Achish’s reaction — “why did you bring him to me?” — shows that a mad person was of no political value.
21.15: hăḥasēr məšuggāʿîm ʾānî — “Am I short of madmen?” Achish’s rhetorical question dismisses David as socially worthless. The irony is deep: the man who killed Achish’s greatest champion is dismissed as a worthless madman — survival through humiliation. The same pattern — apparent weakness concealing divine protection — runs throughout David’s wilderness period.

28.1: Introductory temporal clause (wayhî bāyyāmîm hāhēm, "in those days") links this episode to the Philistine mobilization context. Achish's command to David — yādōaʿ tēdaʿ (literally "knowing you will know," infinitive absolute + imperfect = emphatic certainty) — underscores that this is not an invitation but a binding military commitment. The irony that David is now obligated to fight against Israel will drive chs 28–29.
28.2: ʾattāh tēdaʿ ʾēt ʾăšer yaʿăśeh ʿabdəḵā — "you will see what your servant can do." David's reply is deliberately ambiguous — it commits to nothing specific. Achish interprets it as complete loyalty and names him permanent bodyguard (šōmēr lərōʾšî, keeper of my head). David never fights the battle; the Providence that resolves his dilemma comes from the Philistine commanders in ch29.
28.3: ʾôḇ (H178) and yiddəʿōnî (H3049) — "mediums and necromancers." ʾÔḇ (singular and collective) denotes the hollow pit or wineskin through which spirits were thought to speak, and by extension the practitioner. Yiddəʿōnî derives from yādaʿ (to know) — the "knowing one," a spirit-familiar. Both are banned by the Mosaic code (Lev 19:31; 20:6, 27; Deut 18:11). Saul himself expelled (hiʿbîr, caused to pass/removed) them — and will now secretly seek one. The narrative plants the irony: the law Saul enforced against others he will break himself.
28.4: Geographical orientation: Shunem (Philistines, north side of Jezreel Valley) vs. Gilboa (Israelites, south side). The valley between them becomes the killing ground of ch31. The same Gilboa site will prompt David's lament in 2 Sam 1:21 ("mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew or rain on you").
28.5: wayyarʾ šāʾûl ʾet maḥănēh pəlištîm wayyirāʾ wayyeḥerad libbô məʾōd — "he was terrified; his heart shook with dread." Three fear-words: yārēʾ (to be afraid), ḥārad (to tremble/shudder), and məʾōd (greatly). The phrase wayyeḥerad libbô (his heart shook) uses lēḇ again as the seat of inner response. Saul's fear is extreme and total — the contrast with David, who "strengthened himself in YHWH" (30:6) after an equally devastating situation, could not be sharper.
28.6: Three channels of revelation — all closed. Wayyišʾal šāʾûl baYHWH wəlōʾ ʿānāhû — "Saul inquired of the LORD, but the LORD did not answer him." The three failed channels: (1) baḥălōmôt — dreams; (2) baûrîm — the Urim (part of the priestly ephod, the sacred lot); (3) bannəḇîʾîm — prophets. This triad exhausts the legitimate means of divine revelation (cf. Num 12:6-8). The contrast with 30:7-8 — where David inquires through the ephod and immediately receives a clear answer — marks the theological divide: YHWH answers the king he has chosen and is silent to the king he has rejected.
28.7: baʿălat ʾôḇ — "a woman who is a medium" (literally "mistress of a spirit-pit"). The feminine participle baʿălat (owner/possessor, from baʿal) used for the woman marks her as the authorized practitioner of her craft. Endor is in Manassite territory north of Jezreel — Saul must pass through or near Philistine-controlled terrain to reach her, adding physical danger to moral compromise.
28.8: wayyiṯḥappeś šāʾûl — "Saul disguised himself" (Hithpael of ḥāpas, H2664, to search out / conceal oneself). The Hithpael reflexive indicates active self-concealment. The disguise is both literal (changed clothes, night travel) and symbolic: Saul who presented himself in his full royal height is now hidden. Bāʾ baʿădê hallaylāh — "came in the cover of night" — darkness accompanies the illicit act.
28.9: The woman invokes Saul's own policy against her: ʾattāh yādaʿtā ʾēt ʾăšer ʿāśāh šāʾûl — "you know what Saul has done." She uses the familiar "Saul" as if speaking to an outsider, not knowing she is addressing Saul himself. The dramatic irony peaks here: Saul hears his own name and his own enforcement of the ban described to his face.
28.10: wayyiššāḇaʿ lāh šāʾûl baYHWH lēʾmōr — "Saul swore to her by the LORD." The formula ḥay YHWH (as the LORD lives) is the standard covenant oath. Saul invokes YHWH to protect a woman engaged in a practice YHWH forbids — the theological incoherence is complete. The oath itself is a further breach of the covenant he is already violating.
28.11: The woman asks mî ʾaʿăleh lāḵ — "who shall I bring up for you?" (ʿālāh, to go up — here causative: to cause to come up). The verb is technically used of ascending from Sheol — the underworld conceptualized as below ground (cf. Ps 30:3; 86:13). Whether the woman actually raised Samuel or performed a deception the text does not explicitly adjudicate; the narrative presents what follows as a genuine exchange.
28.12: wattiṣʿaq bəqôl gādôl — "she cried out with a loud voice." The sudden, surprised cry suggests the woman herself was startled — possibly indicating she expected her usual process of deception and received something unprecedented. Her immediate recognition of Saul (lāmmāh rimmîtānî wəʾattāh šāʾûl, "why did you deceive me? You are Saul!") occurs because seeing the genuine apparition revealed to her the identity of her client.
28.13: ʾĕlōhîm ʿōlîm min hāʾāreṣ — "I see a divine figure rising up from the earth." ʾĔlōhîm (H430) is grammatically plural but takes a plural participle (ʿōlîm, "rising"). The term can mean "gods," "divine beings," "angels," or (rarely) "the God." Here the plural form is conventionally explained as a plural of majesty describing a single figure. The ambiguity is significant: the text does not identify this figure as definitively "Samuel's spirit" — the narrator uses the woman's description, leaving the nature of the apparition theologically open while making Samuel's identity the working assumption of the narrative.
28.14: mah tāʾrô — "what does he look like?" (tāʾrô from tōʾar, H8389, form/appearance). The description — old man, wrapped in a robe (məʿîl) — triggers Saul's recognition: wayyēdaʿ šāʾûl kî šəmûʾēl hûʾ (Saul knew it was Samuel). The məʿîl is the priestly robe Samuel wore — the same garment whose edge Saul tore in 15:27. Recognition through a distinctive garment connects this scene to the covenant rupture of ch15.
28.15: lāmmāh hirgaztanî — "why have you disturbed me?" (rāgaz, H7264, to quake/agitate). Samuel's first word is protest: the séance is a violation even of the dead's rest. Bəṣar lî məʾōd — "I am in desperate straits" — the same root ṣārar (to be cramped/distressed) used for extreme crisis. Saul then describes the triple silence of v6. Samuel's response to being called up is not sympathy but the reiteration of the verdict already pronounced.
28.16: sûr mēʿimmāḵ wayhî ʿārêḵā — "the LORD has turned away (sûr, H5493) from you and become your adversary (ṣar, H6145)." Sûr is the departure-verb also used in 16:14 for the departure of the rûaḥ. The noun ṣar (foe/adversary) shares its root with śāṭān in function — the accuser who stands against. YHWH, who was the divine warrior fighting for Israel, is now positioned as Saul's enemy. Saul's question "why do you ask me?" (lāmmāh tiš'al bî) receives the answer: there is no point asking the mediator when the one who would answer has become your opponent.
28.17: wayyaʿaś YHWH lô kaʾăšer dibbēr bəyādî — "the LORD has done to you exactly what he said through me." The formula kaʾăšer dibbēr bəyādî (as he spoke by my hand) identifies Samuel as the instrument of YHWH's word — prophetic fulfillment terminology. The verb qāraʿ (torn) echoes 15:28; the same event is replayed in summary form: the kingdom was torn from Saul's hand and given to David (rēʿeḵā, "your neighbor," same word as 15:28).
28.18: lōʾ šāmaʿtā bəqôl YHWH — "you did not obey the voice of YHWH." The šāmaʿ vocabulary from ch15 reappears as the explicit ground of the judgment. The failure was not listening-and-acting on Amalek (lōʾ ʿāśîtā ḥărôn ʾappô bāʿămālēq) — the fierce judgment not executed. The word ḥărôn (burning anger, H2740) marks the intensity of the divine command that was not carried out.
28.19: māḥār ʾattāh ûḇānêḵā ʿimmî — "tomorrow you and your sons will be with me." The adverb māḥār (tomorrow) is a timed death sentence. The phrase ʿimmî (with me) means "in Sheol/in the realm of the dead" — Samuel is speaking from there. The double transfer announced: Israel and Saul into Philistine hands, and Saul-and-sons to Samuel in death. Both parts are fulfilled in ch31.
28.20: wayyimahēr wayyipôl mᵉlōʾ qômātô ʾarṣāh — "Saul immediately fell full length on the ground." Mᵉlōʾ qômātô (literally "the fullness of his height") — Saul the tall man who stood head-and-shoulders above all Israel (9:2) is now measured out on the ground. The narrative detail is an acted emblem: his physical height had been his royal credential; now he lies that same height on the floor.
28.21: The woman shifts from medium to host. wattarʾ ʾet šāʾûl kî nibhal məʾōd — "she saw how shaken (nāḇhal, H926, to be dismayed/confused) he was." Her concern is practical — he needs strength to make the journey back. The moral texture is complex: the woman who performed the forbidden séance acts with genuine compassion; the narrative does not moralize.
28.22: šimʿā nāʾ gam ʾattāh bəqôl šipḥāteḵā — "please listen to me" (šāmaʿ bəqôl — the obedience-of-hearing formula). The woman uses the vocabulary of covenantal hearing for a mundane request to eat. The phrase is ironic: the chapter turns on whether Saul listened to the right voices; now a necromancer uses the same idiom to urge physical sustenance.
28.23: waymāʾēn wayyōʾmer lōʾ ʾōḵal — "he refused, saying I will not eat." Māʾēn (H3985, to refuse/decline) — the same root used in ch15 for covenant refusal themes. Saul's initial refusal gives way under sustained pressure (wayyipṣar bô ʿăḇādāyw wəgam hāʾiššāh). The eating of the meal is a final act of hospitality; after it, Saul goes to his death.
28.24: The woman's meal: fattened calf (ʿēgel marbēq), flour kneaded, unleavened bread (maṣṣôt). Maṣṣôt is the bread of the Passover and sacred meals; its use here is probably not symbolic but reflects the fastest bread to prepare (no waiting for leavening). The hospitality given to the doomed king at the hands of one who practices what Saul outlawed is one of the story's final ironies.
28.25: wayyāqumû wayyēlǝḵû ballaylāh hahûʾ — "they rose and left that same night." The departure in darkness echoes the arrival in darkness (v8). Saul leaves Endor as he arrived — hidden, broken, and moving toward a death already announced. The chapter closes without commentary; the reader carries the weight of what was said in the darkness.

29.1: Chapter 29 returns to the Philistine military context (interleaved with ch28's temporal setting). ʾăpēq (Aphek) is the marshaling point in the coastal plain; ʿên ʾăšer beyizrəʿēl (the spring at Jezreel) is the Israelite position. The geography establishes the forces converging for Gilboa.
29.2: waʾădōnê pəlištîm ʿōḇərîm lamēʾôt wəlāʾălāpîm — "the Philistine lords passing in review by hundreds and thousands." The administrative review format mirrors Israelite mustering language (cf. 11:8; 15:4) — the Philistines have a structured military hierarchy. David and his men march at the rear with Achish — the most exposed position, furthest from the lords who will object.
29.3: ʾêleh hāʿiḇrîm māh lāhem — "What are these Hebrews doing here?" ʿIḇrî (H5680, Hebrew) — a term used by outsiders for Israelites; it carries a social-class or ethnic-distinction connotation rather than being the covenant name. Achish's defense of David — lōʾ māṣāʾtî bô məʾûmāh miyyôm nāpəlô ʿāday (I have found nothing against him from the day he "fell" to me) — uses nāpal ʾel (fell to, defected to), the standard idiom for changing sides.
29.4: wəhāyāh lāmô ləśāṭān bamməlḥāmāh — "he could turn against us in battle" (literally "he will become a śāṭān to us" — an adversary/accuser). The commanders use śāṭān (H7854, accuser/adversary) precisely: David as a potential fifth column. Their concern — ûbammāh yitrāṣeh ʾel ʾădōnāyw (how better to win back his lord's favor?) — correctly reads the political calculus: David would need to demonstrate loyalty to Saul by turning on the Philistines.
29.5: The commanders cite the battle-hymn of 18:7: hikkāh šāʾûl bəʾălāpāyw wədāwid bərəḇāḇōtāyw — "Saul has killed his thousands, David his ten thousands." The song that made Saul jealous (18:8-9) now surfaces as evidence that David is more dangerous than any ally. The song that nearly destroyed David in Saul's court now saves him from fighting against his own people.
29.6: ḥay YHWH kî yāšār ʾattāh — "As the LORD lives, you have been trustworthy/upright" (yāšār, H3477, straight/upright). Achish swears by YHWH — a pagan king invoking Israel's God — to attest David's innocence. ʾên māṣāʾtî bəḵā rāʿāh — "I have found nothing wrong with you" — the forensic formula of acquittal. The double attestation (Achish at vv3, 6, 9) places a threefold hostile-witness declaration of David's innocence in the record. The irony: David is publicly exonerated by a Philistine lord at the very moment Saul is seeking illegal counsel to face the same battle.
29.7: wəšûḇ ûlēḵ bəšālôm — "return and go in peace." The formula of peaceful dismissal releases David without dishonor. The narrative does not record David's inner response to being dismissed — whether relief or frustration is left to the reader, though the following chapter (Ziklag) shows the cost of the absence.
29.8: David's protest uses the formula of covenant service: meh māṣāʾtā bəʿabdəḵā — "what have you found against me?" The same language as Achish's own attestation (v6). Whether David genuinely wished to fight or was performing a necessary deception is left ambiguous; the text neither condemns nor commends the protestation.
29.9: ṭôḇ ʾattāh bəʿênay kəmalʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm — "you are as good in my sight as a messenger from God (malʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm)." Malʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm (H4397 + H430) — divine messenger/angel — is the highest praise Achish can offer: David is like a being from the divine realm in his trustworthiness. The phrase uses the same ʾĕlōhîm that appeared in 28:13 for the apparition at Endor; the juxtaposition — a spirit rising from the earth there, a trustworthy servant compared to a heavenly messenger here — frames the theological contrast between the two kings.
29.10: wəhiškamtem babbōqer — "rise early in the morning." The command matches the narrative tempo: decisions in crisis must be executed quickly. David leaves without fighting either Israel or Philistia — his dilemma resolved by the very commanders who opposed him, with no moral compromise required of him.
29.11: The brief closing verse — David and his men return to Philistia, the Philistines march to Jezreel — leaves the two trajectories proceeding simultaneously. The reader knows both outcomes: David will face Ziklag (ch30), and the Philistines will face Saul at Gilboa (ch31). The narrative interleaving creates dramatic irony.

30.1: bayyôm haššəlîšî — "on the third day." The three-day delay during which Ziklag was burned matches the travel time from Aphek. Amalêq (Amalek) — the same enemy Saul failed to destroy in ch15. The destruction visited on David's base is precisely the enemy that Saul's disobedience left alive. The theological link between 15:7-9 and 30:1 is structural: Saul's incomplete ḥērem now strikes at YHWH's chosen king.
30.2: wəlōʾ hemîtû ʾîš — "killing no one." The Amalekites' restraint in not killing the captives makes total recovery possible; the verb hemîtû (Hiphil of mût, to kill) marks the exception. The captive-taking rather than slaughter anticipates the total recovery — nothing is permanently lost.
