30.3: The discovery sequence: they came to the city, found it burned (śərûpāh ʾēš), wives and children gone. The verb wəhinnēh (and behold/look) introduces the shocking sight — a narrative particle that places the reader alongside the arriving soldiers at the moment of discovery.
30.4: wayyiśśəʾû ʾet qôlām wayyiḇkû ʿad ʾăšer lōʾ hāyāh lāhem kōaḥ liḇkôt — "they wept aloud until they had no more strength to weep." The physical exhaustion of grief — weeping until strength is gone — is measured precisely. Kōaḥ (H3581, strength/power) spent on mourning; it will need to be restored (v6).
30.5: The specific naming of David's two wives — Ahinoam and Abigail — personalizes the crisis: this is not only his men's tragedy but his own. Šəḇuyôt (captives, from šāḇāh, H7617, to take captive) — the same root as the broader captivity of Israel in later periods; the individual event uses the vocabulary of national catastrophe.
30.6: wayyiṯḥazzēq dāwid baYHWH ʾĕlōhāyw — "David strengthened himself in the LORD his God." The Hithpael of ḥāzaq (H2388) is uniquely reflexive: David is the agent of his own strengthening, drawing from YHWH the strength he needs. This is active faith — not passive reception but deliberate turning toward YHWH in the moment of crisis. The contrast with Saul (28:5: wayyirāʾ, was terrified; 28:7: seeking a medium) is the chapter's theological hinge. ʾĕlōhāyw — "his God" — the possessive marks covenantal relationship; YHWH is David's specifically, not only Israel's generically.
30.7: haggîšāh nāʾ lî ʾet hāʾēpôd — "bring me the ephod." David immediately turns to the legitimate means of inquiry — the priestly ephod — rather than any illicit channel. Abiathar (son of Ahimelech, the priest Saul had murdered at Nob, ch22) is with David; the escaped priest enables the inquiry Saul could not access. The contrast with 28:6 (Urim closed to Saul) is pointed: the same sacred apparatus answers David but was silent to Saul.
30.8: nāśōg tāśîg wəhaṣṣēl taṣṣîl — "you will certainly overtake them and certainly rescue the captives." Two infinitive absolute + imperfect constructions in sequence — the doubled certainty idiom in Hebrew. Nāśag (H5381, to overtake/reach) + hiṣṣîl (Hiphil of nāṣal, H5337, to deliver/rescue). The double absolute construction removes all uncertainty: YHWH's promise is unconditional and total. Compare 15:3 where the ḥērem command was also absolute — what Saul violated, David will fulfill in reverse through complete recovery.
30.9: naḥal habbesôr — the brook Besor. The name is uncertain but may derive from a root meaning "fresh water." The brook divides those who go on (400) from those who remain (200). This geographical dividing point becomes the occasion for David's ruling in v24.
30.10: wayirdōp dāwid hûʾ wəʾarbaʿ mēʾôt ʾîš — David goes with 400; 200 "were too exhausted (pāgar, H6296, to be faint/worn out) to cross the Besor." Pāgar denotes physical depletion — they were not cowardly but depleted. The distinction matters for David's ruling in v24.
30.11: The Egyptian slave found in the field functions as a providential discovery — an abandoned source of intelligence. Wayyimṣəʾû ʾîš miṣrî baśśādeh — "they found an Egyptian in the field" — the passive discovery (māṣāʾ, to find) rather than active pursuit. Food and water are immediately given before any questioning — basic hospitality before information extraction.
30.12: wayyittənû lô peleaḥ dəḇēlāh ûšətê ṣimmûqîm — fig cake and raisin clusters — concentrated energy food for a man three days without sustenance. Wattāšob rûḥô ʾēlāyw — "his strength/spirit returned to him" (rûaḥ, H7307, spirit/breath). The restorative function of food and drink prepares the Egyptian to serve as guide.
30.13: The Egyptian's story: slave of an Amalekite, abandoned when ill three days ago. Wayyaʿazbēnî ʾădōnî kî ḥālîtî — "my master abandoned me when I fell sick." The contrast with David's treatment of his men (vv9-10) is implicit: David does not abandon the exhausted at Besor; the Amalekite abandoned a sick slave. The abandoned slave leads David to the raiders.
30.14: The Egyptian's geographical inventory of the raid: Negeb of the Cherethites, Negeb of Judah, Negeb of Caleb, then Ziklag burned. Nəḡeḇ (H5045, south/Negeb region) — the semi-arid zone south of Judah where David operated. The Cherethites are associated with the Philistine sphere; the raid covered both Philistine and Judahite territory, explaining why both David (Philistine subject) and Judah (Israelite territory) were hit.
30.15: wəhišbaʿ lî bēʾlōhîm ʾim timitēnî — "swear to me by God that you won't kill me or hand me back to my master." The oath invokes ʾĕlōhîm — the Egyptian uses the generic divine name rather than YHWH; David swears by the God of Israel to an Egyptian. The exchange is entirely practical and mutually beneficial — no coercion on David's side.
30.16: pəšûṭîm ʿal pənê kol hāʾāreṣ ʾōḵəlîm wəšōṯîm wəḥōgĕgîm — "spread out across the land, eating, drinking, and celebrating." The Amalekites are caught in post-victory celebration, which renders them militarily vulnerable. Ḥōgĕgîm (from ḥāgag, H2287, to celebrate a feast/dance) — pilgrimage-festival language applied to pagan revelry. The verb's sacred connotation in Israel makes its use here ironic.
30.17: wayyakkēm dāwid minnešep wəʿad hāʿereb limmāḥărātām — "David struck them from twilight until the evening of the following day." The extended battle time — overnight into the next day — signals determined, total pursuit. Lōʾ nimlaṭ mēhem ʾîš kî ʾim ʾarbaʿ mēʾôt ʾîš naʿar — only 400 young men escaped on camels. The completeness of the defeat (with only the camel-mounted escape force surviving) contrasts with Saul's incomplete destruction of Amalek in ch15.
30.18: wayyaṣṣēl dāwid ʾēt kol ʾăšer lāqəḥû hāʾămālēqîm — "David recovered everything the Amalekites had taken." The verb yaṣṣēl (Hiphil of nāṣal, same root as the promise in v8) — the promise and its fulfillment use identical vocabulary. Wəʾēt šətê nāšāyw hiṣṣîl dāwid — "including his two wives." The personal element of recovery is specified first before the general summary in v19.
30.19: wəlōʾ neʾdar lāhem minnaqṭōn wəʿad gādôl ûminbānîm ûḇānôt ûmišlāl — "nothing was missing — young or old, sons or daughters, any plunder." The Hebrew uses neʾdar (Niphal of ʿādar, H5737, to be missing/lacking) — the negative of "lacking." Total recovery is expressed as the absence of any deficit. The comprehensive list reinforces the double absolute certainty of v8: YHWH's word proved exactly true.
30.20: wayyiqḥ dāwid ʾet kol haṣṣōʾn wəhabbāqār — David also seizes the livestock beyond what was originally taken from Ziklag. The men acknowledge this as David's spoil (šəlal dāwid zeh) — he has gained more than he lost. The surplus of recovery over loss will govern the distribution dispute of vv22-25.
30.21: wayyēlek dāwid ʾel māʾtāyim hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer pāgərû — "David returned to the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to follow." Wayyišʾal lāhem ləšālôm — "he greeted them warmly" (literally "asked them of peace"). The formal greeting of peace (šālôm) before any question of spoil-sharing is deliberate — David establishes relational regard before the economic dispute surfaces.
30.22: ʾanšê hārāʿ wəhaббəlîyāʿal — "wicked and worthless men" (rāʿ, H7451, evil; bəlîyāʿal, H1100, worthlessness — literally "without profit/value"). Bəlîyāʿal is a term of contempt in the DtrH corpus (2:12, Eli's sons; Judg 19:22; 20:13). Their ruling — "they get only their wives and children" — reduces the exhausted men to non-participants. The justice issue is whether combat service alone qualifies for equal share.
30.23: lōʾ tǝʿăśû kēn ʾaḥay ʾēt ʾăšer nātan YHWH lānû — "No, my brothers — that is not right with what the LORD has given us." David's counter grounds the ruling in theology, not equity: the spoil is YHWH's gift (nātan), not the warriors' earnings. Wayyišmor ʾōtānû — "he kept us safe" — YHWH's protection of those who fought is the source of success, making human effort the secondary cause.
30.24: kəḥēleq hayyōrēd bamməlḥāmāh ûkəḥēleq hayyōšēḇ ʿal hakkēlîm yəḥaddāw yəḥallēqû — "the share of the one who goes into battle is the same as the share of the one who stays with the supplies — they share equally." Kēlîm (H3627, vessels/equipment/supplies) — the "baggage" is military equipment, not personal belongings. The equal-share ruling is the practical expression of the theological principle: victory is YHWH's, the spoil is his gift, and the distribution reflects covenantal solidarity rather than proportional performance.
30.25: wayhî mēhayyôm hahûʾ wāmāʿlāh wayyəśîmēhā ləḥōq ûlemiṣpāṭ — "from that day onward David made it a fixed rule (ḥōq, H2706) and ordinance (miṣpāṭ, H4941) for Israel." Ḥōq + miṣpāṭ is the paired legal terminology of covenant law (cf. Exod 15:25; Deut passim). David legislates at the moment of crisis — the ad hoc ruling becomes constitutional precedent.
30.26: wayyišlaḥ miššəlāl lə ziqnê yəhûdāh lərēʿēhû — "he sent portions of the spoil to the elders of Judah — his friends." Rēʿa (H7453, neighbor/friend) — the same word used in 15:28 and 28:17 for David as Saul's "neighbor" who received the kingdom. Now David proactively builds covenant relationships through gift-giving. The list that follows (vv27-31) is a geographical network of Judahite loyalty-building.
30.27: The distribution towns — Bethel, Ramoth-negeb, Jattir — are in the Negeb of Judah, the region David traversed during his outlaw period. The gifts retroactively honor communities that provided cover and resources during David's years of flight.
30.28: Aroer, Siphmoth, Eshtemoa — further Judahite communities in the Negeb. The distribution is comprehensive rather than strategic — every community that sheltered David receives acknowledgment.
30.29: Racal, the Jerahmeelite towns, the Kenite towns — extending the gift-network to non-Israelite groups in the Negeb who maintained relationship with David. The Kenites (earlier spared from the Amalekite campaign at 15:6) reappear as allies.
30.30: Hormah, Bor-ashan, Athach — locations in the Negeb district; several unidentified with certainty. The list's completeness is the point: no community is overlooked, establishing David as a king who remembers those who helped him.
30.31: ûbəḥeḇrôn ûḇəkol hammaqōmôt ʾăšer hithallak šām dāwid hûʾ waʾănāšāyw — "in Hebron — everywhere David and his men had wandered." Heḇrôn (Hebron) appears last and climactically: it will be the site of David's first anointing as king (2 Sam 2:1-4). The gift-network of ch30 anticipates the political base that will enable the transition of 2 Samuel.

31.1: ûpəlištîm nilḥāmîm ʾel yiśrāʾēl wayyānusû ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl — "the Philistines attacked Israel, and the Israelites fled." The verb nûs (H5127, to flee/escape) marks total military collapse; this is not a fighting retreat but a rout. Wayyippəlû ḥălālîm bəhar haggILBŌʿA — "falling slain on Mount Gilboa." The battle is narrated in five verses with the economy of catastrophe.
31.2: wayyadbiqû pəlištîm ʾet šāʾûl wəʾet bānāyw — "the Philistines closed in hard on Saul and his sons." Dāḇaq (H1692, to cling/press hard against) — a pursuit verb implying the Philistines are faster and closing. Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua are named — all three sons die. Jonathan's death is noted without elaboration here; 2 Sam 1:17-27 provides the full lament.
31.3: wayyiḵbad hamməlḥāmāh ʾel šāʾûl — "the battle pressed hard against Saul" (kāḇēd, H3513, to be heavy — the same root as kāḇōd/honor; "weighty" battle). Wayyimṣāʾuhû hammôrîm ʾănāšîm baqqešet wayyāḥel məʾōd méhammôrîm — "the archers found their mark, and he was badly wounded" (ḥûl, H2342, to writhe/be in severe pain — often used of birth pangs). Saul is hit and incapacitated before making the decision of v4.
31.4: ʾāḇāh / dāqar — "Draw your sword (šəlōp ḥarbaḵā) and run me through (dāqar, H1856, to stab/thrust through)." Saul's motive: pen yāḇōʾû hāʿărēlîm hāʾēlleh ûdqārûnî wəhiṯʿallǝlû ḇî — "lest these uncircumcised men come and abuse me." Hithʿallel (Hithpael of ʿālal, H5953, to act against/abuse/mock) — the fear is public dishonor rather than death itself. Wayyimāʾēn — "but his armor-bearer refused" (māʾēn, the same refusal-verb used in ch28:23 and ch15). Saul then falls on his own sword — wayyiqaḥ šāʾûl ʾet haḥereb wayyippôl ʿāleyhā — the self-destruction of the king is narrated in one clause.
31.5: wayyarʾ nōśēʾ kēlāyw kî mēt šāʾûl wayyippôl gam hûʾ ʿal ḥarəbô — "when the armor-bearer saw that Saul was dead, he too fell on his own sword and died with him." The armor-bearer's death is briefly noted without commentary; his act is one of ancient honorable solidarity — the servant follows the master into death. The pairing of their deaths — master, then servant — frames Saul's end as comprehensively isolated from divine support.
31.6: wayyāmat šāʾûl ûšəlōšet bānāyw wənōśēʾ kēlāyw gam kol ʾănāšāyw bəyôm hahûʾ yaḥdāw — "so Saul died, along with his three sons, his armor-bearer, and all his men — all on that same day (yaḥdāw)." Yaḥdāw (together/at once) provides the temporal closure: the Saulide dynasty ends in a single day, as Samuel prophesied (māḥār in 28:19). The word "together" (yaḥdāw) is the same word used for the equal sharing in 30:24 — but there it described communal solidarity; here it describes communal annihilation.
31.7: wayyaʿazbû ʾet hehārîm wayyānusû — "they abandoned their cities and fled." The Israelite withdrawal from the Jezreel Valley and Transjordan follows the military collapse. Wayyāḇōʾû pəlištîm wayyēšəḇû ḇāhēm — "the Philistines came and settled in them." Philistine settlement of Israelite towns is the immediate geopolitical consequence; 2 Samuel opens in this situation.
31.8: wihyî mimmmāḥărāt — "the next day." Philistine plundering of the dead was standard ancient military practice. Wayyimṣəʾû ʾet šāʾûl wəʾet šəlōšat bānāyw nōpəlîm bəhar haggILBŌʿA — "they found Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa." The verb māṣāʾ (to find) — a discovery, not a pursuit. Saul is now an object to be found rather than a king who ruled.
31.9: wayyəḇaśśərû — "to announce the victory" (bāśar, H1319, Piel, to bring good news / announce glad tidings). The root of bəśōrāh (good news/gospel) in its Hebrew form. The Philistines "evangelize" their gods (ʿăṣabbêhem) and people with Saul's death. The irony that the death of Israel's king is proclaimed as "good news" in pagan temples echoes the later NT inversion: the death of Israel's true King is proclaimed as good news to all nations. Wayyāsîrû ʾet rōʾšô wəʾet kēlāyw — head removed and armor stripped; the head would be displayed as a trophy (cf. David's display of Goliath's head, 17:54).
31.10: wayyāśîmû ʾet kēlāyw bêt ʿaštārôt — "they placed his armor in the temple of Ashtaroth." Ashtaroth was the Canaanite goddess of war and fertility, associated with the Philistine pantheon. Dedicating captured armor to a deity was standard ancient Near Eastern victory practice — the spoil belongs to the god who gave the victory. Saul's armor ends as a votive offering; the man who was to be YHWH's covenant king becomes a trophy in a pagan sanctuary.
31.11: The people of Jabesh-gilead hear (šāmᵉʿû) what the Philistines did — the hearing that leads to action. Jabesh-gilead is the city Saul rescued in his first military act as king (ch11, from Nahash the Ammonite). The loyalty they display now is covenant-gratitude (ḥesed is the word 2 Sam 2:5 uses for their deed).
31.12: wayyāqumû kol ʾîš ḥayil wayyēlǝḵû kol hallaylāh — "all their fighting men rose, traveled through the night." The night march mirrors Saul's night journey to Endor (28:8). But where Saul traveled in darkness to seek an illegal oracle, the men of Jabesh-gilead travel in darkness to perform a loyal burial. Wayyiśrəpû ʾōtām šām — "burned them there." Cremation was unusual in Israelite practice; this may reflect the advanced state of decomposition or Philistine mutilation, making burial of intact bodies impossible.
31.13: wayyiqəḥû ʾet ʿaṣmōtêhem wayyiqbərû taḥat hāʾēšel — "they gathered the bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree (ʾēšel, H815)." ʾĒšel is the tamarisk — a tree associated with significant events (Abraham planted one at Beersheba, Gen 21:33; Saul sat under one at 22:6). The burial under a significant tree is a dignified interment for a fallen king. Wayyāṣumû šiḇʿat yāmîm — "they fasted for seven days." Seven-day mourning is the full period of intensive grief (cf. Job 2:13). The men of Jabesh-gilead honor Saul with the complete mourning rite — an act of covenant loyalty that will be explicitly commended by David in 2 Sam 2:5-6.
